**Headline: Unique Items and Charitable Causes: A Roundup of Unusual News Stories**

In a world where people often seek to capitalize on the unusual and the infamous, several recent news stories have highlighted the intersection of collectibility, charity, and the human touch. From the personal effects of a notorious terrorist to the luggage of a Hollywood heartthrob, here's a summary of the latest news:

**The Unabomber's Auction**

The U.S. Marshal's office is currently holding an online auction of the personal effects of Ted Kaczynski, also known as the Unabomber. The auction, which ends on June 2, aims to raise $15 million in restitution for the victims and their families. While the items on the auction block are largely mundane, several high-profile items have attracted significant interest, including Kaczynski's 35,000-word handwritten manifesto, which has a bid of $17,525, and his iconic hoodie, which is up to $20,025. Art historian and appraiser Elyse Luray believes that the auction has some value, citing the historical significance of the manifesto and the typewriter used to write it.

**Kellan Lutz's Charity Luggage**

Twilight heartthrob Kellan Lutz is teaming up with JetBlue and DoSomething.org to auction off his luggage for a good cause. The celebrity baggage auction, which is now in its second round, aims to raise funds for youth groups across the country. Lutz was happy to take part, saying that it's a great cause and he hopes his bag will help raise some serious funds.

**A Rare Film Reel**

Collector Morace Park bought a film reel initially because he liked the look of the tin, but it's now set to command a six-figure sum. The film, Charlie Chaplin in Zepped, is the only known surviving copy and shows the famous British actor bringing down a German Zeppelin during a London raid. The seven-minute long reel is thought to contain some of the earliest known animation and was made as a morale-boosting film for British troops during World War One.

These stories highlight the complexities of the human experience, from the tragic and the infamous to the charitable and the unusual. Whether it's the Unabomber's manifesto or Kellan Lutz's luggage, these items offer a glimpse into the lives of those who created them and the people who seek to collect and preserve them.
details.ground_truth: Get out your checkbooks, kids: There is a slew of famous and infamous items up for auction. The US Marshal's office's online auction of the Unabomber's personal effects ends tomorrow, and Aol's Weird News astutely observes that Ted Kaczynski's 35,000-word handwritten manifesto ($17,525) is currently going for less than the gray hoodie ($20,025) that figured so prominently into his FBI Wanted poster. Bonus: sunglasses included. Looking for more of a bargain? A bow and arrows in a Sears box is currently a steal at $743. And in other bizarre auction news: Balloon boy's dad is trying to auction off the infamous "flying saucer" balloon for $1 million. But don't worry, reports KTLA: Richard Heene has vowed to give all the money to charity (specifically, Japan). A bunch of celebrities are hoping JetBlue loses their luggage ... right into the hands of eager bidders. The Celebrity Baggage Auction will benefit DoSomething.org. Buy a signed Jessica Simpson-brand bag or a leather Coach bag personally used by Rosario Dawson, and get two roundtrip JetBlue tickets, too. Click for more. Maybe eBay is the place to buy stuff: Morace Park bought a film reel for $5.25 that turned out to be the only known copy of the Charlie Chaplin film Zepped. The Telegraph reports that it's expected to fetch six figures in a June 29 auction.

details.ground_truth: A former Boston TV news anchor says that last year, when her son was 18, he was at a bar in Natucket, Mass., when he met Kevin Spacey. The actor "bought him drink after drink after drink and when my son was drunk, Spacey made his move and sexually assaulted him," said Heather Unruh at a press conference Wednesday. She says Spacey stuck his hand down her son's pants and onto his genitals, and tried to convince him to go to a party where they could continue to drink, USA Today reports. A bystander asked the teen if he was OK and the teen said no, at which point the woman urged the teen to flee and he took her advice, Unruh said. Unruh, who appeared with an attorney, says her son filed a police report last week and handed over evidence; she says a criminal investigation has been opened but the Nantucket Police Department has not confirmed that. The attorney with Unruh noted that her son's accusations are "well within" both the civil and criminal statutes of limitations. USA Today has a list of the 14 people so far accusing Spacey of sexual assault or harassment. Meanwhile Jon Bernthal, who worked with Spacey on Baby Driver, told a SiriusXM radio show Tuesday that he lost respect for Spacey while working on the film. "I thought he was a bit of a bully," Bernthal said, per People. "I didn’t really care for the way he was behaving toward some of the other people on set."

**Mali Crisis: A Threat to West Africa and the World**

A recent coup in Mali has sparked a crisis that threatens not only the stability of the West African region but also the world. The coup, led by a group of disgruntled soldiers, overthrew the democratically elected government of President Amadou Toumani Touré on March 22. The power vacuum created by the coup has allowed the Tuareg rebellion, a separatist movement that seeks to establish an independent Tuareg state called Azawad, to gain control of the northern part of the country.

The Tuareg rebels, who are divided into two groups - the National Movement for the Liberation of Azawad (MNLA) and Ansar Dine, which is linked to the North African branch of al-Qaeda - have taken advantage of the situation and made rapid advances in the past few days. They now control a third of the West African country, including the key northern towns of Gao, Kidal, and Timbuktu.

**Threat to the Stability of the Region**

The crisis in Mali has sparked concerns among leaders across the region, who fear that a similar situation could occur in their own countries. The Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), representing 15 nations in the region, has been harsh in its condemnation of the coup and has imposed sanctions on Mali, including a ban on the importation of fuel and a freeze on the country's account at the regional central bank.

The sanctions have had a significant impact on the country, with long lines forming at petrol stations and the country's electricity grid expected to falter in the coming weeks. The landlocked country imports all its fuel, which is trucked in overland from neighboring Ivory Coast and Senegal, both located on Africa's Atlantic Coast.

**Threat to the World**

The crisis in Mali has also sparked concerns among the international community, who fear that the situation could have significant consequences for the whole of the Sahel region. The UN Security Council is due to hold an emergency meeting on the crisis later on Tuesday, and the UN's cultural agency, Unesco, has warned that the fighting in northern Mali could damage the World Heritage Site of Timbuktu.

Timbuktu, a city on the edge of the Sahara desert, is littered with impressive architecture, using mud and wood in a unique style. The city is home to about 700,000 ancient manuscripts, held in approximately 60 private libraries, and is an important centre
details.ground_truth: Since we last checked in, the situation in Mali following a soldiers' coup has spiraled, with rising concerns over food and gas shortages, violence, and indeed the country's "very existence," according to Le Monde. An update: The coup has driven more than 200,000 people from their homes to other parts of the region, the AP reports. The UN worries that major food shortages could be imminent, and "mayhem in these towns and cities is increasing," says a rep. Neighboring countries have established an embargo against the rebels who ousted the president; they've closed off their borders and frozen the country's regional bank account. That has prompted residents of Mali, which imports all its fuel, to rush to gas stations to collect fuel. The country's electricity grid may also be headed for failure. UNESCO is citing a threat to Timbuktu, a World Heritage Site full of "architectural wonders," notes the BBC. Mosques there are "essential to the preservation of the identity of the people of Mali," says the group. Yet "nothing seems to be able to stop" the Tuareg coup, Le Monde reports. West African leaders need to help Mali's military fight back before the instability spreads beyond the country's borders.

**Global Efforts to Eradicate Child Institutionalization by 2050**

A recent campaign launched by author and philanthropist JK Rowling, #LetstalkLumos, aims to raise awareness about the global issue of child institutionalization. According to Lumos, a charity dedicated to closing child institutions and orphanages, there are approximately 8 million children worldwide who are separated from their families and locked away in often atrocious conditions.

The practice of institutionalizing children has been shown to cause measurable and sometimes irreparable harm, including malnutrition, developmental delays, and psychological trauma. Children who are institutionalized are also more likely to be abused or trafficked, and are often unable to find employment or reintegrate into their communities as adults.

However, Lumos estimates that the institutionalization of children can be eradicated globally by 2050, and has already seen successes in several countries. In Moldova, for example, there has been a 70% reduction in the number of children in institutions since Lumos began working there in 2007. In the Czech Republic, Lumos's demonstration area has seen a 75% fall in admissions, and in Bulgaria, the number of children in institutions has reduced by 54% since Lumos began working there.

International donors play a vital role in supporting efforts to keep families together and provide alternative care options for children who cannot be with their parents. Ending the practice of institutionalizing children is not only a moral imperative, but also makes excellent economic sense, as it is far more cost-effective to support a child in a family than in an institution.

The EU, the US, and the Global Alliance for Children have made commitments to support these efforts, and there is now a critical mass of expertise and evidence on which to build. As JK Rowling says, it is her dream that, within her lifetime, the very concept of taking a child away from its family and locking it away will seem to belong to a cruel, fictional world.
details.ground_truth: JK Rowling makes the case today that millions of kids around the world are living in appalling conditions—the stuff of Grimms' fairytales—in institutions that are supposed to be caring for them. Rowling wants to change that, not by improving these orphanages with "pretty murals" or teddy bears, but by eliminating them altogether, she writes in the Guardian. The big challenge is providing support for often-poor families who think they have no choice but to place their child in such an institution. If a child's biological family isn't up to the task, then foster families can step in. But Rowling argues that institutions are absolutely the wrong answer. Rowling has started a charity called Lumos (yes, after one of her Harry Potter spells), and writes that it has made genuine progress in reducing new placements in countries such as Bulgaria, Moldova, and the Czech Republic. She wants to raise awareness and donations with campaigns like this one. "I recently committed to becoming president of Lumos for life," writes Rowling. "It is my dream that, within my lifetime, the very concept of taking a child away from its family and locking it away will seem to belong to a cruel, fictional world." Click for her full column.

**US and Pakistan Agree to Cooperate on Future Operations Against "High-Value Targets"**

In a bid to ease tensions and improve their strained relationship, the United States and Pakistan have agreed to work together on future operations against "high-value targets" in Pakistan. The announcement was made in a joint statement following a visit by US Senator John Kerry to Pakistan.

**Key Developments:**

1. **Return of US Helicopter Tail:**** Pakistan has agreed to return the tail of a US military helicopter that was damaged during the raid that killed Osama bin Laden. The handover is seen as a significant step towards improving relations between the two countries.
2. **Increased Intelligence Sharing and Joint Operations:**** The two countries have committed to increase intelligence sharing and joint operations to defeat their common enemies. This includes cooperation on operations against "high-value targets" in Pakistan.
3. **US Economic Assistance Package:**** US Senator John Kerry, who chairs the US Senate's Foreign Relations Committee, has conveyed to Pakistani leaders that they must demonstrate a commitment to fighting Islamist militancy to address the concerns of members of Congress who have called for an end to US aid.
4. **No Designs on Nuclear Arsenal:**** The US has assured Pakistan that it has "no designs" on its nuclear arsenal, which is seen as a significant relief for Pakistan.
5. **Drone Strikes:**** The issue of drone strikes remains a contentious one, with Pakistan tacitly allowing but publicly protesting them. The US has not made any commitments to stop or reduce drone strikes.

**Background:**

The US-Pakistan relationship has been strained since the US raid that killed Osama bin Laden in May. Pakistan has been angry about not being informed of the raid in advance, while US officials have questioned whether Pakistani officials colluded with bin Laden. The situation has led to a near standstill in intelligence sharing and joint operations between the two countries.

**Next Steps:**

Senior White House officials will visit Pakistan to discuss the implementation of the agreed-upon steps. This will determine whether Secretary of State Hillary Rodham Clinton will move forward with plans to visit Pakistan this month.
details.ground_truth: The US and Pakistan have agreed to work together in the future against "high value targets" in Pakistan, the countries announced in a joint statement today. The news comes after Sen. John Kerry arrived in Pakistan last night, intending to send a strong message following the death of Osama bin Laden: Cooperate more when it comes to rooting out terrorists, or face "profound" changes in the US-Pakistan relationship—which could include kissing billions in aid goodbye. Kerry is the most senior US official to go to Pakistan since bin Laden's death, but since issuing the joint pledge, he said Secretary of State Hillary Clinton will soon announce her own plans to visit, the AP reports. Kerry's meetings with Pakistani leaders began last night, the Washington Post reports. Many in Washington think Pakistan is harboring Islamist militants, and some members of Congress are calling for the US to sever the billions of dollars in aid it provides to Pakistan. Kerry is in Pakistan to lay out the new stakes following bin Laden's death, and question officials about how bin Laden was able to hide in Pakistan for years. In Kabul, Kerry said there is "some evidence" that the Pakistan government has knowledge of insurgent activities, calling it "very disturbing."

**North Korea's Half-Brother Kim Jong-nam Assassinated with VX Nerve Agent**

In a shocking and mysterious case, Kim Jong-nam, the half-brother of North Korean leader Kim Jong-un, was assassinated with the highly toxic VX nerve agent at the Kuala Lumpur International Airport in Malaysia on February 13. The announcement was made by Malaysia's police chief, Khalid Abu Bakar, who stated that the presence of the nerve agent had been detected in swabs taken from Kim's eyes and face.

**Background on VX Nerve Agent**

VX is a clear, amber-colored, oily liquid that is tasteless and odorless. It is the most potent of the known chemical warfare agents and can penetrate the skin, disrupting the transmission of nerve impulses. A drop on the skin can kill in minutes, while lower doses can cause eye pain, blurred vision, drowsiness, and vomiting. VX can be disseminated in a spray or vapor when used as a chemical weapon, or used to contaminate water, food, and agricultural products.

**North Korea's Chemical Weapons Capabilities**

North Korea is estimated to have a chemical weapons production capability of up to 4,500 metric tons during a typical year and 12,000 tons during a period of extended crisis. It is widely reported to possess a large arsenal of chemical weapons, including mustard, phosgene, and sarin gas. North Korea is also believed to have the third-largest stockpile of chemical weapons, after the US and Russia.

**Diplomatic Crisis and Investigation**

The case has unleashed a diplomatic crisis between Malaysia and North Korea, with North Korea denying any responsibility for the attack and accusing Malaysia of fabricating evidence. Malaysian authorities have arrested four people, including one North Korean and the two women who interacted with Kim at the airport. Seven North Koreans are being sought, including a diplomat. The investigation is ongoing, with police still investigating how the lethal agent entered Malaysia.

**Public Safety Concerns**

The use of VX nerve agent has raised serious questions about public safety in the airport, which was not decontaminated for 11 days after the attack. The nerve agent can take days or even weeks to evaporate, and can contaminate not only the airport but anyplace else Kim had been, including medical facilities and the ambulance he was transported in.

**Expert Analysis**

Dr. Bruce Goldberger, a leading toxicologist, said that even a tiny amount of VX nerve agent is capable of killing and that symptoms from VX
details.ground_truth: Kim Jong Nam's assassins killed him with a banned chemical weapon, Malaysian police revealed Friday. The country's police chief said toxicology reports on swabs from the face and eyes of the exiled North Korean found VX nerve agent, which the BBC notes is classed as a weapon of mass destruction by the United Nations. He said that one of two women believed to have rubbed the extremely toxic substance on Kim's face with their hands suffered from vomiting after the attack. The New York Times reports that VX agent can be created by mixing two compounds—and police suspect the two women put the substances on Kim's face, one after the other, to create a deadly dose. The police chief said the airport where Kim was attacked is now being decontaminated. North Korea—which is widely suspected to have been behind the killing of leader Kim Jong Un's half brother—never signed the Chemical Weapons Convention that banned VX, the AP notes. Pyongyang denies involvement and says Malaysia's investigation is full of "holes and contradictions." The father of one of the two women being held, Vietnamese citizen Doan Thi Huong, tells the Times his daughter trained as a pharmacist and he has seen little of her in recent years. (Police say that after Malaysia refused to give Kim's body to North Korean diplomats, somebody tried to break into the morgue.)

**Horrific Gang Rape and Beating at Richmond High School: Multiple Arrests and Investigations Ongoing**

A 15-year-old girl was brutally gang-raped and beaten on the grounds of Richmond High School in Richmond, California, after her homecoming dance on October 24, 2009. The incident has sent shockwaves through the community and raised questions about campus security and the bystander effect.

According to police, up to two dozen people watched the assault without intervening, and as many as 10 assailants, ranging in age from 15 to their early 20s, were involved in the attack. The men laughed and took photos as they took turns assaulting the girl, who was left semiconscious and naked from the waist down near a picnic table.

Two suspects have been arrested so far: Manuel Ortega, 19, who was booked on suspicion of rape, kidnapping, and robbery, and a 15-year-old boy who attends the school and knew the victim. A 21-year-old Richmond man was also arrested on suspicion of sexual assault and rape.

Police are offering a $20,000 reward for information leading to the conviction of any of the assailants and are expected to make more arrests. Investigators have described the incident as "disgusting" and "a sad state of affairs," and have expressed confidence that the list of people involved will expand.

The attack has occurred in a city that has dealt with its share of vicious crimes in recent years, and the school recently approved surveillance cameras after a series of violent crimes. However, none of the cameras were working at the time of the attack.

**FBI and DOJ Under Scrutiny for Alleged Surveillance Abuse**

The House Intelligence Committee has released a memo, known as the Nunes memo, which alleges that the FBI and the Justice Department abused their surveillance authority when they sought a secret court order to monitor a former Trump campaign adviser, Carter Page. The memo claims that the government failed to disclose important information about the source of the information used to obtain the warrant, Christopher Steele, a former FBI source who was paid by the Democratic National Committee and the Hillary Clinton campaign to compile a dossier on Donald Trump.

**Steele's Bias and Lack of Reliability**

The memo also highlights Steele's bias against Trump and his lack of reliability as a source. Steele was suspended and then terminated as an FBI source for his unauthorized disclosure to the media of his relationship with the FBI. The memo also notes that Steele's reporting was only minimally corroborated and that he had a clear motive to discredit Trump.

**FBI's Handling of the Steele Dossier**

The memo criticizes the FBI's handling of the Steele dossier, which was used to obtain the warrant to surveil Carter Page. The memo notes that the FBI failed to disclose the political origins of the dossier and that Steele's bias and lack of reliability were not taken into account.

**Other Allegations of Abuse**

The memo also alleges that the FBI and the Justice Department engaged in other forms of abuse, including the use of a Yahoo News article to corroborate the Steele dossier, which was actually derived from information leaked by Steele himself. The memo also notes that the FBI's counterintelligence division head, Assistant Director Bill Priestap, said that corroboration of the Steele dossier was in its "infancy" at the time of the initial Page FISA application.

**Twitter's Location Feature**

In a separate development, Twitter has announced that users can now add location information to their tweets, such as their city or precise location. Users also have the option to delete their tweet location history.

Overall, the Nunes memo and the allegations of abuse by the FBI and the Justice Department raise serious questions about the integrity of the surveillance process and the use of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA) to obtain warrants to surveil American citizens.

**Gerry Adams Released After Four Days of Questioning in Connection with 1972 IRA Murder**

Sinn Fein leader Gerry Adams was released on Sunday evening after four days of questioning by police in Northern Ireland in connection with the 1972 IRA murder of Jean McConville, a 37-year-old widow and mother of 10. Adams, who has consistently denied any involvement in the murder, was arrested under the terrorism act and taken to Antrim police station for questioning.

**Background on the Case**

Jean McConville was abducted from her home in west Belfast in December 1972 and was shot and secretly buried. Her body was not found until 2003, when it was discovered on a beach in County Louth. The case is one of the many "Disappeared" cases in Northern Ireland, where people were abducted, murdered, and buried in secret by republicans during the Troubles.

**Adams' Response**

Speaking at a press conference in Belfast after his release, Adams criticized the timing of his arrest, saying that it was a "sustained, malicious, untruthful campaign" to allege his involvement in the murder. He also claimed that the police could have used discretion rather than arresting him, and that his arrest had sent out the "wrong signal." Adams reiterated that he was innocent of any involvement in the murder and that Sinn Fein remained fully committed to the political process in Northern Ireland.

**Reaction from Other Parties**

Other political parties in Northern Ireland have rejected claims that the timing of the arrest was political. The Democratic Unionist Party's Jeffrey Donaldson said that there was nothing within the law of Northern Ireland or the UK that said the police had to suspend the investigation of murder during an election campaign. The Ulster Unionist Party's Mike Nesbitt said that the McConville family's 42-year wait for truth and justice made Gerry Adams' complaint over the "two-month" delay between him offering to speak to the PSNI and their response seem churlish.

**Next Steps**

The decision to release Adams means that prosecution lawyers will now decide if charges will be brought. The file sent by the police to the Public Prosecution Service will detail the evidence gathered, and it will be up to PPS lawyers to decide if there is enough evidence to bring any charges and what those charges would be. It could take some time for police to prepare the file for the PPS, with prosecutors then taking a further period to assess whatever evidence is presented.

**Five Toddlers Injured with Hot Glue at Chicago Daycare, Two Workers Arrested and Charged**

A disturbing incident at a Chicago daycare has left five 2-year-old children with injuries after one of the workers allegedly used a hot glue gun on them. The incident occurred on December 1 at the Children's Learning Place in the 3100-block of West Fullerton.

According to surveillance footage, Lizandra Cosme, 32, of Chicago, applied the hot glue gun directly to the hands and arms of the five children, causing them to wince and whine in pain. Her assistant, Susana Gonzalez, 27, of Melrose Park, was present in the room and allegedly laughed as the incident occurred.

Cosme, who had 16 children under her care at the time, brought the glue gun to the daycare to use on a Christmas project. She later attempted to apply Neosporin to one of the children's burns and asked the father of another child if the child had been injured at home in an apparent effort to cover up the incident.

Cosme was arrested on Sunday and charged with five counts of aggravated battery of a child under 13 years old causing great bodily harm. She was denied bail at a court appearance on Monday and is scheduled to return to court on December 26.

Gonzalez was charged with five misdemeanor counts of causing the circumstances of child endangerment. Both women have been terminated from their positions at the daycare.

The state Department of Children and Family Services is investigating the allegations of abuse, and the daycare's spokeswoman, Lissa Druss Christman, stated that the well-being of their students is paramount and that they notified DCFS immediately after learning of the incident.

The extent of the children's injuries is unclear, but the incident has raised concerns about the safety and well-being of children in daycare settings.
details.ground_truth: Two workers at a Chicago day care were fired and arrested after a disturbing incident that left five toddlers injured. Police say surveillance video captured one of the workers burning the 2-year-olds with a hot glue gun while the other woman watched and laughed, People reports. Lizandra Cosme, 32, has been charged with five counts of aggravated battery of a child causing great bodily harm, the Chicago Sun-Times reports. Susana Gonzalez, 27, faces five misdemeanor charges of causing the circumstances of child endangerment over the Dec. 1 incident at the Children's Place day care. It's not clear how seriously the children were injured. Prosecutors say Cosme, who brought the glue gun for a Christmas project, was captured on video applying the hot glue directly to the hands and arms of the three girls and two boys. "Each of the child victims winced and some whined at the hot glue gun application," a prosecutor said during a Monday court appearance. Prosecutors said Cosme tried to cover up the incident, asking a father whether his child had been burned at home, ABC7 reports. They said her actions were discovered when one child's mother, an ER physician, saw the burns and demanded to see surveillance footage. Cosme's lawyer said his client "screwed up" but did not mean to hurt the children.

**Texas Gubernatorial Candidate Wendy Davis Discloses Abortion History in Memoir**

Texas Democratic gubernatorial candidate Wendy Davis has revealed in her upcoming memoir, "Forgetting to Be Afraid," that she had two abortions, one in 1996 and another in 1994, due to severe fetal abnormalities and a medically necessary ectopic pregnancy, respectively.

According to the book, Davis's first abortion in 1996 was due to a severe brain abnormality in the fetus, which would have left the child deaf, blind, and in a permanent vegetative state if she survived delivery. Davis writes that she could feel the fetus's body tremble violently and knew that she needed to end the pregnancy to prevent her suffering.

The second abortion, in 1994, was medically necessary due to an ectopic pregnancy, which is generally not considered viable and can endanger the mother's life. Davis writes that terminating the pregnancy was considered an abortion in Texas, and doctors had to report it as such.

Davis's decision to disclose her abortion history in her memoir comes as she runs for governor against Republican Attorney General Greg Abbott, who is heavily favored to replace Republican Gov. Rick Perry next year. Abbott's campaign did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

However, anti-abortion groups, including Texans Right to Life, expressed sympathy for Davis's difficult position but said they hoped all decisions end in choosing to continue a pregnancy. Cecile Richards, president of Planned Parenthood and the daughter of former Texas Gov. Ann Richards, praised Davis's "unwavering courage" and said that she is grateful to her for sharing her story and shining a light on a subject that is too often hidden in the shadows of shame and stigma.

Davis's memoir also details her hardscrabble life after her father left the family, her struggles to pay the bills as a young mother, and her community college education as the first step on an upward path including graduation from Harvard Law School and service on the Fort Worth City Council and in the state Senate.

The book's dedication is to Davis's three daughters, including the one she lost in 1996, and her parents, whose tumultuous relationship is detailed in the book. Davis ends the book with a story of a time she felt her lost daughter said good-bye to her, when she was caught in a cylindrical swirl of leaves on a golf course.

"Forgetting to Be Afraid" goes on sale Tuesday and is priced at $27.97 from Blue Rider Press, an imprint of the Penguin Group.
details.ground_truth: Texas gubernatorial candidate Wendy Davis' new memoir is dedicated in part to daughters Amber, Dru, and Tate, and those familiar with Davis' life story might be puzzled by the last name on that list. That's because Davis is revealing for the first time that Tate is the unborn child that she and her husband aborted in the second trimester, after doctors discovered a severe brain abnormality. The San Antonio Express-News and the AP obtained advanced copies, and the news is generating headlines given that Davis shot to national fame while filibustering against an abortion bill in Texas. Davis writes that she decided to abort in 1996 after doctors said the baby would be blind, deaf, and in a vegetative state if she survived the pregnancy. Davis also felt the fetus “tremble violently" in the womb, "as if someone were applying an electric shock to her, and I knew then what I needed to do. She was suffering." Her doctor "quieted" the unborn child's heart and delivered her by C-section. Davis and her husband had Tate baptized. After the abortion, "an indescribable blackness followed," writes Davis in Forgetting to Be Afraid, "and when I finally did come through it, I emerged a different person." Davis also writes of a previously disclosed procedure to terminate an earlier ectopic pregnancy in which the embryo was implanted outside the uterus. She says she opted not to talk about either case during her famous filibuster because she feared it would "overshadow the events of the day."

**Human Trafficking and Prostitution Ring Bust in Massachusetts**

In a recent investigation, two men, Joseph E. Van Wert, 65, and Randy W. Lambach, 45, were arrested and charged with human trafficking and related crimes for allegedly running a citywide prostitution ring in Pittsfield, Massachusetts. The investigation, which began in the spring of 2017, was sparked by reports of increased prostitution activity in the city.

According to police, Lambach, who had been running the prostitution ring for several years, would seek out women with severe drug addictions and advertise their services on social media. He would set up meetings and rates with prospective clients, and would take a significant cut of the women's money or pay them in heroin, cocaine, or prescription drugs.

Van Wert, who allowed some of the meetings to take place in his third-floor apartment at a senior living facility, would also pay for sex with the women and was paid by Lambach in cash and drugs. Some of the women Lambach recruited were formerly his foster children.

The investigation revealed that Lambach would transport the women to and from specific locations, including outside of the state, and would threaten to turn them in if they stopped working for him. He also allegedly showed a witness photos of the prostitutes, including one who estimated to be as young as 15.

Lambach has pleaded not guilty to four counts of human trafficking, while Van Wert has pleaded not guilty to conspiracy to commit human trafficking, deriving support from prostitution, maintaining a house of prostitution, and sexual conduct for a fee. The investigation remains ongoing, and additional charges may be coming.

The Pittsfield Police Department detective bureau, anti-crime unit, drug unit, and members of the state police detective unit assigned to the District Attorney's Office are assisting with the investigation.

They were also told that some of the women were Lambach's former foster children. Both men have pleaded not guilty, Lambach to four counts of human trafficking and Van Wert to conspiracy to commit human trafficking, deriving support from prostitution, maintaining a house of prostitution, and sexual conduct for a fee.

**Archaeological Discoveries Unveil the Secrets of Britain's Ancient Past**

In recent weeks, archaeologists have made two significant discoveries in the Vale of Pewsey, Wiltshire, shedding new light on the lives of people in the areas surrounding Stonehenge. The findings, which include a 4,000-year-old Bronze Age skeleton and a remarkably well-preserved Neolithic house, are set to revolutionize our understanding of Britain's ancient past.

**The Bronze Age Skeleton**

A team of archaeologists from the University of Reading, led by Dr. Jim Leary, discovered the skeleton of a child believed to be around 4,000 years old. The body was found lying in a foetal position and wearing an amber necklace. Scientific analysis will provide information on the child's gender, diet, pathologies, and date of burial, which may shed light on where the young individual had lived.

**The Neolithic House**

The team also uncovered a remarkably well-preserved Neolithic house, believed to be one of the best-preserved from the period ever found in the UK. The house, which dates back to around 2,400 BC, is a rectangular building made of chalk and plaster, with beautifully worked flint arrowheads and blades, decorated pottery, shale and copper bracelets, and a Roman brooch. The house was likely used for great gatherings and feasts, with pig bones and scorch marks from a charcoal firepit suggesting that it was not a permanent residence.

**Marden Henge**

The discoveries were made at the Marden Henge, a giant ditch and bank enclosure that was once one of the biggest and most impressive in Britain. The site, which is believed to have been used for status and showing off wealth and power, is vast and takes 40 minutes to walk from one end to the other. The team has also uncovered a previously unrecorded Roman complex, including the foundations of an impressive barn.

**Wilsford Henge**

The team has also been excavating the Wilsford Henge, a site that is believed to have been linked to the river Avon by a navigable flow. The site, which is part of the Vale of Pewsey dig, a partnership between the University of Reading, Historic England, the Arts and Humanities Research Council, and Wiltshire Museum, aims to gain a better understanding of the people who lived in the areas surrounding Stonehenge.

**Open Day**

details.ground_truth: You think Stonehenge is impressive? Archaeologists in Britain are excavating a monument ten times larger than the iconic structure, though it appears to the naked eye to be little more than farmland. A henge is a circular earthwork, and the one in question is Marden Henge, which sits a few miles north of Stonehenge in Wiltshire. National Geographic reports that roughly 4,500 years ago it featured 10-foot-tall earthen berms that encompassed some 40 acres. The Guardian writes that the henge was "far larger than the Avebury or Stonehenge circles, and too large for any imaginable practical use." But over the ages Marden Henge's berms have "slumped" and farmers have worked the land, and archaeologists have gravitated to the more breathtaking Stonehenge. Until now. Jim Leary of the University of Reading has just kicked off a three-year study of the site; his excavations (he worked at the site in 2010, too) are the only ones to have occurred there in nearly 50 years. He hopes the berms will help explain the "insane, utterly unsustainable" construction boom that led to Marden Henge and four other nearby Neolithic monuments, including Stonehenge. "Not nearly enough attention has been paid to the archaeology of the fertile valley in between these places," says Leary. So far he's found the remains of a 4,000-year-old teen wearing an amber necklace just outside Marden Henge, per the BBC; fancy arrowheads; and a stone building within the henge containing the bones of at least 13 pigs, suggesting a huge feast was held there. "For all the attention that has been lavished on Stonehenge over the years, we may well find out that Marden was where it was really at during the Neolithic," he says. (There's a giant "super henge" under Stonehenge.)

**BP Shareholders Rebel Against CEO Bob Dudley's Pay Package**

In a historic and unprecedented move, a majority of BP PLC's shareholders voted against the company's executive pay policy, including a 20% increase in CEO Bob Dudley's total pay for 2015. The non-binding vote, which saw 59% of investors reject the pay decisions, is a stinging rebuke to Dudley and his board, who have been under increasing pressure to navigate the company through the low oil price environment.

**Background to the Pay Package**

Dudley's pay package was criticized by several shareholder advisory groups, including Glass Lewis, which recommended that shareholders vote against the payment plans. The company's compensation committee awards executive bonuses based on the company's performance in several strategic areas, including its safety record and internal targets for operational cash flow and underlying profits. Dudley's pay was defended by the company, which said that he had met or exceeded every one of the targets he had been set.

**Shareholder Reaction**

Shareholders who criticized the pay deal included Aberdeen Asset Management, Royal London Asset Management, and investor group Sharesoc, which branded the pay deal "simply too high". Several shareholders publicly criticized the pay increase at the company's annual general meeting in London, with one senior corporate governance investor saying that the company had "ignored warnings" and needed to "get this right next time, or it could mean people will have to step down".

**BP's Response**

BP's Chairman Carl-Henric Svanberg promised to review the terms on which pay is awarded in consultation with shareholders before next year. The company defended the pay rise, saying that Dudley had met or exceeded every one of the targets he had been set. However, after the vote, Svanberg said that the company wouldn't adjust Dudley's pay.

**Industry-Wide Pressure**

The BP vote is part of a growing trend of institutional investors and advisers around the world taking a more aggressive stance over pay. US banks from Citigroup to Bank of America have faced pressure to toughen bonus "clawback" regimes, which put executives on the hook for future losses. The Institute of Directors said the shareholder rebellion would "determine the future of corporate governance in the UK".

**Conclusion**

The BP vote is a significant moment in the debate over executive pay, and highlights the growing pressure on companies to navigate the low oil price environment while also keeping their shareholders happy. details.ground_truth: Oil giant BP lost $5.2 billion last year, but the company somehow saw fit to propose maximum bonuses for 2015 for its top executives, including a 20% pay increase for CEO Bob Dudley—a proposal that 59% of shareholders roundly rejected by proxy vote at Thursday's annual meeting, MarketWatch reports. The company had indicated earlier in the day that it may also have to reduce its dividend, the Wall Street Journal reports. Dudley is set to receive the full bonus he was eligible for, which comes to $4.2 million (including $1.4 million in cash and a portion in deferred BP shares), per an earlier MarketWatch report. This amount was bumped up from the $3 million ($1 million in cash) he received in 2014. BP's CFO was also on the list to rake in his full bonus. "We think it sends the wrong message," a rep for shareholder Royal London Asset Management tells the BBC. "It shows that the board is out of touch." Not only did BP suffer straight-up monetary losses as the price of oil fell, it also announced it will be getting rid of about 7,000 jobs and taking other belt-tightening measures. And the Financial Times notes that other energy company execs saw their pay slashed in 2015. But a BP spokesman says "executives performed strongly in a difficult environment in 2015, managing the things they could control and for which they were accountable." Andy Critchlow, writing for the Reuters Breakingviews blog, agrees. "Dudley has to work harder than his predecessors," he writes, noting the CEO has helped the company recover from the 2010 Deepwater Horizon disaster and improved safety protocol. "The mild-mannered American has had possibly the toughest job in the oil industry. His rewards look in line with that task." Carl-Henric Svanberg, chairman of BP's board, says the nonbinding shareholder vote won't alter the payouts they've already decided on, but that the board will take investors' concerns into account when coming up with next year's compensation packages.

**Peru's Top Investigator Put on Leave for Misleading Public on Serial Killers**

In a shocking turn of events, Peru's top organized crime investigator, Eusebio Felix, has been put on leave from his job after he misled the country by claiming to have caught a gang of serial killers who acted out an ancient Andean legend and sold their victims' fat. The legend, known as the "Pishtacos," tells the story of mythical killers who roam the Andes mountains extracting fat from travelers.

According to Felix, the gang murdered up to 60 people and exported their fat to Italian cosmetics makers for thousands of dollars a liter. However, police in Lima, the capital, later admitted that the investigation had been botched and that there may have been only one victim. The police also displayed what they said was human fat stored in an empty bottle of Inca Kola, a popular soft drink in the Andes, and a video of police pulling body parts from a shallow grave at a house in the mountainous region of Huanuco.

The police's account has been widely criticized, with anthropologists saying that investigators foolishly believed the Pishtacos legend when searching for a motive for the murder, and then played on people's fears by turning the legend into reality. Doctors have also dismissed the theory that the fat was sold for thousands of dollars, saying that it would be pointless to kill people to harvest their fat when it could be easily collected from plastic surgery clinics that perform tummy tucks.

The police's reputation has been severely damaged, with General Miguel Hidalgo, the head of Peru's police, saying that the incident affects the image and respectability of the police. Politicians have also blamed the police for scaring away tourists, with Jorge Espinoza, president of the region of Huanuco, calling the incident a "ruse of bad taste."

The incident has raised questions about the reliability of the police and the media, with some observers saying that the story was just one of many embellished or invented news stories used as a smokescreen to distract the general public from the real issues facing Peru.
details.ground_truth: The Peruvian cop who claimed to have busted a crime ring that killed dozens of people to harvest their fat has been suspended for lying. Felix Murga, the country's top organized crime investigator, said that a gang had killed 60 people to sell their fat at $15,000 a liter. Investigators now believe there was just one victim and his murder was linked to drug trafficking, the BBC reports. Murga appears to have revived an ancient Andean legend of killers who roam the mountains extracting fat from travelers, say authorities, who blame him for damaging the police force's reputation and scaring tourists away. "This has been a ruse of bad taste," a local politician tells Reuters.

**Pacific Crest Trail (PCT) Sees Increased Interest and Crowds**

The Pacific Crest Trail, a 2,650-mile hiking route from the Mexican border to the Canadian border, has seen a significant increase in interest and crowds in recent years. The release of the book and movie "Wild" by Cheryl Strayed, which tells the story of her 1,100-mile hike on the PCT, has been credited as a major factor in the surge in popularity.

**Increased Crowds and Environmental Concerns**

The PCTA has reported that more than 4,000 long-distance hiking permits have been issued in 2016, with many of these permits being for hikers who plan to walk the entire length of the trail. This has raised concerns about the environmental impact of the increased crowds, with fragile desert ecosystems being particularly vulnerable to damage. The PCTA has implemented a quota system, limiting the number of hikers to 50 per day, in an effort to mitigate the impact.

**Financial Costs and Rescues**

The increased number of hikers has also led to a rise in the number of rescues and helicopter evacuations, with 20 rescues already reported in the first 50 miles of the trail in 2016. This has put a strain on essential services and has raised concerns about the financial costs of the increased crowds.

**Changing Demographics and Hiking Trends**

The PCT has also seen a shift in demographics, with more women and younger hikers taking on the challenge. The trail has become a popular destination for those looking to challenge themselves and find a sense of purpose and meaning. However, some hikers have been criticized for their lack of preparation and experience, with some hikers attempting to hike the entire trail with little more than a backpack and a water bottle.

**Call for Responsible and Sustainable Hiking**

In response to the increased crowds and environmental concerns, the PCTA has launched a campaign, #responsiblywild, to promote sustainable and responsible hiking practices. This includes protecting water quality, burying human waste, and building safe campfires. Hikers are being encouraged to take a more mindful and considerate approach to their hiking experience, and to respect the fragile natural environments and communities they encounter along the way.

**Conclusion**

The Pacific Crest Trail has become a popular destination for those looking to challenge themselves and find a sense of purpose and meaning. However, the increased crowds and environmental concerns have raised concerns about the sustainability and responsibility of hiking on the trail. As the PCT
details.ground_truth: Hikers on the Pacific Crest Trail can expect to encounter harsh-but-beautiful landscapes, extreme fatigue, and—these days, at least—traffic. "You can’t talk about the Pacific Crest Trail without mentioning the crowds," Pete Brook writes for Outside. He should know: Brook currently is trekking the 2,650-mile PCT, which runs from the Mexican border through California, Oregon, and Washington to the border with Canada. But don't take his word for it: The Pacific Crest Trail Association so far this year has issued more than 4,000 permits to people planning on tackling at least 500 miles of the trail. To put things in perspective: 300 people attempted to complete the entire PCT in 2006, according to the AP, with about 120 of those making it. In 2014, 1,000 people set off and about half completed the trail. More traffic means more stress on the land, more improperly disposed of poop, and more inexperienced hikers requiring rescue. So what's with the increase in traffic over the past few years? You can thank Cheryl Strayed and her enormously popular memoir-turned-major-motion-picture Wild. After the book came out in 2012, there was a small increase in interest in the trail, the PCTA's Jack Haskel told the AP in 2015. But after the movie, starring Reese Witherspoon, was released in 2014, interest in the PCT exploded. "Millions are hearing about it now and are being inspired," Haskel said. Strayed, more or less unprepared for the journey, hiked 1,100 miles of the trail as a form of therapy as she grappled with personal demons. "However for others that follow her strategy of deliberate ineptitude, things might not turn out so well," writes DontHikeLikeWild.org. "Our advice to would-be hikers: Grow a spine first. Then get out there and hike the PCT." (This female hiker is the "biggest badass you've never heard of.")

**Pete Davidson's Journey to Sobriety and Medical Marijuana Use**

Pete Davidson, a cast member of the popular late-night sketch show Saturday Night Live (SNL), has recently opened up about his struggles with addiction and his reliance on medical marijuana to manage his Crohn's disease.

In an interview with HIGH TIMES, Davidson revealed that he has been sober for the first time in eight years, crediting his great girlfriend, friends, and a sense of good fortune for his success. He also expressed his gratitude to his fans for their love and support, stating that they are a family and that he will always be there for them.

Davidson's struggles with addiction and mental health have been well-documented in the past. He has spoken publicly about his father's death in the 9/11 attacks and has used medical marijuana to cope with the pain and anxiety that followed. In fact, he has been so open about his use of medical marijuana that he has credited it with allowing him to perform on SNL, stating that he "wouldn't be able to do SNL if I didn't smoke weed."

Davidson has also spoken about the impact of music on his life, crediting Kid Cudi's music with saving his life. He has stated that he would have taken his own life if it weren't for the rapper's music, and has expressed a sense of comfort and solidarity with his hero.

As SNL prepares to premiere its new season on October 1st, Davidson's fans are no doubt eager to see what the future holds for the young comedian. With his newfound sobriety and his continued advocacy for medical marijuana, Davidson is poised to take on the challenges of the entertainment industry with a renewed sense of purpose and passion.

**Upcoming Events:**

* SNL Season 42 premieres on October 1st at 11:30 p.m. EST on NBC
* Pete Davidson's first hour-long stand-up special, "SMD," airs on Comedy Central on October 29th at 11:00 p.m. EST
details.ground_truth: Noticed Pete Davidson's absence from SNL as of late? Well, there's a good reason behind it: The 23-year-old has been focusing on his sobriety. After telling High Times last fall that he couldn't function without the medical marijuana he's prescribed for Crohn's disease, Davidson now says, "I quit drugs and am happy and sober for the first time in eight years" in an Instagram post. Rapper Kid Cudi might have inspired that sobriety, per the New York Daily News. Last fall, Davidson told a radio station, "I would have killed myself if I didn't have Kid Cudi," who'd checked himself into rehab a few weeks earlier.

**Sexual Abuse Allegations in Hollywood: A Growing Scandal**

A series of lawsuits has been filed against several high-profile Hollywood executives, including director Bryan Singer and producer Gary Goddard, alleging that they sexually abused underage boys. The allegations have sparked a growing scandal in the entertainment industry, with many victims coming forward to share their stories.

Jeff Herman, a Florida-based lawyer, has been at the forefront of the scandal, filing four lawsuits in the past two weeks against Singer, Goddard, and other Hollywood executives. Herman has a reputation for taking on high-profile cases, including those involving the Catholic Church and the Boy Scouts of America. He has a personal connection to the issue, having been sexually abused as a child and having a sister who was also abused by their grandfather.

The scandal has also sparked a wider conversation about the need for greater accountability and transparency in the entertainment industry.

Herman has called the scandal a "watershed moment" for Hollywood, saying that it is a chance for the industry to come to terms with its past and to create a safer, more accountable future for its workers. The scandal has also sparked a wider conversation about the need for greater support and resources for victims of sexual abuse.

details.ground_truth: Bryan Singer has already been hit with two lawsuits alleging sexual abuse of minors—and the lawyer who filed both of them, plus other suits against Hollywood bigwigs, says more are coming. "I’ve heard from victims about very recent claims of being sexually exploited in Hollywood," Jeff Herman said at a news conference yesterday, according to Variety. He said one coming lawsuit involves another Hollywood "sex ring." Herman also shared photos showing the second alleged victim (with his face redacted) and Singer at the London premiere of Singer's Superman Returns in 2006; John Doe, then 17, says Singer abused him after the premiere, the New York Daily News reports. **Double Shooting in Portland, Texas: A Family's Fight for Recovery and Justice**

On June 23, 2012, a senseless act of violence shook the small town of Portland, Texas, when 19-year-old Mollie Judith Olgin was killed and 18-year-old Mary Kristene Chapa was left critically injured after being shot in the head. The two women, who were in a same-sex relationship, were shot in a grassy area of Violet Andrews Park while waiting to see a movie.

The investigation into the shooting is being led by the Portland Police Department, with assistance from the Texas Rangers. While there is no evidence to suggest that the attack was a hate crime, the police are not ruling out the possibility. Mollie Olgin's father, Mario, has expressed confidence that his daughter's killer will be brought to justice and has stated that "justice will be served."

Mary Kristene Chapa, who is making an "amazing" recovery, according to her brother Hilario, has been able to communicate with her family through sign language. She has made physical progress on her right side, but is still waiting for a response from the left side of her body. The family is facing a financial burden, as Kristene is without insurance, and has set up a donation page on WePay to help pay for her medical bills.

The outpouring of support from the community and across the country has been a source of comfort for the families of both Mollie and Kristene. A Facebook event has been set up for a candlelight walk in memory of Mollie and to keep Kristene in prayers for a speedy recovery, with over 700 people planning to participate. The gay and lesbian community has also shown its support, with Kristene's brother Hilario expressing his gratitude and pride for his sister's identity.

As the investigation continues, the families of Mollie and Kristene are holding on to hope that justice will be served and that their loved ones will not be forgotten. A memorial service for Mollie will be held on Friday at 4:30 p.m. at Limbaugh Funeral Home in Portland, followed by a candlelight vigil for both girls at 6 p.m. at the Violet Andrews Park.
details.ground_truth: The dad of the teenage lesbian shot dead in a Texas park called for justice yesterday. He immediately had a "bad feeling" when his 19-year-old daughter, Mollie, failed to show up for work, said Mario Olgin. “It was not like Mollie," he told KIII-TV. "If she had some place to be she was going to be there.” Mollie and her 18-year-old girlfriend, Mary Kristene Chapa, had been shot in the head by an unknown assailant. Mollie died at the scene, but Chapa is making an "amazing" recovery, her brother told NBC Latino. Mollie has just finished her first semester of college and dreamed of becoming a psychiatrist. "She was happy," Olgin recalled. Now, "she's my guardian angel. I know she's looking down on us, in a better place." He's confident police will find her killer. "Justice will be served," he declared. Texas Rangers have joined Portland police in continuing to investigate the crime, but haven't yet discussed any progress. They haven't labeled the shootings a hate crime, but aren't ruling it out.

**Scientists Uncover Iridescent Dinosaur with Rainbow Feathers**

A team of researchers has made a groundbreaking discovery in northeastern China, uncovering the fossilized remains of a small, bird-like dinosaur with iridescent feathers that shimmered and shifted in the light, similar to those of modern hummingbirds. The dinosaur, named Caihong juji, or "rainbow with a big crest," lived 161 million years ago during the Jurassic Period.

**Unique Features of Caihong juji**

Caihong juji had a number of distinctive features that set it apart from other dinosaurs. Its feathers were iridescent, with colors that changed depending on the angle of the light. The dinosaur's head and chest were covered in long, flat melanosomes that were organized into sheets, similar to those found in the iridescent throat feathers of hummingbirds. Its tail was frond-like, with asymmetrical feathers that would have borne the aerodynamic load of flight.

**Feathered Display and Color Vision**

The discovery of Caihong juji provides further evidence that feathers evolved in dinosaurs for display and color vision, rather than just for flight. The dinosaur's iridescent feathers would have been a striking display, possibly used for social or sexual purposes, similar to the peacock's tail. The researchers also suspect that the dinosaur had color vision, which would have allowed it to take advantage of its striking, feathery displays.

**Comparison to Other Dinosaur Fossils**

Caihong juji is the oldest known animal to have asymmetrical feathers, a key adaptation for flight. It is also similar to the iconic bird-like dinosaur Archaeopteryx, which lived 10 million years later. The researchers compared the melanosomes from Caihong juji to those from modern bird feathers, and found that they were similar in shape and size.

**Taphonomic Analysis and Preservation**

The researchers used scanning electron microscope (SEM) imaging to analyze the nanostructures from the fossilized feathers of Caihong juji. They found that the nanostructures were similar in shape and size to those from extant avian melanosomes, and were not consistent with a microbial hypothesis for their origin. The researchers also used taphonomic analysis to model the effect of compaction during burial on the nanostructures, and found that it was unlikely to have caused the observed patterns.

**Implications for the Evolution of Birds**

The discovery of Caihong juji provides further evidence for the evolution of birds from small,
details.ground_truth: Scientists are now saying there may have been "a more colorful Jurassic World than we previously imagined," thanks to the recent discovery of a fossil in China's Hebei province. Reuters reports that a closer look at the "exquisitely preserved," almost completely intact fossil of a crow-like dinosaur that lived about 161 million years ago revealed that Caihong juji had luminous, brilliantly hued feathers that closely resembled those of hummingbirds. The study in the journal Nature Communications documenting the find explains that "Caihong" means "rainbow" in Mandarin. Scientists think the colorful plumage, which appeared to have covered the bony-crested creature's head, neck, and chest areas, may have kept the dinosaur warm, as well as attracted potential mates; National Geographic compares it to peacock feathers. So how were researchers able to tell what color feathers the creature had from preserved bones? They used high-tech microscopes able to home in on 66 sites on the fossil, detecting tiny cell structures called melanosomes, which underlie pigmentation. Which colors they led to can be found by their shapes, and the melanosomes in Caihong were long and flat like pancakes—similar to those found in hummingbirds, which boast iridescent plumage. Although they can't pick out from the Pantone wheel Caihong's exact colors, researchers say the creature likely sported feathers with a "rainbow glimmer." Study co-author Xing Zu tells National Geographic that Caihong was a predator that spent its days gliding from tree to tree. And "glide" is the operative word, as this dinosaur likely didn't fly: Its feathers were located on its tail, not on its wings like birds, Discover notes. (A study postulated that nearly all dinosaurs had feathers.)

**Middle East Protests: A Wave of Unrest Against Authoritarian Regimes**

A wave of protests has swept across the Middle East, with demonstrators demanding an end to authoritarian rule and calling for democratic reforms. The protests, which began in Tunisia and Egypt, have spread to Libya, Yemen, Algeria, and Bahrain, with each country's government responding with a mix of force and concessions.

**Libya: Crackdown and Bloodshed**

In Libya, the government has cracked down on protesters, with security forces firing on mourners in the city of Benghazi, killing at least 15 people. The protests, which began on February 15, have been the most serious challenge to the 42-year rule of Colonel Muammar Gaddafi. The government has also cut off the internet, making it difficult for protesters to organize and communicate.

**Yemen: Protests and Violence**

In Yemen, protests have been ongoing for 10 days, with demonstrators demanding the resignation of President Ali Abdullah Saleh. The government has responded with violence, with riot police opening fire on thousands of protesters, killing one person and injuring five others. Saleh has promised not to seek re-election in 2013, but the protests have continued, with demonstrators calling for an end to his rule.

**Bahrain: Protests and Military Withdrawal**

In Bahrain, protests have been ongoing for several days, with demonstrators demanding an end to the Sunni monarchy's power and addressing claims of discrimination against the Shiite majority. The government has responded with force, with army units shooting at marchers and injuring over 50 people. However, in a surprise move, the military withdrew from the streets on February 19, allowing protesters to retake the central Pearl Square.

**Algeria: Protests and Police Crackdown**

In Algeria, protests have been ongoing for several weeks, with demonstrators demanding an end to the state of emergency, which has been in place since 1992 to combat an Islamist insurgency. The government has responded with a police crackdown, with officers brandishing clubs and tackling protesters. A demonstrating lawmaker was hospitalized after suffering a head wound when he fell after police kicked and hit him.

**International Response**

The international community has responded to the protests with a mix of support and criticism. President Barack Obama has called on the Bahraini government to respect the "universal rights" of its people and embrace "meaningful reform." British Foreign Secretary William Hague has welcomed the government's military withdrawal and strongly supported efforts to initiate a dialogue.

**Conclusion**

The protests in the Middle East
details.ground_truth: In direct contrast to the "delirious joy" in Bahrain, the death toll keeps climbing in Libya's protests. Moammar Gadhafi's minions killed another 20 people today, bringing the five-day total to at least 104, says Human Rights Watch. Gadhafi has effectively shut off Internet service and forbid media coverage, but witnesses told AP of attacks by police and government loyalists wielding guns, knives, and even anti-aircraft missiles. While the accounts are impossible to verify—and at least one puts the death toll closer to 200—a grim cycle has emerged, reports the New York Times: "Security forces fire on funeral marches, killing more protesters and creating more funerals." In Yemen, meanwhile, President Ali Abdullah Saleh's forces also opened fire on protesters, killing at least one, reports AP.

**Rare Nirvana Discoveries and Internet Archive Efforts**

In recent news, two distinct stories have captured the attention of music enthusiasts and the online community. The first involves the unearthing of rare photographs from Nirvana's first concert in March 1987. Maggie Poukkula, the 19-year-old daughter of Tony Poukkula, a member of the Seattle band Laytem who hosted the fledgling Nirvana in his basement, shared the photographs on Twitter. The pictures show Nirvana's original lineup, consisting of Kurt Cobain, Krist Novoselic, and Aaron Burckhard, with Poukkula joining in on guitar for Led Zeppelin covers. The discovery comes as part of a larger trend of rare Cobain and Nirvana artifacts being shared, including those featured in the acclaimed documentary Montage of Heck.

The second story involves the efforts of the Internet Archive to preserve and fix broken links on the web. The organization is using crawls to archive pages as they are created and the pages they refer to, allowing for a link to the original version to be preserved even if the referenced page is changed or removed. The goal is to create a more stable and reliable online environment, where users can access the original content intended by the authors. This effort is part of the "No More 404" initiative, which aims to support supported sites in their efforts to preserve and fix broken links.

**Key Dates:**

- August 7th: Montage of Heck returns to movie theaters
- July 16, 2015: Maggie Poukkula shares the rare Nirvana photographs on Twitter
details.ground_truth: Most teens don't find music history in their dad's stuff, but Maggie Poukkula's dad is Seattle musician Tony Poukkula, who was a friend of Kurt Cobain. Her find? Photos of Nirvana's first concert, performed by then 20-year-old Cobain and his new band in March 1987 in Tony Poukkula's basement. "My dad showed me them awhile back, but he never mentioned that's what was going on in the photos," Maggie Poukkula, 19, tells Rolling Stone. "I found out because of all the articles. I didn't realize it was such a historical thing. I thought they were just cool pictures of my dad and Kurt jamming together." She tweeted the photos last week, and the post quickly went viral. That first concert featured the early Nirvana lineup of Cobain, bassist Krist Novoselic, and drummer Aaron Burckhard playing original songs and Led Zeppelin covers. Similar early-days footage of Cobain and Co. featured in the recent documentary Montage of Heck has renewed interest in the grunge band. The Emmy-nominated project includes home movies, never-before-heard music, and intimate interviews with Cobain's family and friends, notes SPIN. After being critically acclaimed at festivals across the country, the film will return to theaters on Aug. 7, adds Rolling Stone. (Want a little more Cobain history? Here's the mix tape he made at 21.)

Clarissa Dickson Wright, the renowned British chef, food historian, and television personality, has passed away at the age of 66. She was best known for her role as one half of the BBC's culinary duo, Two Fat Ladies, alongside Jennifer Paterson, who passed away in 1999.

Born Clarissa Theresa Philomena Aileen Mary Josephine Agnes Elsie Trilby Louise Esmerelda Dickson Wright in 1947, Clarissa came from a wealthy and privileged background. Her father, a prominent surgeon, was also an abusive and alcoholic alcoholic, which had a profound impact on her life. She became a barrister at the age of 21, but her drinking career began soon after, and she eventually quit law to pursue a career in cooking.

Clarissa's culinary journey took her to various grand homes, where she worked as a cook, and later to running her own catering business, working on a yacht in the Caribbean, and serving 60 meals a day at her London luncheon club. She also became one of only two women in England to become a guild butcher.

In 1996, Clarissa was paired with Jennifer Paterson for the BBC's Two Fat Ladies, which became a huge success and was later picked up by the Food Network in the United States. The show's irreverent and eccentric style, as well as Clarissa's sharp wit and strong opinions, made it a fan favorite. The show led to a book, "Cooking With the Two Fat Ladies," which received mixed reviews, with some finding the recipes impossible to follow.

After Jennifer Paterson's passing, Clarissa continued to work in television, fronting several shows, including Breakfast, Lunch, and Dinner in 2012. She was also an outspoken campaigner for rural life and a keen supporter of the Countryside Alliance. In 2011, she published a well-received book, "A History of English Food."

Clarissa's life was marked by her fearlessness, conviction, and strong opinions, which often landed her in controversy. She was known to be non-PC and fought for what she believed in, always. Her agent, Heather Holden-Brown, described her as a "marvellous cook, hugely knowledgeable about food and food history, and possessed of a formidable intelligence."

Clarissa Dickson Wright will be remembered as a true British eccentric, a brilliant cook, and a television personality who left an indelible mark on the culinary world.
details.ground_truth: Both of TV's "Fat Ladies" have sung. Clarissa Dickson Wright, one half of the BBC's "Two Fat Ladies" cooking duo, died in Edinburgh Saturday at age 66, the BBC reports. Wright was a former lawyer who filmed four of the "Fat Ladies" series, going on food-related road trips across the UK in a motorbike and sidecar with Jennifer Paterson, before Paterson died in 1999 from cancer. The New York Times describes Wright as a "rebel," both hosts as "irreverent and eccentric," and the recipes as "sometimes confounding." Wright's eclectic working life also included stints as a cook, an author, and a cookbook shop manager; she also ran a catering business, was a guild butcher, and once worked on a yacht in the Caribbean. In fact, she recently said, "I've had a fantastic life and I've done everything I could have wanted to do and more." It wasn't until her 40s, after she'd recovered from alcoholism, that she got into cooking seriously. As for the perhaps-controversial title of the show that brought her fame? "If you're fat you're fat," she once said. "I hate this modern-day political correctness, that you don't call things by their proper name." Her agent remembers Wright similarly in a statement: "Loved dearly by her friends and many fans all over the world, Clarissa was utterly non-PC and fought for what she believed in, always, with no thought to her own personal cost." There's no word on Wright's cause of death, but the Guardian reports that she had been undergoing treatment at a hospital since the beginning of the year.

**Mountain Walker's Fatal Fall: Inquest into Tryfan Incident Reveals Safety Concerns**

A 53-year-old experienced mountain walker, Jane Wilson, fell to her death while attempting to find a safe route off Snowdonia's Tryfan peak in March 2023. An inquest into the incident has revealed that the couple, who were both experienced walkers, relied on a smartphone app to navigate the mountain, which may have contributed to the tragedy.

According to the inquest, Jane and her husband Gary decided not to reach the summit of Tryfan due to the approaching dusk. They then used Gary's phone to find a route down, but Jane went ahead to look for a suitable path. When Gary asked if it was safe, Jane replied that they would only go if they both agreed. However, a short time later, Gary heard a cry and the sound of a rock fall, and realized that his wife had fallen.

Jane's body was found over 150 meters lower down, with a fractured skull and several other severe injuries. The inquest heard that the couple's decision to traverse the west face of the mountain, which is known for its loose rocks and steep inclines, was a contributing factor to the accident.

Detective Constable Tim Bird, the leader of the Ogwen Valley Mountain Rescue Team, said that the change in route was an attempt to avoid the summit, but that it would have been better to have retreated the way they had come or gone to the North Ridge. He also highlighted the limitations of smartphone apps for navigation, saying that there would not have been as much detail on the Ordnance Survey app as on a normal map.

The inquest also heard that Jane was a fit and active woman, who had run in the New York Marathon and the Great North Run. The coroner recorded a conclusion of accidental death.

The incident has raised safety concerns about the use of smartphone apps for navigation in the mountains. Chris Lloyd, a member of the Ogwen Rescue team, said that the route the couple took was "not a straightforward path" and that Tryfan is responsible for "about 30 per cent" of the incidents the team has to attend. Andy Simpson, a spokesman for Mountain Rescue England and Wales, also urged walkers to use a map and compass in addition to electronic navigation aids, saying that batteries on mobile phones have a nasty habit of running out just when you need them most.

The incident serves as a reminder of the importance of careful planning and navigation when walking in the mountains, and the need
details.ground_truth: A hiker's fatal fall in March has been blamed on using a smartphone app map instead of an actual map. The BBC reports Jane Wilson and her husband Gary were hiking on Tryfan in Wales when it started to get dark and they decided to skip the summit. While attempting to descend the mountain, Gary was looking at an Ordnance Survey app while Jane went a short way ahead to make sure the path was safe, according to the Telegraph. The Manchester Evening News reports Gary heard his wife fall, got to safety, and called for help. Rescuers found the 53-year-old librarian's body at the bottom of a 30-foot cliff with a fractured skull. At a hearing Friday, Jane's was ruled an accidental death due, in part, to using an app instead of a map. "Apart from potential difficulties caused by poor detail on an electronic map, batteries on mobile phones have a nasty habit of running out just when you need them most," the Telegraph quotes a spokesperson for Mountain Rescue England and Wales as saying. A detective says the app map "would have been small and not as detailed" and took the couple on a route that was not the safest. A director with Ordnance Survey says hikers should always use the app in conjunction with an actual map "due to the nature of mobile devices when navigating the real world." Authorities also say Jane wasn't wearing appropriate footwear for the hike. (Go inside the futile search for "Inchworm" on the trail.)

**Summary of Recent News Developments**

**Politics and Elections**

The 2012 presidential election is heating up, with several key figures weighing in on the candidates and the issues. New York Mayor Michael Bloomberg praised President Barack Obama as a "credible, formidable candidate" for reelection, while also criticizing his use of billionaire investor Warren Buffett as an example for why taxes should be increased on the wealthy. Bloomberg also praised Texas Gov. Rick Perry for his 2007 executive order requiring sixth-grade girls to receive the HPV vaccine and said Mitt Romney's health care plan for Massachusetts worked.

Indiana Gov. Mitch Daniels, who considered a White House run himself, says fellow Republicans should forgive Texas Gov. Rick Perry's uneven debate performances as the GOP presidential campaign goes forward. Daniels also offered some unsolicited praise for Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.) for his work earlier this year with the Gang of Six senators who were working to cut spending from the federal budget.

**Government Shutdown and Spending**

The specter of a government shutdown because Congress can't agree to another short-term spending bill is "embarrassing," according to Sen. Mark Warner (D-Va.). Warner blamed tea party-affiliated Republicans for the stalemate, saying that they are making every issue a "make-or-break" issue. Sen. Lamar Alexander (R-Tenn.) blamed Majority Leader Harry Reid (D-Nev.) for holding up the bill, saying that he "manufactured a crisis all week about disaster aid when there is no crisis."

**Foreign Policy and National Security**

Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) says the United States needs to leave "all options on the table" in dealing with Pakistan after U.S. military officials accused the country's intelligence officials of assisting terrorist operations. Graham emphasized that Pakistan was engaged in "hostile acts against the U.S." and that it's now a time of choosing, so they should choose wisely.
