Seattle's Sweetened Beverage Tax: A Failure in Implementation

Seattle's sweetened beverage tax, which went into effect on January 1, 2018, has been met with widespread opposition and criticism from residents, businesses, and experts. The tax, which adds 1.75 cents per ounce to the price of sugary drinks, was touted as a way to reduce sugar consumption, raise revenue for important projects, and subsidize purchases of healthy foods by low-income families.

However, the tax has had the opposite effect, driving down sales and revenue for the city. According to a study by the Mercatus Center at George Mason University, Seattle is one of the top three states with the most paternalistic laws, and the sweetened beverage tax is a prime example of this.

Costco, a popular retailer in Seattle, has seen a 34% decline in sales of beverages affected by the tax, while its stores just outside the city limits have seen a 38% increase in sales. Other retailers, such as Fairplay Foods, have also reported significant declines in sales, with some owners blaming customers for shopping elsewhere to avoid the tax.

The tax has also been criticized for its lack of consistency, as it does not apply to sugary drinks that list milk as their primary ingredient, such as many flavored coffee beverages. Starbucks, a Seattle-based company, has rejected its hometown tax, citing the lack of consistency.

The Cook County sweetened beverage tax, which was repealed in December 2017, has also been cited as an example of the failure of such taxes. The tax, which added a penny-per-ounce tax on sugar and artificially sweetened beverages, was met with widespread opposition and criticism from residents, businesses, and experts.

A bill to bring the sweetened beverage tax statewide to Washington has been re-introduced, but local representatives are skeptical about its chances of passing. Some residents and business owners are worried about the impact of the tax on their community, with one lead bartender at a local bar saying that the tax would be "just another cost that we're trying to overcome."

The city of Seattle is expected to make $15 million this year from the tax, which will be used to fund a program that provides healthy meals for low-income families. However, the tax has been criticized for its lack of effectiveness in reducing sugar consumption and its impact on low-income families, who may be disproportionately affected by the tax.

In conclusion, Seattle's sweetened beverage tax has been a failure in implementation, driving down sales and revenue for the city
details.ground_truth: "That new soda tax in Seattle is working out about as well as Chicago's," proclaims a headline at Hot Air. It's not a compliment. The tax, which went into effect Jan. 1, slaps an additional 1.75 cents on each fluid ounce of sugar-sweetened drinks, a group that includes soda, sports drinks, and kombucha. That's nearly double the one-cent levy the Chicago-encompassing Cook County tried, a tax it repealed after about two months. The Chicago Tribune reported at the time that Costco's nine affected locations saw their sales of beverages impacted by the tax fall 34%, while sales jumped 38% at locations just beyond the county line. And Costco is again at the fore of what's happening: Because bulk purchases contain significantly more ounces than an individual one, the impact is starker, as KIRO discovered when it spotted an updated Costco sign for Gatorade in Seattle. The sign details Costco's price for the 35-pack of 16-ounce bottles—$15.99—and separately lists the city's $10.34 tax, for a new total of $26.33. But that's not all the sign says: It also directs consumers to nearby locations that are outside city limits and exempt from the tax. The tax is meant to fight obesity and raise funds for worthy expenditures, but the blog for Citizens Against Government Waste sees the ends quite differently: "Let’s be clear. Soda taxes don’t make people healthier. They don't raise revenue—they drive it outside city limits. They don’t help ease inequality—they make the poor poorer." But some in government seemingly remain hopeful: KXLY reports a bill was reintroduced Monday that would push the tax statewide. (Speaking of Seattle and beverages, the world's largest Starbucks is no longer located there.)

**Tragic Birthday Party Incident: Woman Fails to Stop After Hitting Man in Front of Car**

A 34-year-old man, Jonathan Carlyle Merkley, was killed in a tragic incident at a birthday party in San Diego, California. The incident occurred just after midnight on Sunday when Merkley's female friend, who had been drinking and celebrating with him and their friends, attempted to leave the party.

According to witnesses and police, Merkley tried to prevent his friend from leaving by lying down in front of her car, a BMW BMW. The woman, who has not been named or arrested, failed to stop and ran over Merkley, causing major trauma to his chest. He was later pronounced dead at a hospital.

The police have identified the woman and have impounded a car that "may have been involved" in the crash. However, as of now, she has not been arrested. The police are still investigating the incident and are asking for any information from the public.

The community has been shocked and saddened by the incident, with many expressing their outrage and disappointment that the woman failed to stop and help Merkley after hitting him. "We don't value each other's lives," said Gloria Williams, a hotel guest who witnessed the incident.

The investigation is ongoing, and the police are urging anyone with information to contact them or Crime Stoppers at 888-580-8477.

**Update:** As of August 23, 2016, the police have not made any arrests in the deadly crash. They have interviewed the female friend and are still evaluating the other parties' culpability, if any, in this collision.
details.ground_truth: A birthday party in California turned tragic after the guest of honor was run over and killed by his friend. Police say Jonathan Carlyle Merkley was celebrating his 34th birthday early Sunday at a hotel near San Diego when a woman decided to leave the party. Merkley wanted her to stay and reportedly walked toward her BMW and laid down in front of what the Orange County Register describes as a moving car. The woman didn’t stop, ran him over, and kept going. Police cite witnesses as saying both had been drinking, Fox 5 reports. Merkley, who suffered major chest trauma, died about 45 minutes later at a hospital. Police tracked the woman down and a car that "may have been involved" was impounded, cops tell the San Diego Union-Tribune. The woman was not arrested but police said on Tuesday that the investigation is continuing. "There's a lot we still need to find out," says San Diego Police Sgt. Tim Underwood.

**Humboldt County's High Rate of Missing Persons: A Complex and Frustrating Issue**

Humboldt County, California, has been making headlines in recent weeks due to its high rate of missing persons reports. According to a recent article in the North Coast Journal, the county has the highest per-capita rate of people reported missing in California, with 35 individuals currently listed on the California Attorney General's database.

One of the individuals on the list is Bekah Martinez, a 22-year-old woman who was reported missing by her mother in November 2017. However, it was later discovered that Martinez was actually a contestant on the reality TV show "The Bachelor." Her mother had reported her missing after she told her she was going to work on a marijuana farm in Humboldt County, but Martinez had actually been in the area for a different reason.

The high rate of missing persons in Humboldt County is attributed to a number of factors, including the county's remote and rural location, the prevalence of the cannabis industry, and the lack of standardization in reporting missing persons cases. According to a Journal analysis, Humboldt County averaged 717 missing persons reports per 100,000 residents between 2000 and 2016, compared to a statewide average of 384.

Many of the missing individuals in Humboldt County are adults who were en route to the area when they last spoke to their family, or members of the homeless population who have fallen out of touch with their loved ones. However, some cases are more complex and frustrating, such as the disappearance of Jeff Joseph, a dispensary owner from Los Angeles who was visiting his farm near Weitchpec when he suddenly stopped returning calls from his sister and girlfriend. His sister, Vikki Joseph, has been doing her own investigative work and tracking down leads, but she is not satisfied with the lack of options for what to do with the information she has.

The National Missing and Unidentified Persons System (NamUs) is a federal database of missing persons and unidentified bodies that connects each case with a caseworker who compares it against reports, looks for patterns, and collaborates with families and local law enforcement. However, the patchwork of information available through local and state organizations doesn't offer the full picture that a national registry might.

In an effort to shed more light on the issue, the North Coast Journal has been monitoring the AG's website and comparing several of its listings against any extra information they could find online. They were able to contact one of the reported missing individuals
details.ground_truth: It turns out missing people are pretty easy to find when they appear every week in your living room. The San Francisco Chronicle reports a 22-year-old woman reported missing in November in California was found this week—because she's a current contestant on The Bachelor. The strange story started when Bekah Martinez's mom reported her missing to the Humboldt County Sheriff's Office on Nov. 18. According to BuzzFeed, Martinez's mom said her daughter had gone to the area to work on a marijuana farm and she hadn't heard from her in a week. The Sheriff's Office was unable to find Martinez or contact anyone who might know her, so Martinez was added to the California Department of Justice's list of missing people. That list was the basis for a story in the North Coast Journal about the high number of missing people in Humboldt County. The newspaper shared the story Thursday on Facebook and asked readers if they recognized any of the missing people. Amy O'Brien did. "I was like wait a minute, she looks so familiar," O'Brien tells the Chronicle. "I instantly thought of The Bachelor." The Journal informed the sheriff's office, which contacted Martinez and removed her from the list. "MOM. how many times do I have to tell you I don’t get cell service on The Bachelor??," Martinez tweeted after learning she was a missing person. The Bachelor started filming in September, and it's unclear if Martinez was still filming when she was reported missing. She tweets "the scariest thing" about being a missing person "is that my efforts to conceal The Worst Drivers License Photo Of All Time have been thwarted"

**Reality TV Star Snooki's Pregnancy and Career Concerns**

Nicole "Snooki" Polizzi, a reality TV star from the show "Jersey Shore," has announced that she is pregnant with her first child. Despite her initial denials, sources close to the production of her spin-off show "Snooki and JWoww v. The World" have confirmed that she is indeed three months pregnant and the father of the child is her boyfriend, Jionni LaValle.

**Fetal Alcohol Spectrum Disorders (FASD)**

FASD is a group of conditions that can occur in a person whose mother drank alcohol during pregnancy. The conditions can include an abnormal appearance, short height, low body weight, small head size, poor coordination, low intelligence, behavior problems, and problems with hearing or seeing. The most severe form of the condition is known as fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS).

**FASD Diagnosis and Prevention**

Diagnosis of FASD relies on protocols of observation of the child's physiology and behavior rather than maternal self-reporting. The only certain way to prevent FAS is to avoid drinking alcohol during pregnancy. The US Centers for Disease Control estimates that 3 million women in the United States are at risk of having a baby with FASD, and recommended that women of child-bearing age should be on birth control or abstain from drinking alcohol as the safest way to avoid this.

**Snooki's Drinking Habits**

In an interview with Ellen DeGeneres, Snooki confessed that she used to overdo it on alcohol as often as once a month. She admitted that she doesn't want to be a role model and that living with reckless abandon is not always as fun as it may seem. "It sucks," she said. "You're like, 'What did I do? Why did I wake up in a garbage can?'"

**Impact of FASD on Individuals**

FASD can have a significant impact on an individual's life, including primary disabilities such as functional difficulties with CNS damage due to prenatal alcohol exposure, and secondary disabilities such as mental health problems, disrupted school experience, trouble with the law, and alcohol and drug problems. However, there are also universal protective factors that can reduce the incidence rate of these secondary disabilities, including living in a stable and nurturing home, being diagnosed with FAS before age six, and never experiencing violence.

**Conclusion**

Snooki's pregnancy and career concerns are a reminder of the importance of responsible behavior

(now ClickSoftware Solution), was designed to enable employees to create electronic timecards and track their work hours, but Arias claims that it also allowed her bosses to monitor her every move, including her driving speed and routes.

Arias seeks an injunction and general, special, and punitive damages for invasion of privacy, retaliation, labor code violations, intentional interference with contract, wrongful termination, and unfair business practices. Her attorney, Gail A. Glick, intends to pursue the case aggressively and get her client the justice she deserves.

This case highlights the importance of employee privacy and the need for employers to balance their legitimate business interests with their employees' right to privacy. The StreetSmart web-based management application, developed by Xora Inc. (now ClickSoftware Solution
details.ground_truth: Myrna Arias didn't like the GPS app on her phone that constantly tracked her, so she uninstalled it. The problem: Arias' iPhone was issued by her employer, which required her to run the app constantly, and after she removed it, the California woman was fired. Now she's suing her former employer, money transfer service Intermex, for invasion of privacy, unfair business practices, and retaliation, among other things, Ars Technica reports. Arias started researching the app, then called Xora—its website touts the app's ability to let employers "instantly see where their employees are and where they have been"—and eventually she and some of her colleagues asked a trainer from the app company to clarify. Boss John Stubits then, per the suit, "admitted that employees would be monitored while off duty and bragged that he knew how fast she was driving at specific moments." Arias says she has no problem being monitored while at work, but she complained to Stubits about being tracked in her free time; he was unsympathetic and said she should have no problem because of how much she was being paid by Intermex. "Her manager made it clear that he was using the program to continuously monitor her, during company as well as personal time," the complaint states, noting that he told Arias she needed to keep her phone on at all times in case a customer called. Arias' attorney tells Courthouse News that there was no way to turn the app off, as it would constantly run in the background. "She found it very offensive that they were treating her like a felon," the attorney says. Arias says her boss "scolded" her for uninstalling the program last year, and fired her soon after. (Elsewhere, a woman got fired over a text mistakenly sent to the boss.)

**Freeway Shooting Cases: Update and New Developments**

In recent news, there have been two separate incidents involving freeway shootings in the United States. The first incident occurred in Phoenix, Arizona, where a man named Leslie Allen Merritt Jr. was charged with four freeway shootings that took place in August and September 2015. Merritt's bond was reduced to zero by a judge, allowing him to be released from jail under electronic monitoring.

Merritt's defense lawyers have raised questions about the evidence linking him to the crimes, citing ballistic tests and phone records that provide an alibi for their client. They have also pointed to road rage shootings that occurred on Phoenix-area freeways after Merritt's arrest, suggesting that the true culprit may still be at large.

In a separate incident, a driver suspected of firing a gun at motorists on an Alabama highway and exchanging gunfire with officers was killed in a crash. The incident began around 7 a.m. Tuesday in Blount County, where 911 dispatchers received reports of someone opening fire on motorists on U.S. 278. The suspect's vehicle crashed and he exchanged gunfire with law officers before being pronounced dead.

The suspect's name and the officer's name have not been released, and no other details, such as the suspect's race or the officer's identity, have been made public. Blount County is located about 45 miles northeast of Birmingham.

These two incidents highlight the ongoing concerns about freeway shootings and the challenges of investigating and prosecuting these crimes. The Merritt case in Phoenix raises questions about the reliability of evidence and the potential for wrongful convictions, while the Alabama incident serves as a reminder of the dangers of road rage and the importance of public safety.
details.ground_truth: A judge on Tuesday allowed a man charged in freeway shootings that rattled Phoenix last year to be released from jail amid questions about evidence authorities say links him to the crimes. The judge overseeing the case of Leslie Merritt Jr. reduced his bond to zero and said he can return to his home under electronic monitoring, the AP reports. He was expected to be released later in the day. The reduction of the bond—once $1 million—was a major victory as defense lawyers contend that ballistic tests cast doubt on the claim by authorities that Merritt was behind four of the freeway shootings. "With all due respect your honor, there's no evidence against him to show he's responsible for this," defense lawyer Jason Lamm said. "He is no more the I-10 shooter than, respectfully, you are." Merritt lifted up his shackled arms in celebration as he walked from the courtroom. After the hearing, family members hugged and shook the hands of defense lawyers. Merritt has pleaded not guilty to drive-by shooting, aggravated assault, and other charges. The shootings caused panic on Phoenix-area freeways, where 11 vehicles were hit in August and September. Detectives took Merritt into custody on Sept. 18; in court the next day, Merritt adamantly denied shooting any cars, telling the judge, "I'm the wrong guy." His lawyers immediately began raising questions about the evidence, citing ballistics information and phone records they say provided an alibi for their client. (Also Tuesday, a motorist suspected of shooting at other vehicles on an Alabama highway died after a shootout with officers.)

**Multiple Shootings in the US: A Tragic and Alarming Trend**

In the past 24 hours, the United States has witnessed a disturbing and alarming trend of workplace shootings, resulting in the loss of innocent lives and leaving many more injured. The latest incident occurred at a Rite Aid distribution center in Harford County, Maryland, where a 26-year-old temporary employee, Snochia Moseley, shot and killed three people and injured three others before taking her own life.

According to officials, Moseley was a disgruntled employee who showed up for work at her normal time at the facility near Aberdeen. She used a 9mm Glock pistol and brought several magazines for the gun, which was purchased legally and registered to her. The motive for the shooting is still unknown, and police are continuing to investigate.

This incident is the third high-profile shooting in the US in two days, following a shooting at an office complex in Middleton, Wisconsin, which left three people hurt, and a gunman who wounded four people at the Masontown Borough Municipal Center in Pennsylvania.

The Rite Aid shooting is also a stark reminder of the country's ongoing struggle with gun violence and the need for stricter gun control laws. The incident has sparked outrage and calls for action from anti-gun violence activists, including former US Rep. Gabrielle Giffords, who was shot in the head at a "Congress on Your Corner" event in 2011.

In total, four people were taken to Johns Hopkins Bayview Medical Center, with two in stable condition and two "very seriously injured." One other victim is in serious condition at Christiana Hospital in Newark, Delaware. The Harford County Emergency Operations Center was partially activated, and a family reunification center was set up at the Level Fire Company in Havre de Grace.

The incident has also raised questions about the security and safety measures in place at the distribution center. While there is security and metal detectors in an adjacent building, the Liberty building where the shooting occurred does not have the same safeguards.

As the country grapples with the aftermath of these tragic incidents, it is clear that more needs to be done to prevent such senseless acts of violence from occurring in the future.

**New Study Suggests Acetaminophen May Increase Risk of ADHD in Children**

A recent study published in the Journal of the American Medical Association (JAMA) Pediatrics has found that pregnant women who frequently take acetaminophen, commonly found in over-the-counter pain relievers such as Tylenol, may be at a higher risk of having children diagnosed with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and similar disorders.

The study, which analyzed data from over 64,000 children in the Danish National Birth Cohort study, found that women who took acetaminophen for longer periods and later in pregnancy were associated with higher risks of their children receiving ADHD medication and exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors. The study's authors concluded that prenatal exposure to acetaminophen may increase the risk of a child being diagnosed with hyperkinetic disorder (HKD) or being prescribed ADHD medications.

While the study does not show a cause-and-effect relationship between acetaminophen and ADHD, it highlights the importance of not taking a drug's safety during pregnancy for granted. The study's authors suggest that acetaminophen may interfere with maternal hormones that are critical for fetal brain development, citing a previous study done using acetaminophen in rats and a study of acetaminophen and autism done with humans.

**Experts Weigh in on the Study's Findings**

Dr. Beate Ritz, a professor and chair of the epidemiology department at the University of California, Los Angeles Fielding School of Public Health, says that pregnant women shouldn't worry at this point, but advises them to try to avoid taking painkillers as much as they can until more is known about the association.

Dr. Max Wiznitzer, a pediatric neurologist and associate professor at Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine, says that the study's findings are not relevant to the human condition and that women should not think that they caused their child's problem when the study does not tell us that.

Dr. Jeffrey Chapa, head of maternal-fetal medicine at Cleveland Clinic Children's Hospital, suggests that nonpharmacological ways to deal with pain, such as massages, baths, and acupuncture, may be a better alternative to medications.

**What to Do if You're Pregnant and Taking Acetaminophen**

If you're pregnant and taking acetaminophen, it's essential to consult your doctor before taking any medication. The study's authors suggest that women may want to avoid using acetaminophen frequently until more studies have been
details.ground_truth: The bad news about acetaminophen just keeps on coming: A new study finds that use of the drug during pregnancy is linked to "ADHD-like behavioral problems" in children, CNN reports. The Danish study looked at data from more than 64,000 children and found that those who had prenatal exposure to acetaminophen had a 13% higher risk of exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors, a 29% higher risk of being prescribed ADHD medication, and a 37% higher risk of receiving a diagnosis of hyperkinetic disorder, a severe form of ADHD, USA Today reports. The risk increased the later in pregnancy the drug was taken and the longer it was taken. Women who used acetaminophen for 20 weeks or longer had a 50% higher chance of their children being prescribed ADHD medication. But the authors did not find a cause-and-effect relationship, and they note that "exhibiting ADHD-like behaviors" is not the same as actually having ADHD. Right now, doctors consider acetaminophen the "safest" pain reliever for pregnant women, NBC News reports; the study author says pregnant women "shouldn't worry at this point." What could account for the link? The authors think acetaminophen may interfere with key maternal hormones that impact fetal brain development. Another recent study also found that frequent acetaminophen use during pregnancy was linked with a 70% higher risk of behavioral problems in children.

**Bill Cosby Sentenced to Prison, Accuses Racism and Sexism in His Downfall**

On September 25, 2018, Bill Cosby, the 81-year-old actor and comedian, was sentenced to three to 10 years in state prison for drugging and sexually assaulting a woman, Andrea Constand, at his home outside Philadelphia in 2004. The conviction marked a significant milestone in the #MeToo era, which has seen numerous high-profile figures accused of sexual misconduct.

In a bizarre and fiery press conference outside the courthouse, Cosby's publicist, Andrew Wyatt, accused the judge and his wife of being part of a "sex war" that is currently unfolding in Washington, D.C. He also compared Cosby's downfall to the accusations of sexual misconduct against Judge Brett M. Kavanaugh, who is currently embroiled in controversy after two women accused him of sexual assault and misconduct in incidents dating back to the 1980s.

Wyatt claimed that Cosby's trial was the "most racist and sexist trial in the history of the United States," and that Cosby was a "great educator of men and boys" and one of the "greatest civil rights leaders." He also criticized the psychologists who testified against Cosby and claimed that audio footage of Cosby played in the courtroom was falsified.

Cosby was taken away in handcuffs and is currently being processed at the Montgomery County Correctional Facility. He will serve his sentence at SCI Phoenix, a new 3,830-bed state prison in suburban Philadelphia. The Department of Corrections says he will be allowed phone calls and visitation in accordance with policy, as well as the opportunity to exercise during the classification process.

The connection between Cosby's case and the Kavanaugh controversy is a new development in Cosby's team's strategy, which has repeatedly argued that racism played a role in Cosby's conviction. They have invoked Emmett Till, a black boy murdered after a white woman falsely accused him of whistling at her, and called the trial a "public lynching."
details.ground_truth: Bill Cosby was labeled a "violent sexual predator" by the judge who sentenced him to three to 10 years in prison on Tuesday, but his publicist had a different description: one of the "greatest civil rights leaders" in history and one of the "greatest educators of men and boys." Outside the courtroom in Norristown, Pa., publicist Andrew Wyatt claimed the 81-year-old Cosby had faced the "most racist and sexist trial" in American history, the New York Times reports. Wyatt claimed that three psychologists who testified against Cosby in the sexual assault trial were trying to "make money off of accusing black men of being sexual predators." He also read a statement from Camille Cosby, the disgraced entertainer's wife, alleging that an audio recording played in the trial had been doctored. Wyatt, who previously called Cosby's trial a "public lynching," accused Judge Steven O'Neill of being part of a "sex war" and linked the sentencing to the treatment of Supreme Court nominee Brett Kavanaugh. Vox reports that the strangest moment came when Wyatt likened his client to Jesus. "They persecuted Jesus, and look what happened," he said. "Not saying Mr. Cosby is Jesus, but we know what this country has done to black men for centuries." Cosby, who left the Montgomery County Courthouse in handcuffs, spent his first night behind bars at SCI Phoenix, a state prison that opened two months ago in suburban Philadelphia, reports WPVI. (Cosby's mug shot was released Tuesday afternoon.)

**U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton Rules Out Presidential Run in 2012 and 2016**

In a series of interviews during her Asia-Pacific tour, U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton has made it clear that she has no plans to run for the top U.S. office in either 2012 or 2016. Clinton, who was the Democratic presidential nominee in 2008, stated that she is "very pleased to be doing what I'm doing as Secretary of State" and that she does not intend to pursue a presidential run.

Clinton's comments were made in response to speculation that she might run for president again, particularly in the wake of the Democrats' poor showing in the mid-term elections. However, Clinton emphasized that she hopes the United States is ready for a female president, adding "it should be" and that it is nice to visit countries that have already proven that they can elect women to the highest governing positions.

Clinton's comments were echoed in a separate interview with TV New Zealand, where she defended President Barack Obama and put the Democrats' losses in the midterms in perspective. Clinton acknowledged that the losses were "big" but added that they were not out of the pattern of historical political elections.

**Incident at New Zealand Parliament**

During a visit to New Zealand, Clinton was introduced as "President Clinton" by Prime Minister John Phillip Key, who later corrected himself and referred to her as "Secretary Clinton." The incident was met with laughter and applause, according to the State Department's transcript.

**Clinton's Visit to New Zealand**

Clinton's visit to New Zealand was part of a nearly two-week Asia-Pacific tour, which will conclude in Australia. During her visit, Clinton joined New Zealand Prime Minister John Phillip Key and Foreign Minister Murray Stuart McCully for remarks at Wellington's Parliament Theatrette.

Overall, Clinton's comments on a presidential run have put to rest speculation that she might pursue a second bid for the White House. However, her visit to New Zealand and other countries in the Asia-Pacific region highlights her continued role as a key diplomat and representative of the United States.
details.ground_truth: A day after being accidentally introduced as "President Clinton," Hillary seems to be taking pains to kill talk of a future run in New Zealand's media, reports Reuters. Asked whether she'd "ruled out" running through 2016, she replied, "Oh yes, yes. I'm very pleased to be doing what I'm doing as secretary of state." Asked in another interview whether she could be the first female president, she answered, "Well, not me. But it will be someone ..." The significance? Probably not much, writes Laura Rozen in Politico, noting how one of Clinton's answers is tied to her being secretary of state—a post she likely won't hold in 2016. On another front, Clinton said the midterm losses were "big—but not out of the pattern of historical political elections." She added, "I think the president made very clear was he made decisions which were essential for the well-being of the American people."

**Amy Schumer in the News: Pregnancy, Politics, and Social Activism**

Comedian and actress Amy Schumer has been making headlines in recent weeks with a series of announcements and statements that reflect her commitment to social activism and politics. Here's a summary of the key news:

**Pregnancy Announcement**

In a clever and strategic move, Schumer announced her pregnancy on Instagram by directing her followers to check out the account of journalist Jessica Yellin, who is promoting her #NewsNotNoise campaign. The announcement was hidden at the bottom of a lengthy list of Schumer's recommended candidates for the upcoming midterm elections.

**Super Bowl Commercial Boycott**

In a separate statement, Schumer revealed that she will not be appearing in any Super Bowl LIII commercials this year, citing her support for Colin Kaepernick's take-a-knee protest against racism and police brutality. She challenged white NFL players to kneel as well, asking "Otherwise how are you not complicit?"

**Midterm Elections and #NewsNotNoise**

Schumer's support for the #NewsNotNoise campaign, which aims to educate people about what they need to know versus what noise they can tune out, is not surprising given her passion for politics. She has been a vocal advocate for various causes, including gun control, the transgender community, and Colin Kaepernick's peaceful protest of the NFL.

**Recent Activism**

In the last week alone, Schumer has spoken out about supporting the transgender community, in the wake of the Trump Administration's memo reported on by the New York Times. She also supported Colin Kaepernick's peaceful protest of the NFL by urging Maroon 5 to pull out of the Super Bowl Halftime Show as Rihanna reportedly did.

Overall, Schumer's recent announcements and statements reflect her commitment to social activism and politics, and her willingness to use her platform to raise awareness and inspire action.
details.ground_truth: Amy Schumer took the long way around to announce she's pregnant with husband Chris Fischer. The comedian and actress broke her baby news Monday on the Instagram stories of friend and journalist Jessica Yellin, the AP reports. Yellin, of the site NewsNotNoise.org, showed at the end of a list of Schumer's recommended congressional and gubernatorial candidates the line: "I'm pregnant-Amy Schumer." Schumer had teased the announcement on her own Instagram page, captioning a photo of hers and Fischer's heads atop Prince Harry's and Meghan Markle's bodies, "About to announce some exciting news on @jessicayellin insta page. Please follow her for up to the minute #newsnotnoise she breaks down what’s really going on. She agreed to post a lil noise today for me! Follow her and VOTE!!" "We are 15 days out from the midterms and there is a lot going on," Yellin explained before going into Schumer's "lengthy" list of recommendations, per Bustle. "I wanted to share some news from our community. Maybe it's noise, but it's happy noise," Yellin continued. "These are the recommendations of Amy Schumer, one of the most consistent and early supporters of #NewsNotNoise. Now, read all the way to the bottom. You'll see there's some news down there. Congratulations, Amy." Schumer is known for her liberal politics: She was recently arrested protesting the confirmation of Judge Brett Kavanaugh to the US Supreme Court. The 37-year-old married Fischer, a chef, in February. (Here's why Schumer won't appear in any Super Bowl ads.)

**Tyson Foods Faces Animal Abuse Allegations, Promises to Retrain and Improve Animal Welfare**

Tyson Foods, one of the world's largest meat producers, is facing new allegations of animal abuse after an undercover investigation by the animal rights group Compassion Over Killing. The video, released in August 2016, shows Tyson workers throwing, punching, and kicking chickens, as well as sticking plastic rods through their beaks in a process known as "boning." The video also depicts workers wringing birds' necks, running over them with forklifts, and leaving injured birds to die in "dead piles."

In response to the allegations, Tyson Foods fired 10 workers who were shown in the video and discontinued the practice of "boning" at two of its facilities. The company also promised to retrain all of its live poultry workers on its animal welfare policies.

The latest video is not the first time that Tyson Foods has faced animal abuse allegations. In the past 13 months, advocates for various animal rights groups have been able to infiltrate Tyson-connected facilities and secretly record footage of workers abusing chickens and cramped living conditions for chickens before they are slaughtered.

Tyson Foods has responded to the abuse seen in such videos as inexcusable, while stressing that the abusers were not reflective of the company's institutional commitment to animal welfare. However, with the latest video, the company said it believes it hasn't gone far enough and must do more to stop this inexcusable behavior.

The company is now evaluating further steps it can take to ensure animal well-being procedures are being followed throughout its operations. It has also begun meeting with every employee in its chicken operations that handles live birds to aggressively re-emphasize the importance of proper animal handling and the consequences of not complying with the company's animal welfare policies.

Compassion Over Killing, which is headquartered in Washington, D.C., believes that Tyson Foods as a company needs to be held accountable for the abuse. The group has made formal complaints about the abuse to prosecutors and animal control officers in Mecklenburg, Lunenburg, and Buckingham counties, but no charges have been filed as of Wednesday.

The new video has also spurred Tyson to discontinue the practice of inserting plastic tubes in rooster's beaks, something known as beak modification, that helps chicken growers prevent males from eating females' feed.

The issue of animal abuse in the meat industry is a complex and contentious one, with some arguing that it is a necessary evil in the production of meat. However, Compass

**Donald Trump's Campaign Shake-up and Michael Cohen's "Says Who?" Moment**

In a bid to regain favor in the polls, Donald Trump's campaign has undergone a major shake-up, with the appointment of new senior officials, including Kellyanne Conway as campaign manager and Stephen Bannon as CEO. However, Trump's lawyer, Michael Cohen, has been at the center of controversy after a heated exchange with CNN's Brianna Keilar on the campaign's changes.

During the interview, Keilar pointed out that the campaign was "down" in the polls, to which Cohen responded with the now-infamous phrase, "Says who?" Keilar pressed on, saying "polls. Most of them. All of them?" Cohen continued to deny the notion, asking "Says who?" again, before eventually being told "all of them."

The exchange has sparked a wave of ridicule on social media, with many users responding to Cohen's tweets with the same phrase, "Says who?" The hashtag #SaysWho has been trending on Twitter, with users poking fun at Cohen's semantic nitpicking.

Despite the controversy, Cohen has defended the campaign's changes, saying that bringing on Conway was a "great move" and that she understands the data coming in. The campaign's shake-up comes just a few months before the election, and it remains to be seen whether it will be enough to turn the tide in Trump's favor.

In other news, Twitter has announced that users can now add location information to their tweets, and Hillary Clinton has been leading in most recent polls, including a double-digit lead in Virginia and Colorado. However, Trump has won at least one poll, a Monmouth University poll of Indiana, where he is ahead of Clinton by 11 points.

**Key Takeaways:**

* Donald Trump's campaign has undergone a major shake-up, with the appointment of new senior officials, including Kellyanne Conway and Stephen Bannon.
* Michael Cohen's "Says who?" moment has sparked a wave of ridicule on social media, with many users responding to his tweets with the same phrase.
* Hillary Clinton has been leading in most recent polls, including a double-digit lead in Virginia and Colorado.
* Trump has won at least one poll, a Monmouth University poll of Indiana, where he is ahead of Clinton by 11 points.
details.ground_truth: Donald Trump's lawyer has apparently decided that it's his turn to make the gaffes. In an interview with CNN Wednesday, notoriously combative Trump Organization attorney Michael Cohen interrupted host Brianna Keilar when she said that the campaign was "down," Politico reports. "Says who?" he asked. "Polls. Most of them. All of them?" she replied. "Says who?" Cohen asked again. When she repeated it was polls, he responded, "OK, which polls?” and Keilar said: “All of them." USA Today reports that Twitter users were quick to mock Cohen and are now replying "Says who?" to all his tweets. Cohen, in what Law Newz calls "typical lawyer fashion," also took issue with Trump's overhaul of campaign staff being called a "shake-up," insisting it was actually a "change-up." Cohen also raised eyebrows when he told Kielar that Trump knows about "the African-American problem in this country," the Independent reports. "What I meant to say is the problem that exists in the African-American community," he later said. (Michael Moore says he "knows for a fact" that Trump never really wanted to be president.)

**South Carolina Deputy Fired After Viral Video of Student Confrontation**

A South Carolina sheriff's deputy, Senior Deputy Ben Fields, has been fired and terminated from his position as a school resource officer at Spring Valley High School in Columbia, South Carolina. The decision comes after a viral video of Fields forcefully removing a student from a classroom went public, sparking widespread outrage and calls for his resignation.

**The Incident**

According to reports, the incident occurred on Monday when a student became disruptive in her algebra class and refused to leave when asked by the teacher and an administrator. Fields, who was called to the classroom, ordered the student to stand up or be forcibly removed. When she refused, he body-slammed her to the ground, dragged her and her desk across the floor, and handcuffed her. A second student, 18-year-old Niya Kenny, was also arrested and charged with disturbing schools.

**Investigation and Reactions**

The Richland County Sheriff's Department conducted an internal investigation, which concluded that Fields' actions were "not acceptable" and that he had violated the department's policies. The FBI, the Justice Department, and state law enforcement have also opened separate investigations into the incident. School district officials and the president of the South Carolina chapter of the NAACP have called for Fields to be charged with assault and have blasted his actions as "outrageous" and "reprehensible."

**Fields' Background and Response**

Fields, 34, has been a school resource officer at Spring Valley High School for seven years and has a history of allegations of racial bias and excessive force. He has denied that his actions were motivated by racial bias, citing his long-term relationship with an African-American woman. In a statement, he said he was sorry for how the confrontation played out and that he would have done things differently if given the chance.

**Aftermath and Calls for Reform**

The incident has sparked a national conversation about the use of police in schools and the need for reform. Many districts across the country have put officers in schools since the 1999 Columbine High School massacre, but experts say that schools now routinely summon police to discipline students, often with disastrous consequences. The school district has announced that it will strengthen its training efforts with school resource officers to ensure such an incident doesn't happen again.

In a statement, Sheriff Leon Lott said, "Police officers make mistakes too. They're human and they need to be held accountable, and that's what we've done with Deputy Ben Fields." The decision to fire
details.ground_truth: The school resource officer suspended after videos of him throwing a black female high-school student around a South Carolina classroom went viral Monday has been fired, CBS News reports. Richland County Sheriff Leon Lott announced the firing of Ben Fields Wednesday after an investigation found his "use of force was unacceptable" and he didn't follow procedure during the arrest. According to NBC News, the decision was made after the sheriff's department looked at videos of the incident and interviewed witnesses. "From the very beginning that's what's caused me to be upset, and continued to upset me, is that he picked the student up and threw the student across the room," Lott says. The Department of Justice is also conducting its own investigation into the incident. This isn't the first time Fields, who is white, has faced accusations of excessive force and racial bias. He's been involved in three other such cases dating back to 2005, and he's scheduled to go to trial over a 2013 racial bias incident in January.

**Headline:** Mark David Chapman's Wife Hopes for His Release After 38 Years in Prison

Mark David Chapman, the man who murdered John Lennon on his New York doorstep in 1980, is set to face his 10th parole hearing. His wife, Gloria Hiroko Chapman, 67, has expressed her hope that he may soon be released from prison after nearly four decades behind bars.

Gloria revealed that her husband had told her two months before the murder that he planned to kill Lennon, but she believed him when he said he had dumped the gun and changed his mind. However, when she heard the news of Lennon's death, she knew immediately that her husband was the assassin.

The couple met in Hawaii in 1978, and Gloria was drawn to Mark's dedication to the church and hospitals. Despite his violent outbursts and abuse, Gloria remained committed to their marriage, citing her Christian faith and the vows they exchanged in 1979.

The couple has only been allowed to spend 44 hours a year together during conjugal visits, which they spend making pizzas, watching TV, and making love. Gloria has been praying for her husband's release and believes that he has changed and is no longer a threat to society.

**Internet Archive Effort to Fix Broken Links**

In a separate development, the Internet Archive is launching an effort to archive and preserve web pages as they are created, with the goal of fixing all broken links on the web. The archive will crawl supported "No More 404" sites and preserve the pages that they refer to, allowing users to access the original content even if the linked page is no longer available.

This effort is part of the Internet Archive's mission to provide free access to knowledge, culture, and the web. By preserving and archiving web pages, the archive hopes to create a permanent record of the internet's history and provide a valuable resource for researchers, historians, and the general public.

**No Related News Articles**
details.ground_truth: The man who murdered John Lennon will remain behind bars after his 10th unsuccessful parole hearing, reports the Daily News. Mark David Chapman, now 63, was turned down again this week. No reasons have yet been made public, but the newspaper reports that Yoko Ono sent the parole board a letter asking that Chapman remain in the Wende Correctional Facility in upstate New York—not only for the safety of her and her family, but for his own safety. Chapman, who shot Lennon in New York City in 1980, is up again for parole in about two years. Chapman's wife recently told the UK Mirror that he talked of killing Lennon two months before actually doing so.

**Pentagon's Twitter Mishap: Erroneous Retweet of Trump Resignation Call**

The Pentagon's official Twitter account, @DeptofDefense, recently made a mistake that sparked a controversy. On November 16, 2017, the account retweeted a tweet from @proudresister, calling for President Donald Trump to resign from the presidency due to allegations of sexual misconduct. The tweet also mentioned Alabama Senate candidate Roy Moore and Sen. Al Franken, who have also faced similar allegations.

The Department of Defense quickly disavowed the retweet, stating that it was an "error" made by an authorized operator of the account. Pentagon spokesman Col. Rob Manning issued a statement explaining that the operator "erroneously re-tweeted content that would not be endorsed by the Department of Defense." The operator caught the error and immediately deleted it.

Pentagon press secretary Dana White also tweeted a clarification, which was shared by the Pentagon's official Twitter account. Many Twitter profiles contain a disclaimer saying that retweets do not constitute an endorsement, but the Pentagon account does not.

The @proudresister account, which made the original tweet, links to a website selling anti-Trump collectibles, such as T-shirts. This has raised questions about the motivations behind the tweet and the potential for bias in the Department of Defense's social media presence.

**Background on Trump's Sexual Misconduct Allegations**

The controversy surrounding the Pentagon's Twitter account comes as the White House is once again facing questions about President Trump's behavior. In the past, Trump has called the allegations against him "fake news." However, the recent allegations against other figures in Washington and Hollywood, such as Roy Moore and Al Franken, have forced the White House to address the issue.

Last month, CBS News White House reporter Jacqueline Alemany asked White House press secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders if it is the White House's position that the women who have accused Trump are lying. Sanders declined to comment further, stating that the president has spoken on the issue.

**Twitter's Location Feature and the Pentagon's Twitter Mishap**

In a separate development, Twitter has announced that users can now add location information to their tweets, such as their city or precise location. Users also have the option to delete their tweet location history. This feature is designed to provide more context and transparency to Twitter users.

However, the Pentagon's Twitter mishap highlights the importance of accuracy and transparency in social media presence, particularly for official government accounts.

In her own tweet, Pentagon spokesperson Dana White said the call for Trump's resignation was "erroneously retweeted" and contained "content that would not be endorsed by the Department of Defense."

**The Lovely Bones: A Film Review and Analysis**

"The Lovely Bones," directed by Peter Jackson and based on the bestselling novel by Alice Sebold, is a film that has received mixed reviews from critics. The movie tells the story of Susie Salmon, a 14-year-old girl who is murdered by her neighbor, George Harvey, and watches from the afterlife as her family and killer go on with their lives.

**Casting and Performances**

The film features a talented cast, including Saoirse Ronan as Susie, Stanley Tucci as George Harvey, and Mark Wahlberg and Rachel Weisz as Susie's parents. Ronan's performance as the wide-eyed and innocent Susie has been praised by critics, while Tucci's portrayal of the killer has been described as "nightmare-inducing" and "unsettling."

**Tone and Themes**

The film's tone has been a subject of debate among critics, with some describing it as a "sad-but-hopeful, dramatic-but-gentle fairy tale" and others as a "heavy-handed" and "preachy" thriller. The film explores themes of grief, loss, and the afterlife, but some critics have argued that it shies away from the more complex and nuanced themes of the novel.

**Visual Effects and World-Building**

The film's visual effects and world-building have been praised by some critics, with the description of the afterlife as a "gumdrop-colored hobbit shire" and the use of special effects to bring the afterlife to life. However, others have described the world as "cheesy" and "unoriginal."

**Overall Reception**

The film has received a mixed reception from critics, with some praising its performances and themes, while others have criticized its tone and world-building. The film has a rating of PG-13 for mature thematic material involving disturbing violent content and images, and some language.

**Box Office and Release**

The film is set to open nationwide on Friday, with a running time of 2 hours and 15 minutes. Some critics have argued that the film is not well-suited for the big screen, with its reliance on the musings of a dead, omniscient narrator coming off artificial and emotionless on-screen.

details.ground_truth: Peter Jackson's crafts a fantastic vision of the afterlife in The Lovely Bones, say critics, but some feel it came at the expense of the human side of the story of a murdered teen, adapted from the book by Alice Sebold. "By turns warmly sentimental, serial-killer sinister, and science-fiction fantastical, The Lovely Bones was an unlikely book to achieve worldwide success," Kenneth Turan writes in the Los Angeles Times. "Those mismatched elements come back to haunt" the film version, "making the final product more hit-and-miss than unblemished triumph." "Sebold's Lovely Bones is fleshed out with the perilous, irresistible power of sex—a real world of extramarital sex and sex between young lovers in addition to the heinous rape from which moviegoers are shielded." But Jackson "shies from the challenge, shortchanging a story that isn't only about the lightness of souls in heaven but also about the urges of bodies on earth," writes Lisa Schwarzbaum at Entertainment Weekly. "Through Jackson's art" and the magic of star Saoirse Rogan, Richard Corliss writes at Time, "the obscenity of child murder has been invested with immense gravity and grace." "Some books are not meant to be adapted to the big screen," and The Lovely Bones is one of them, writes Claudia Puig at USA Today. What works in the book, she writes, comes off as "artificial and emotionless on-screen."

**Mexico's Military Under Fire: Video Footage Sparks Scandal Over Extrajudicial Killings**

A disturbing video footage has emerged in Mexico, showing a soldier allegedly executing a civilian lying face down in the street with a shot to the back of his head. The video, which was first published by Diario Cambio, appears to depict part of the military's encounter with fuel pipeline thieves in the central state of Puebla on May 3, 2017. The incident has sparked widespread condemnation and calls for a swift and thorough investigation by the Attorney General's Office and the Mexican National Human Rights Commission.

According to the video, which has been authenticated by the Mexican National Human Rights Commission, soldiers advanced on a car after it stopped, and a man stepped out with his back to the camera. A second man exited from the rear door and lay down in the street. The driver was briefly illuminated as he stepped through the beam of the tail light, then two soldiers rushed in from the darkness and the driver quickly got on the ground beside the other man. A minute later, a soldier pushed a third man from around the back to the front of the car and out of the frame. Seconds later, the man who had gotten out of the back of the vehicle emerged from the darkness and was pushed by a soldier out of the frame in the same direction.

The driver, who remained face down in the street for six minutes, was then shot in the back of the head by a soldier. The video does not show what happened next, but a narrator says more than 1½ hours has passed. The man's body remains in the street and soldiers walk toward the camera and the image suddenly jerks and goes white.

The Mexican military has been implicated in extrajudicial killings before, including the killing of 22 suspects at a warehouse in the Mexico state community of Tlatlaya in 2014. After investigating that incident, the rights commission said soldiers executed at least a dozen suspects after they surrendered.

The Mexican defense ministry has urged an investigation of the video, which threatens to spark a new scandal over the army's use of extrajudicial force. The ministry said in a statement that prosecutors should "comprehensively clear up" whether the video indeed showed the soldier shooting the detained man after a clash last week between security forces and suspected criminals.

The incident has also highlighted the ongoing crisis of violence and impunity in Mexico, which has seen a significant increase in murders and gang-related violence in recent years. According to a report by
details.ground_truth: A video released online appears to show a Mexican soldier executing a suspected oil thief at point-blank range as he lays defenseless on the ground. The heavily edited video, taken from a surveillance camera, shows a car coming under fire during what the military describes as a series of ambushes that left four soldiers and six suspects dead in Palmarito on May 3. As soldiers surround the vehicle, its occupants exit and are forced to lie down in front of the car. Soldiers are then seen dragging a suspect to the same spot. He appears injured but rolls from his back onto his front. Six minutes later, a soldier appears at the side of the frame and appears to shoot the man in the back of the head, per the AP. A dark stain spreads around him, reports Reuters. "There was already concern about the use of excessive force by the military. Now this video seems to give us the proof," says the chairwoman of Mexico's Senate Commission on Human Rights, per the Washington Post, which identifies the man shot as Raul Jimenez Martinez, 46. Mexico's defense ministry, however, says it will cooperate fully with an investigation launched by the attorney general's office on May 4. "Under no circumstances can conduct contrary to the law and human rights be justified," the ministry says. Residents of Palmarito have already taken to the streets to protest the deaths of the suspects, placing the blame on the army, though the defense ministry previously claimed the suspects used residents as human shields.

**Tragic Death of 20-Year-Old Photography Enthusiast at Four Seasons Hotel in Manhattan**

A 20-year-old man from Rockaway, New Jersey, Connor Cummings, fell to his death on Wednesday while attempting to take pictures from the scaffolding of the 52-story Four Seasons Hotel in Manhattan. Cummings, a sophomore psychology major at the University of Massachusetts Amherst and an avid photography enthusiast, was with his 18-year-old friend Dimitri Olivares when the incident occurred.

According to police, Cummings and Olivares had taken the hotel elevator to the top floor and then climbed up two flights of stairs to the rooftop exit. They then accessed a restricted area of the roof, where Cummings lost his footing and fell 25 feet onto the hotel's roof. He was pronounced dead at the scene.

Olivares, who was left shaken by the incident, initially fled the roof and took a taxi cab, but later returned to the hotel and called his mother and informed hotel security about what had happened. He was questioned by police and released without charges.

Cummings' family and friends have described him as a kind and talented individual who was passionate about photography. His fraternity fraternity at the University of Massachusetts Amherst posted a message on Facebook mourning his death and highlighting his "fiery passion" for photography.

The incident has raised questions about the safety and security measures in place at the hotel, as well as the motivations and actions of Cummings and Olivares on the night of the incident. An investigation is ongoing, and the hotel has released a statement expressing its condolences to Cummings' family and emphasizing its commitment to the safety and security of its guests and employees.

**Key Details:**

* Date: Wednesday
* Time: Approximately 10:40 p.m.
* Location: Four Seasons Hotel, 52-story building in Manhattan
* Victim: Connor Cummings, 20, from Rockaway, New Jersey
* Cause of death: Falling 25 feet from a catwalk on the hotel's roof
* Investigation: Ongoing, with police questioning Olivares and releasing him without charges
* Hotel response: Expressing condolences to Cummings' family and emphasizing commitment to safety and security
details.ground_truth: Connor Cummins' family describes him to WABC-TV as a photography devotee—and that enthusiasm may have cost him his life. The New Jersey man, said to be either 20 or 24 years old, and an 18-year-old pal ascended the scaffolding outside NYC's Four Seasons hotel Wednesday night in search of the perfect panoramic picture—but Cummins may have slipped on railings made slippery by the rain and fell nine stories to his death, cops tell NJ.com. An NYPD spokesman tells the New York Daily News that the initial investigation suggests the men weren't guests of the hotel. Cummins, who originally hails from Rockaway and whose Facebook page indicates he's now a student at the University of Massachusetts Amherst, per ABC, landed on one of the hotel's lower-level rooftops around 11:15pm and was pronounced dead at the scene, reports the New York Post. His friend wasn't injured and was questioned by authorities to fill in the blanks; he was released Thursday morning without being charged. "He was a loving boy, that's all I can say," one of Cummins' relatives tells the Post. "I don't know what happened." (There's more suspicion surrounding a Muslim teen's fatal fall off a Seattle building.)

**Truckers' Protest in Washington, D.C. - A Summary of the News**

A group of truck drivers, organized under the name "Truckers for the Constitution," has planned to protest the federal government by backing up the Capital Beltway around Washington, D.C. The group's main grievances include low wages, high fuel costs, and the Obama administration's regulations of their industry. They also express their discontent with the government's spying on American citizens and the country's debt.

The protest, initially dubbed as "Truckers to Shut Down America," has gained significant attention and support, with a Facebook page advertising the event having close to 50,000 likes. The organizers, Earl Conlon and Zeeda Andrews, have stated that their goal is to call attention to the truckers' frustrations and express their disapproval of national political leaders.

However, the protest has taken a more extreme turn, with Conlon stating that they will seek the arrest of congressmen for allegedly disregarding the Constitution. He has identified House Minority Leader Nancy Pelosi, D-Calif., and Sen. Dianne Feinstein, D-Calif., as politicians he will seek to arrest for alleged acts against the Constitution. Conlon has also stated that they will not ask for the impeachment of President Barack Obama, but rather the arrest of everyone in government who has violated their oath of office.

It is worth noting that attempting to "arrest" a member of Congress would be considered a serious crime, and it is almost certain that anyone attempting to do so would be arrested themselves for attempted kidnapping.

The protest is scheduled to take place on the morning of Oct. 11, with tractor-trailer drivers circling the beltway "three lanes deep." The left lane will be kept open for emergency vehicles, but commuters who wish to be allowed past the convoy must have "T2SDA" written on their vehicle.

The outcome of the protest remains to be seen, but it has already sparked a significant amount of attention and debate.
details.ground_truth: Protesting truckers are planning a shutdown of their own—on Washington's beltway. Starting on Friday morning, they intend to fill the road "three lanes deep" with tractor-trailers, an organizer of "Truckers Ride for the Constitution" tells US News and World Report. In a demonstration against what they view as violations of the Constitution, planners like Earl Conlon are calling for "the arrest of everyone in government who has violated their oath of office." Their grievances include truckers' wages, industry regulations, and NSA spying, the Hill reports. They're also accusing President Obama of treason for allegedly putting weapons in the hands of rebels with ties to al-Qaeda. Organizers have received some 3,000 RSVPs, says one. Moving at the 55mph speed limit, they'll leave a lane open for emergency vehicles; vehicles with "T2SDA," or "Truckers to Shut Down America," displayed will also be allowed to pass. "Everybody that doesn't have a supporter sticker on their window, good luck: Nobody in, nobody out," says Conlon. A Facebook page for the event has more than 55,000 "likes."

**Secret Service Scandal Rocks the Nation: Multiple Agents and Supervisors Implicated in Prostitution Scandal**

A growing scandal within the Secret Service has left the nation reeling, with multiple agents and supervisors implicated in a prostitution scandal that took place in Cartagena, Colombia, ahead of the Summit of the Americas. The incident, which has become a major embarrassment for the Obama administration, has led to the forced ouster of two senior supervisors, David Chaney and Greg Stokes, and the resignation of a third junior member of the team.

**Chaney's Facebook Jokes Exposed**

At the center of the scandal is David Chaney, a 48-year-old supervisor who was forced out of the agency this week. Chaney's Facebook page, which has been exposed by the media, shows him making light of his official protective work, joking about a picture of himself standing watch behind Sarah Palin during the 2008 Republican vice presidential campaign. In the comments section, Chaney wrote: "I was really checking her out, if you know what I mean?" The comments have sparked outrage, with Palin herself calling Chaney's behavior "pretty embarrassing" and saying he should be fired.

**Multiple Agents and Military Personnel Implicated**

The investigation into the scandal has revealed that at least 11 Secret Service agents and 10 military personnel were involved in the incident, which included heavy drinking, visits to a strip club, and payments to women working as prostitutes. The agents involved have undergone drug tests and polygraph exams, and agency investigators in Colombia have visited all of the hotels where Secret Service personnel stayed and interviewed each of the maids who cleaned rooms in the Hotel Caribe.

**Congressional Response**

The scandal has sparked a strong response from Congress, with lawmakers calling for leadership changes at the Secret Service and demanding a thorough investigation into the incident. Rep. Peter King, the chairman of the House Homeland Security Committee, said he expected more resignations and firings in the case, and Sen. Jeff Sessions called the incident a symptom of a deeper cultural problem within the institution.

**Obama Administration's Response**

The Obama administration has sought to downplay the scandal, with President Barack Obama's chief spokesman, Jay Carney, saying that the president's security in Cartagena was never compromised and asking for patience as official investigations continue. However, the scandal has become a major embarrassment for the administration, and lawmakers are calling for greater accountability and transparency in the wake of the incident.

Overall, the Secret Service scandal has left the nation reeling, with
details.ground_truth: One of the two Secret Service supervisors to lose their jobs so far over the Colombia sex scandal joked that he was checking out Sarah Palin as he guarded her during the 2008 campaign, the Washington Post finds. **Falling from the Top: 5 Notable Individuals Who Lost Their Stature**

In a culture that often glorifies fame, fortune, and power, it's not uncommon for individuals to experience a precipitous loss in stature. 24/7 Wall St. recently compiled a list of 50 well-known individuals who have fallen from the top, and we take a closer look at 5 of them.

**1. Anthony Scaramucci: The Short-Lived White House Communications Director**

Anthony Scaramucci's tenure as White House Communications Director was the shortest in the position's nearly 50-year history, lasting just 11 days. His tumultuous time in the Trump administration was marked by protocol breaches, conflicting statements, and a profanity-laced interview with The New Yorker. His wife's divorce filing during his brief stint didn't help his public image.

**2. Chris Christie: The Governor with the Lowest Approval Ratings**

Chris Christie, the outgoing Governor of New Jersey, has the lowest approval ratings of any governor in the country and in the history of the Garden State. His reputation was sullied by the Bridgegate scandal, and his decision to fawn over Donald Trump after the presidential election didn't help. His recent decision to lounge on a state-owned beach while the public was denied access due to a government shutdown was a public relations nightmare.

**3. James Comey: The FBI Director Who Lost His Job**

James Comey's time as FBI Director was marked by controversy, particularly during the 2016 presidential election. His decision to publicly attack Hillary Clinton's judgment and announce an investigation into her just days before the election was widely criticized. Trump's abrupt firing of Comey in May 2017 was met with widespread outrage, and the White House's conflicting justifications for the firing only added to the controversy.

**4. Mark Fields: The CEO Who Failed to Inspire Confidence**

Mark Fields, the former CEO of Ford Motor Company, was shown the door in 2017 after his failure to inspire confidence on Wall Street. Despite his efforts to invest in electric and autonomous vehicle technology, Ford shares fell by nearly 40% during his tenure as CEO. His attempts to woo shareholders by announcing a 10% reduction in the global workforce only didn't help, and he was eventually let go.

**5. Tiger Woods: The Golfer Who Lost His Way**

Tiger Woods, the golf superstar with 14 major wins to his name, has been embroiled in controversy on and off the course since reports of his
details.ground_truth: Power: One day you have it, the next you don't. No one likely knows that better than the world's 50 "least powerful" people, described by 24/7 Wall St. as "well-known individuals around the world who have recently experienced a precipitous loss in stature." The top 10: Anthony Scaramucci: Where to begin? Try here, here, and here. Chris Christie: The former head of Donald Trump's transition team has the lowest approval rating of any governor in New Jersey's history. James Comey: The former FBI director lasted not even five months under Trump. Mark Fields: The Ford CEO was canned after shares fell almost 40% during his tenure. Tiger Woods: Blame his DUI arrest and 899th ranking in men's pro golf. Kendall Jenner: Remember that Pepsi commercial? Montgomery Moran: After a wave of issues at Chipotle, the co-CEO stepped down last year. Tomi Lahren: The conservative media personality was suspended from the Blaze after announcing she was pro-choice. Park Geun-hye: The former president of South Korea was impeached and jailed after a corruption scandal involving Samsung. Mitch McConnell: As Senate majority leader, McConnell failed to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act. Click for the full list, which also includes Sean Spicer, R. Kelly, and Theresa May. **Government Shutdown Looms as Congress Fails to Reach a Deal**

The United States is bracing for a partial government shutdown as Congress fails to reach a deal on a spending bill by the Monday night deadline. The House of Representatives has passed a plan to fund federal agencies through December 15, but it includes conditions related to the new federal health law, which Senate Democrats say they will reject.

**House Republicans' Strategy**

House Speaker John Boehner (R-OH) has a powerful incentive to continue to press his party's fight against the health law, as conservatives in the House Republican conference are clamoring for a confrontation over it. Boehner does not want to stand down from a fight too soon, and Republicans believe there is some chance that the Senate will agree to repeal the medical device tax, giving the House GOP a victory.

**Senate Democrats' Strategy**

Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid (D-NV) has taken the lead role in pushing a hardball Democratic legislative strategy, making the Republicans cave. Reid believes that giving even a few small concessions on a short-term stopgap funding measure will lead to bigger concessions on the debt ceiling. Democrats plan to vote down any bill that includes anti-Obamacare provisions, and Reid will reconvene the Senate on Monday afternoon to kill the House's anti-Obamacare provisions.

**Economic Impact of a Shutdown**

The Washington region, home to the largest concentration of federal workers and contractors in the nation, could lose an estimated $200 million a day and see more than 700,000 jobs take a financial hit if the federal government shuts down. The region's economy is weak, and a shutdown will have real costs, according to economist Stephen Fuller. Federal benefits may continue to be processed, but with fewer workers to process them, no new applications would be accepted and "serious backlogs" would be expected.

**Ripple Effects of a Shutdown**

The shutdown will have widespread ripple effects, affecting state and local governments, Meals on Wheels, police officers, and emergency preparedness training. The Congressional Research Service has reported that the 1996 federal government shutdowns shuttered national parks and monuments, stopped the processing of passport applications, and halted new patient enrollments in clinical trials at the NIH.

**Local Governments Prepare for a Shutdown**

Local governments have been setting aside pots of money in anticipation of a shutdown. Montgomery County accounted for a possible $60 million loss in revenue in its current budget, while Arlington County set aside $3 million and Fairfax County has $8 million.
details.ground_truth: Washington is braced for the first government shutdown in 17 years, which will happen at midnight tonight unless lawmakers manage to break the deadlock over funding and hammer out a deal. Here's what to expect from a day of drama in DC: The House has passed legislation to delay ObamaCare for a year and repeal a tax on medical devices, so the measure will return to the Senate this afternoon, just hours before the shutdown is due to kick in. Harry Reid is expected to table the anti-ObamaCare amendments with a simple Democratic majority and quickly send it back to the House—but if any opportunity arises for Senate Republicans to use delaying tactics, expect Ted Cruz to lead the way, the Wall Street Journal predicts. There's a chance of a straight up-or-down vote in the House, but if it becomes clear that it will be impossible to break the deadlock by midnight—which appears to be the likeliest scenario—the partial shutdown could still be averted if lawmakers pass a short-term funding bill to buy another week or so of debating time. So who will blink first? Neither Reid nor John Boehner appears likely to give way. Reid is determined to play hardball and not give an inch on ObamaCare, Politico reports. Reid and his allies believe that giving any concessions now will only lead to bigger concessions to avoid a debt default next month—and they think the backlash from a government shutdown will hit the GOP hardest. Boehner, meanwhile, will face the wrath of his party's conservatives if he gives way too soon—but polls show the public will blame his House Republicans if there is a shutdown. If there is a shutdown, it won't be an abrupt halt to government, but more "like a spending freeze that will gradually spread through the government like ice forming in water," Quartz explains. Federal employees, 800,000 of whom will be sent home without pay, will be the first affected, and the shutdown will also affect scientific research, trade negotiations, and the federal programs that guarantee home loans and provide capital to business. Air traffic controllers will still be at work, however, and agencies that deal with national security and law enforcement are exempt from the shutdown. A shutdown of a few days might not do too much damage, but experts warn that the national economy will take a serious hit if it is prolonged. In Washington, DC, however, even a brief shutdown will cost the district an estimated $200 million a day—and affect basic services like trash collection. "This is serious," the director of George Mason University’s Center for Regional Analysis tells the Washington Post. "The national economy may not notice a shutdown much unless it lasts three or four weeks. But for the Washington area, this is a tsunami."
