NASA's Parker Solar Probe, previously known as the Solar Probe Plus, is set to embark on a historic mission to explore the sun's atmosphere. The spacecraft will be named after Eugene Parker, a renowned astrophysicist who predicted the existence of the solar wind in 1958. Parker, who is still alive, will be honored with a special ceremony at the University of Chicago, where he is a professor emeritus.

The Parker Solar Probe will be launched in July 2018 and will perform 24 close flybys of the sun, with some of them bringing it within just 3.9 million miles of the solar surface. This is the closest any spacecraft has ever gotten to the sun, and the environment in this region is extreme, with temperatures reaching up to 2,500 degrees Fahrenheit and solar radiation intensities 475 times greater than on Earth.

To withstand these conditions, the spacecraft will be equipped with a 7.5-foot-wide, 4.5-inch-thick heat shield made of advanced carbon-composite material. The inside of the spacecraft and its instruments will remain at a comfortable room temperature, thanks to a thermal protection system and a solar array cooling system.

The mission's objectives include tracing the flow of energy that heats and accelerates the sun's corona and solar wind, determining the structure and dynamics of the plasma and magnetic fields at the sources of the solar wind, and exploring mechanisms that accelerate and transport energetic particles. The findings will help answer questions about how stars work and improve space weather forecasting.

The Parker Solar Probe will make a Venus flyby before reaching its first perihelion or the closest point to the sun. It will then repeat that flyby of the perihelion routine 23 more times over its lifetime until 2025. On the final orbits, the spacecraft will fly within 9 solar radii of the sun's "surface," seven times closer than any other spacecraft has been to the sun before.

The mission is a testament to the importance of Parker's body of work, which has inspired many important science questions that NASA continues to study and further understand every day. Parker himself is looking forward to seeing the science from the mission and is sure that there will be some surprises, as there always are.

The Parker Solar Probe is a groundbreaking mission that will revolutionize our understanding of the sun and the origins of physics. It is a reminder of the importance of space exploration and the potential for discovery and innovation that it holds.
details.ground_truth: NASA is going to the sun. More specifically, it's launching an unmanned probe next year that will travel closer to the star than any spacecraft has done previously. "It's a spacecraft loaded with technological breakthroughs that will solve many of the largest mysteries about our star," says Nicola Fox of the Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory. In less scientific terms, per the Telegraph: "We will finally touch the sun." The nuts and bolts: The mission: The 10-foot probe will launch in July or August of 2018 and eventually get to within 3.7 million miles of the sun, about seven times closer than any previous spacecraft. Eventually, it will be whizzing around the sun at a speed of 450,000 miles per hour, reports CNN. The mission ends in 2025. Corona: The probe will actually fly into the outermost part of the sun's atmosphere, called the corona, per a mission overview at NASA that touts "humanity's first visit to a star." The heat: The probe will rely on a 4.5-inch-thick carbon-composite solar shield to help it withstand temperatures up to 2,550 degrees Fahrenheit, reports Orlando's WKMG. Instruments will remain at room temperature. The name: The probe's name has been changed to the Parker Solar Probe to honor astrophysicist Eugene Parker, who's credited with discovering solar wind. Parker, who turns 90 in June, is a professor emeritus at the University of Chicago, and the Guardian recounts that the theory he put forward in 1958 about a stream of charged particles flowing from the sun was once thought to be "crazy." A first: This is the first time NASA has named a mission after a living scientist. "I'm certainly greatly honored," said the man himself, per Space.com. Two puzzles: Scientists hope to better understand two things in particular: "How is the solar wind accelerated, and why is the ... corona so much hotter than the solar surface?" (It's 3 million degrees Fahrenheit vs. 10,000 degrees Fahrenheit.) Why it matters: Generally, solar storms are relatively harmless when they reach Earth, but these particle bursts occasionally wreak havoc on satellites and here on Earth, and they have the potential to be devastating. "The more we know about how these processes work, the better we can get at predicting when they will happen," writes Loren Grush at the Verge.

**Holiday Season Data Breaches: Target and Neiman Marcus Under Fire**

The holiday season has brought more than just festive cheer and shopping deals, but also a wave of sophisticated data breaches that have compromised the sensitive information of millions of customers. Two high-end retailers, Target and Neiman Marcus, have been at the center of these breaches, with both companies facing criticism for their delayed responses and lack of transparency.

**Target's Data Breach: A Sophisticated International Campaign**

According to a 17-page report from computer firm iSight, the data breach at Target was part of a broader and highly sophisticated international hacking campaign against multiple retailers. The report, which was filed to the U.S. Secret Service and obtained by NBC News, details the type of software used in the massive hack, how it was effective, and mentions regions known to have the software coding prowess to pull off this kind of digital heist. The report does not draw conclusions on who is ultimately responsible for the attack, citing an active U.S. government investigation.

The data breach was caused by a type of malware, similar to a computer virus, placed in a store's point-of-sale systems. The report notes that the intrusion operators displayed innovation and a high degree of skill in orchestrating the various components of the activity. The malware works by getting the encrypted data on credit cards in the memory of the POS system, unencrypted, and then transferring it to the hackers.

**Neiman Marcus' Data Breach: A Delayed Response and Lack of Transparency**

Neiman Marcus disclosed the data theft of customer information late last week, saying it first learned in mid-December of suspicious activity that involved credit cards used at its stores. However, the company had told credit card companies around Christmas that it had evidence that credit cards used at Neiman Marcus were being used to make fraudulent purchases. Neiman Marcus has faced criticism for not telling customers about the breach sooner, with some people questioning its decision not to disclose anything to the public until the shopping season was over.

The company has apologized again and said it did not believe the customers' Social Security numbers or birth dates had been compromised. Neiman Marcus has offered to provide customers with one free year of credit monitoring, similar to what Target has offered.

**A Growing Investigation and a Warning for the Future**

A growing group of state attorneys general, including those from Connecticut, New York, and Illinois, are jointly investigating both the Target and Neiman Marcus breaches. The authorities are examining whether the two attacks are related, with both appearing
details.ground_truth: It looks like the hackers that hit Target had lots of other, well, targets. The Department of Homeland Security recently sent retailers and financial service companies a secret memo warning that the Target hit appeared to be part of a larger international campaign, the Wall Street Journal reports, an insight gleaned with the help of Dallas cybersecurity firm iSight Partners. Yesterday, iSight released its own report, saying that a virus it's calling KAPTOXA "has potentially infected a large number of retail information systems"—and noting that the "intrusion operators displayed innovation and a high degree of skill," particularly in terms of the "operational sophistication" of the hack. The Journal shares this feature by way of example: The virus focused on stealing data during the peak hours of 10am and 5pm; the data was housed in a Target server that the hackers later accessed. The virus attacks point-of-sale systems in a way that is "new to eCrime," subverting traditional efforts to protect consumer data, the report warns, according to NBC. Parts of the code, which is impervious to all known antivirus software, have been online since last spring. It's partly in Russian, which US officials think may indicate a link to organized crime in the former Soviet Union. The finding follows reports of similar breaches at Neiman Marcus. That breach had gone unnoticed since July, the New York Times reports today; Neiman's system was only fully secured Sunday.

**Lion Escapes and Fatalities in Kenya and South Africa Raise Conservation Concerns**

In recent weeks, several incidents of lion escapes and fatalities have occurred in Kenya and South Africa, highlighting the difficult balance between protecting people and conserving lions, a species whose numbers have declined dramatically over the past century.

**Kenya: Lion Fatalities and Escapes**

In Kenya, a 13-year-old male lion named Mohawk was fatally shot by wildlife rangers after it attacked a man in a crowd in the town of Isinya, 12 miles from Nairobi National Park. The incident sparked an outcry among Kenyans, with many asking why the rangers did not tranquilize the lion instead. Mohawk was one of the most famous lions in Kenya, known for his distinctive black mane and his habit of roaming freely in the park.

In the same week, a lion was shot dead in Kenya after it strayed from Nairobi National Park and attacked a man on a motorcycle in the Kajiado district. The lion, a 13-year-old male, was named Mohawk and had recently been involved in territorial fights.

**South Africa: Lion Escapes and Conservation Efforts**

In South Africa, a lion named Sylvester escaped from Karoo National Park and was being hunted by wildlife officials. The officials had planned to kill the lion, but the decision was met with an outcry from people who say the lion should be captured and relocated. Sylvester is a 3-year-old male lion wearing a satellite tracking collar that was installed after his previous escape.

The incidents in Kenya and South Africa raise concerns about the conservation of lions, a species whose numbers have declined by 50% in the past 20 years. Habitat loss, human-wildlife conflict, and poaching are some of the main reasons for the decline of lion populations.

**Conservation Efforts and Human-Wildlife Conflict**

The Kenya Wildlife Service has said that it is unsure why there is a surge in lion escapes, but conservationists cite habitat loss as the central reason. As one of the fastest-growing African cities, Nairobi is encroaching on the park, and the government has started building a highway through a section of the park, agitating the animals with constant noise.

In South Africa, the parks service is considering various options, including returning the lion to the park and improving its fencing, moving the lion to another park, donating the lion to a conservation group, or killing it if it causes "massive" damage or is a direct threat to humans.

The incidents
details.ground_truth: An escaped lion, a taunting crowd, and an ill-prepared first-response team came together in Kenya to cause what some are saying was the unnecessary, senseless death of the lion, the country's Star reports. Mohawk, a 13-year-old lion who was one of Nairobi National Park's most well-known creatures due to his trademark mane and rep as somewhat of a "ladies' lion," had escaped from an unfenced section of the park and ended up in the town of Isinya, where he was promptly surrounded by a jeering group of men, the Washington Post reports. Mohawk became upset by the noise and the growing crowd—Kenya Wildlife Service officials tell the AP about 400 had surrounded him—and lunged at a 27-year-old man, who was sent to the hospital with bruises and severe lacerations on his back. The wildlife service already had an animal management team on the scene, but those rangers had only rifles, not tranquilizers. After Mohawk pounced on the bystander, the rangers felt they couldn't wait for a second team on its way with tranqs—so they unleashed what the Star says was nine bullets, leaving the animal "roaring and writhing in pain" as he died. "If the rangers had the right equipment, this lion would not have died," a local resident tells the paper. "You can't keep a lion in the midst of people for so many hours without stressing it. We blame them for this heinous act." The shooting has prompted plenty of backlash on social media, including a #JusticeForMohawk hashtag, but the wildlife service says it had no choice once the lion started attacking humans. "This action was taken as a last resort after an escalation of the situation and a concern for public safety," the Kenya Wildlife Service says, per CNN. A bunch of lions have busted out of the park lately, driven by what conservationists say is the noise from development projects, including a highway being built through the park. (Same ending, different means for Cecil the lion.)

**Tragic Leopard Attack in Queen Elizabeth National Park, Uganda**

A devastating incident has shaken the community in Queen Elizabeth National Park, Uganda, after a leopard attacked and killed a 2.5-year-old child of a game ranger, Elisha Nabugyere, at the Mweya Safari Lodge. The incident occurred on a Friday evening at around 7:30 pm, when the child was seated at the doorway of their house with a maid. The leopard grabbed and ran with the child, and despite efforts to rescue him, the child's remains were later recovered with only a skull and some bones left.

The deceased's mother, who is also a park ranger, was at a neighbor's house at the time of the incident. The father, SSP Francis Manana Nabugyere, expressed his shock and disappointment at the incident, calling on the Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) to do more to ensure the security of their staff and children. He also stated that he expects reasonable compensation from UWA for the loss of his son's life.

In a separate incident, a 3-year-old toddler was snatched and eaten by a leopard at the same park on the same day. The toddler had been left in the care of a nanny at the unfenced staff quarters of a safari lodge, and the nanny heard the child scream for help before the leopard vanished with it in the bush. A search team later found the child's skull and some other bones.

The UWA has launched a hunt to capture the leopard responsible for the attacks, with the intention of removing it from the wild due to the high temptation to eat human flesh once it has done so. The incident has shocked many in Uganda, where leopard attacks on humans are rare.

This is the fourth unfortunate incident for the UWA in recent weeks, following the death of a pride of 11 lions, a French tourist, and another lion in the park.

**Sources:**

* Kampala Post
* Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA)
* Associated Press
* Buzz60
details.ground_truth: A horrifying story out of Uganda, where a toddler was grabbed and eaten by a leopard Friday at the Mweya Safari Lodge in Queen Elizabeth National Park. The 2-year-old boy's mother is a game ranger at the park and was working, and the boy was with his nanny in the kitchen of the family's home in the park's staff quarters, near a doorway. "He was seated with the maid when the leopard grabbed and ran with him," the boy's father tells the Kampala Post. But a spokesperson for the Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) says the boy followed the nanny outdoors: "The maid was not aware the child followed her. She heard the kid scream for help, she intervened but it was too late the leopard had vanished with it in the bush." The staff quarters are reportedly in a protected area that is popular with tourists, USA Today reports, but the family's home is said to be unfenced. A search team ultimately found some of the boy's bones, including his skull, and they were buried over the weekend. The boy's father says he expects compensation from the UWA and that the organization should do more to protect staff and their families. "UWA gave us the coffin," he says. "I have not talked to them about the incident but I would expect something reasonable to compensate me, although my son's life is gone." Leopard attacks on humans are rare in Uganda. The UWA spokesperson says efforts are being made to locate the leopard and possibly relocate it. "The hunt is on with the intention of capturing the leopard and removing it from the wild because once it has eaten human flesh, the temptations are high to eat another human being, it becomes dangerous," he says, per the Telegraph.

**Archaeological Discovery of the Century: Relics of John the Apostle Found in Bulgaria**

In a groundbreaking archaeological discovery, a team of researchers in Bulgaria has unearthed a lead vessel containing the ashes of John the Apostle, one of the twelve apostles of Jesus Christ. The relic, which dates back to the sixth century C.E., was found in a reliquary at a medieval fortress site in Burgas, Bulgaria.

According to Milen Nikolov, the director of the Burgas Regional Museum of History, the early Christians believed that the relic had healing properties, and it was likely brought to the fortress site by a pilgrim who had visited the grave of John the Apostle in Ephesus, Turkey. Nikolov described the discovery as "one of the most important discoveries" in the museum's history.

The relic, which is less than an inch long, is decorated with crosses and is part of a larger collection of lead tube reliquaries that were produced in ancient Ephesus. Only 43 such lead tubes are known to exist in the world, with 20 of them found in the St. John the Baptist Basilica in Monza, Italy, and 16 in a grave in the Bobbio Abbey in Italy.

The discovery of the relic is significant not only because of its age and rarity but also because of its connection to the early Christian church. John the Apostle is considered one of Jesus' favorite disciples and is believed to have written the Book of Revelation, also known as The Apocalypse.

The relic is now part of the collection at the Burgas Regional Museum of History, where it will be preserved and studied for further insights into the early Christian church and its practices.

**Background on the Burgos Fortress and Port**

The Burgos fortress and port, located on the Cape of Foros in the Bulgarian Black Sea city of Burgas, has a rich history dating back to the Late Antiquity period. The site was first excavated in 2008 by a team of archaeologists, including Milen Nikolov, who discovered a large number of buildings, artifacts, and pottery vessels dating back to the 4th-6th century AD.

The fortress and port were used by various states, including the Roman Empire, the Byzantine Empire, the Bulgarian Empire, and the Ottoman Empire, and were rebuilt and reorganized multiple times throughout the centuries. Some of the more interesting finds include a stone block with an Ancient Roman inscription in Greek, a 2nd century AD inscription carved into stone, and a basilica.

The
details.ground_truth: Archaeologists in Bulgaria think they've found ashes belonging to one of the 12 apostles. The team found a small lead container with ashes while excavating a basilica that dates back to the sixth century AD, reports Archaeology in Bulgaria. The markings on the inch-long vessel match those at the grave of St. John the Apostle in what is now Turkey, leading researchers to surmise that a long-ago Christian from Bulgaria traveled to the grave site and returned home with the sample of ashes. Such journeys were common around that time, reports Novinite. “Probably a pilgrim from the Foros Peninsula went on a pilgrimage to Ephesus, and came back here with this relic, which was then donated to the basilica on Foros,” says the director of the Burgas Regional Museum of History in Bulgaria. (The ancient basilica is located in what is now the Black Sea city of Burgas.) Archeologists also found a Bulgarian royal seal from the 10th century at the dig site, which includes a fortress, notes the Week. (Another archaeological discovery: an ancient underground city in Turkey.)

**Netflix Unveils Groundbreaking Unlimited Maternity and Paternity Leave Policy**

In a move that is set to revolutionize the way companies approach work-life balance, Netflix, the world's leading streaming entertainment platform, has announced an unlimited maternity and paternity leave policy for all its employees. The policy, which was unveiled on Tuesday, allows new parents to take off as much time as they want during the first year after their child's birth or adoption, without any restrictions or penalties.

According to Tawni Cranz, Netflix's Chief Talent Officer, the company's goal is to foster a "freedom and responsibility" culture that gives employees the flexibility and confidence to balance the needs of their growing families without worrying about work or finances. The policy is designed to be flexible, allowing parents to return part-time, full-time, or return and then go back out as needed, with Netflix continuing to pay them normally.

The move is seen as a major coup for Netflix, which is competing for the most talented individuals in the field. Experience has shown that people perform better at work when they're not worrying about home, and this new policy, combined with the company's unlimited time off policy, allows employees to be supported during the changes in their lives and return to work more focused and dedicated.

The policy is also notable for extending leave to fathers, as well as understanding that the entire first year after childbirth can be challenging for new parents. Students must abide by clear standards of behavior. We do not tolerate anti-semitism or any other form of racism."

**Kansas City Couple Robbed and Shot at in Craigslist Car Sale Deal**

A Kansas City couple and their 16-year-old son were robbed and shot at while trying to purchase a car listed on Craigslist. The incident occurred on Wednesday night at an apartment complex in the 5500 block of East 84th Street. The couple, who were not identified, were approached by a man who identified himself as "AJ" and were taken for a test drive in a 2007 Hyundai Sonata. After the test drive, the suspects, who were armed with black handguns, demanded money from the couple. When the woman was pointed at by the suspects, the husband stepped in front of her and was shot four times, suffering serious injuries. The woman's right upper arm was shattered by a single bullet, but the 16-year-old son was not injured.

**Police Investigation and Reward**

The Kansas City Police Department is investigating the incident and has released four people as persons of interest in the crimes. A reward of up to $7,000 is being offered for information leading to the arrest of the suspects. Liberty-based Ferrellgas has contributed $5,000 to the reward, and the company's employee, who was injured in the incident, is a kind and considerate man and a great father.

**Similarities to Previous Robbery**

The incident is similar to a previous robbery that occurred just hours earlier, where a man was robbed and shot at while trying to sell an Xbox on Craigslist. In both cases, the victims were approached by suspects who were armed with black handguns and demanded money. The police are investigating the possibility of a connection between the two incidents.

**Safety Tips**

The police are advising people to use caution when meeting someone for an online sale and to trust their instincts. They recommend going to a crowded area, a business, or a police station, and not bringing cash. Instead, use money orders or cashiers checks.

**Incident Details**

* Date: Wednesday night
* Time: 7:30 p.m.
* Location: Apartment complex in the 5500 block of East 84th Street
* Suspects: Three black men in their late 20s or early 30s
* Reward: Up to $7,000 for information leading to the arrest of the suspects
* Police investigation: Four people released as persons of interest in the crimes
details.ground_truth: A Kansas City couple who went to check out a car they saw on Craigslist were shot and robbed Wednesday night, USA Today reports. They had wanted to purchase the vehicle for their 16-year-old son, who was with them but not injured. Police say the 53-year-old dad and his son had test-driven a 2007 Hyundai Sonata and were discussing payment when the seller said he had to call his wife and momentarily walked away, according to KCTV. That’s when two other men reportedly approached the family and demanded cash, then started shooting. As the dad tried to keep his wife and son out of the barrage of bullets, he was shot four times in the chest, stomach, and legs, while his 55-year-old wife took a bullet to her upper right arm, which was “shattered,” reports the TV station. The men took the wife's purse, which had $3,000 in cash for the car purchase, say police. Authorities believe the incident may be related to an earlier armed robbery that day in which a Kansas City man says he tried to sell his Xbox and gaming accessories to a man who saw his ad on Craigslist, reports the Kansas City Star. As the potential buyer was examining the wares, two other men reportedly arrived sporting a gun; the three men then lifted the Xbox and other items and ran into nearby woods, police say.

**Data Brokers Exposed: Selling Sensitive Personal Information to Marketers**

A recent Senate Commerce Committee report and a subsequent hearing have shed light on the multibillion-dollar data industry, revealing that companies are selling deeply personal and sensitive information to marketers. The report, which queried nine major data companies and invited testimony from privacy groups, found that these companies own an astonishing number of consumer profiles and are selling information in categories such as rape victims, people with cancer, and those suffering from HIV and AIDS.

The data companies, including Acxiom, Experian, and Epsilon, refused to explain how they collected their data or who they were selling it to, citing confidentiality clauses in their contracts. However, Equifax, which was cooperative with the Committee, revealed that they had information as specific as a consumer's purchasing habits, medical history, and even their use of laxatives or yeast infection products.

The World Privacy Forum, a nonprofit nonprofit, also uncovered these lists while investigating how data brokers collect and sell consumer information. The lists include the home addresses of police officers, a mailing list for domestic violence shelters, and a list of people with addictive behaviors towards drug and alcohol. The mere existence of these lists highlights the need for increased government regulations, said World Privacy Forum executive director Pam Dixon.

Currently, data brokers are required by federal law to maintain the privacy of a consumer's data only if it is used for credit, employment, insurance, or housing. However, medical information that data brokers get elsewhere, such as from the purchase of over-the-counter drugs and other health care items, is fair game. In some cases, such lists could put people in harm's way, said Dixon.

The hearing came after a year-long Senate committee investigation into the $156 billion data brokerage industry. The Federal Trade Commission has also called on major data brokers to increase transparency into their data practices. Committee chairman John "Jay" Rockefeller criticized several of the country's largest data brokers for resisting the oversight and said he would continue to push for information on how they get data and who they sell it to.

The revelations have sparked a call to action for lawmakers to remove unsafe, unfair, and deplorable lists from circulation. As Dixon said, "This is where I urge Congress to take action."
details.ground_truth: Secretive data companies are tracking almost every American's every move online—and compiling and selling disturbingly targeted lists based on that spying, a new Senate Commerce Committee report concludes. That includes lists of rape victims, people suffering from ailments including HIV, AIDS, and dementia, and people with substance abuse problems, a privacy group said in a hearing yesterday, as per CNN. Mailing addresses for police officers and domestic violence shelters—the latter of which are usually protected by law—are also for sale, as are lists based on demographics and economics, including "Ethnic Second-City Strugglers," and "Rural and Barely Making It." The World Privacy Forum urged Congress to act to "remove unsafe, unfair, and overall just deplorable lists from circulation." The Direct Marketing Association released a statement saying that while the lists are occasionally "used to disparage certain groups," they represented a "tiny minority" of marketing products. Overall the committee looked at nine companies, some of which refused to explain how they got their data or who they sold it to, Gawker reports. One company admitted it had sold social security numbers and banking information to an alleged identity theft ring.

**Astronomers Discover Evidence of a Ninth Planet in Our Solar System**

A team of researchers from the California Institute of Technology (Caltech) has announced that they have found new evidence of a giant icy planet lurking in the outer reaches of our solar system, which they are calling "Planet Nine." The planet is estimated to be five to 10 times the mass of the Earth and is thought to be orbiting billions of miles beyond the orbit of Neptune. The researchers, led by Mike Brown and Konstantin Batygin, used mathematical and computer modeling to infer the existence of the planet, which they believe is exerting a gravitational influence on the orbits of smaller, icy objects in the outer solar system.

Brown, who is a professor at Caltech, has stated that he believes the evidence for Planet Nine is strong and that it is likely to be confirmed in the near future. He has suggested that it would be better for scientists to focus on the study of Pluto as an interesting and unique member of the Kuiper Belt rather than debating its status as a planet.

**Planet X or Planet Nine?**

The discovery of Planet Nine has led to some confusion and debate over the use of the term "Planet X." However, the researchers who made the discovery have stated that they are calling the planet "Planet Nine" rather than "Planet X" in order to avoid confusion and to reflect the fact that the planet is the ninth in our solar system.

**The Search for Planet Nine Continues**

details.ground_truth: Pluto is gonna be PO'd. While the dwarf planet tries to fight its way back into the good graces of Those Who Deem What Counts as a Planet, another icy orb even further out may snatch that designation first. Astronomers at the California Institute of Technology say a giant celestial body "lurking at the end of our solar system," as the Washington Post puts it, may actually be a planet, and they're even already calling it "Planet Nine." In their research published in the Astronomical Journal, the sky-watching scientists—one of whom is known as the "Pluto killer" for his role in getting Pluto demoted—think their find is five to 10 times as massive as Earth, and per the AP, almost as big as Neptune and orbiting billions of miles past that planet's orbit. Michael "Pluto Killer" Brown and Konstantin Batygin haven't seen the supposed planet directly, but say they can infer the "massive perturber" exists by how the orbits of smaller bodies nearby are affected by its gravitational pull, the Post notes. What's interesting is that Brown and Batygin originally set out to disprove the existence of Planet Nine. "We thought their idea was crazy," Brown says of the scientists who originally floated the idea of a large, hidden planet. But as they did their own research, they soon came to their own conclusion in what Brown calls a "jaw-dropping moment" that Planet Nine could be the real deal. Now they're simply hoping more astronomers join in to actually try to spot the alleged planet—and they're not concerned it will face Pluto's fate. "That's not even a question—it's definitely a planet," Brown says. Not everyone's convinced. "I have seen many, many such claims in my career," a planetary scientist at Colorado's Southwest Research Institute tells Nature. "And all of them have been wrong." (The Washington Post caught up with Brown for a Q&A on the latest Pluto-killing endeavor.)

**Libya's Human Crisis: Thousands Missing, Mass Graves, and Brutal Prisons**

The ongoing conflict in Libya has led to a severe human crisis, with thousands of people missing and feared to be held in underground prisons or mass graves. According to rebel leaders, nearly 60,000 Libyans have disappeared since the six-month-long conflict began, with only about 10,000 prisoners accounted for. The situation is particularly dire in the notorious Abu Salim facility in Tripoli, where Abdul Rauf, a 25-year-old rebel fighter, was held for 48 days. He shared a small cell with 70 people, all sleeping on the floor, and told them about the revolution in the streets against Moammar Gaddafi.

Abdul Rauf's family was reunited with him on Sunday, and he described the brutal conditions in the prison, including being beaten and jabbed with electric prods every morning. His release was made possible by guards secretly working against Gaddafi, who unlocked cell and prison doors the day rebels converged on the capital last week.

In another part of the country, a volunteer volunteer helped remove the charred bodies of nearly 150 prisoners from a warehouse in Salahuddin, Libya. Muneer Masoud Own, 33, said he and his 30-year-old brother were detained by pro-Gaddafi forces for reasons that were not explained and were kept in the warehouse for 18 days. The detainees had no food or water, and the warehouse filled with the stench of human waste. Just before sunset on the promised day of release, guards opened the warehouse doors, but the door did not lead to freedom. They tossed a grenade into the warehouse and started shooting, killing dozens. Own managed to escape, but he has not heard from his brother since that day and assumes that he did not survive.

The situation in Libya is dire, with thousands of people missing and feared to be held in underground prisons or mass graves. The international community must come together to help the Libyan people and bring an end to the human crisis.
details.ground_truth: Troops loyal to Moammar Gadhafi massacred some 150 detainees last week, according to volunteers who cleared charred bodies from the warehouse that had apparently served as the detainees’ prison. An escaped prisoner told his story to CNN: He and his brother were handcuffed and blindfolded for no apparent reason while out walking earlier this month. They were thrown into the warehouse with 60 others ages 17 to 70. The number inside quickly reached 175 as loyalists continued to imprison people. The detainees were given no food or water for days, said Muneer Own. Finally, the troops said they would set them free—but instead hurled a grenade at them and opened fire. About 25 people were able to escape, Own said; his brother apparently was not one of them. The mass of bodies was discovered when rebels eventually took over the area. Many remain unidentified, part of a growing number of prisoners whose whereabouts remain unknown, notes the Washington Post. Thousands—some of whom have been jailed for decades—could be in underground prisons or mass graves, rebels fear. Click through for one family’s story.

**Yo-Yo Dieting and Heart Health: A Growing Concern**

Recent studies have shed light on the alarming consequences of yo-yo dieting, particularly for individuals with pre-existing coronary heart disease (CHD). Also known as weight cycling or the yo-yo effect, this phenomenon refers to repeated cycles of weight loss and weight gain. Research suggests that this pattern of weight fluctuation may significantly increase the risk of heart attack, stroke, and death in people with CHD.

**The Studies**

Two separate studies, both published in the New England Journal of Medicine, have investigated the relationship between yo-yo dieting and heart health. The first study, led by Dr. Sripal Bangalore, analyzed the data of 9,509 men and women with CHD, aged between 35 and 75 years. The researchers found that those who experienced the largest changes in body weight had a 117 percent higher risk of heart attacks, 124 percent higher risk of deaths, and 136 percent higher risk of strokes, compared to those with the smallest body weight changes.

The second study, also led by Dr. Bangalore, built on the findings of the first study, revealing how yo-yo dieting may impact the health of individuals with CHD. The researchers found that subjects with the greatest changes in body weight experienced a weight fluctuation of up to 3.9 kilograms (around 8.6 pounds) during follow-up, while those with the smallest body weight changes had weight fluctuations of 0.9 kilograms (around 2 pounds).

**The Risks**

The studies highlight the alarming risks associated with yo-yo dieting, particularly for individuals with CHD. These risks include:

* Increased risk of heart attack (117 percent higher)
* Increased risk of death (124 percent higher)
* Increased risk of stroke (136 percent higher)
* Increased risk of new-onset diabetes

**The Implications**

The findings of these studies raise concerns about the importance of maintaining a stable weight, particularly for individuals with CHD. Dr. Bangalore notes that "our findings suggest that we need to be concerned about weight fluctuation in this group that is already at high risk due to coronary disease." The researchers believe that their results warrant further investigation, and that examining how to help Americans keep weight off, rather than having it go up and down, is a crucial next step.

**Conclusion**

Yo-yo dieting is a growing concern, particularly for individuals with pre-existing coronary heart disease. The studies highlighted above shed light on the alarming
details.ground_truth: Losing weight is one thing, keeping it off quite another. And now researchers are finding that among those with coronary heart disease (CHD)—which the Mayo Clinic reports develops from damaged or diseased blood vessels typically caused by blockage and inflammation—yo-yo dieting may dramatically increase the risk of stroke, heart attack, and death. Reporting in the New England Journal of Medicine, researchers say they followed nearly 10,000 men and women with CHD between the ages of 35 and 75 for about five years, monitoring changes in body weight and other health outcomes. The takeaway? "It's important to lose weight, but this data says you have to keep it off," lead researcher Dr. Sripal Bangalore at the New York University School of Medicine tells the New York Times. The highest weight fluctuation was on average 8.6 pounds over five years, while the smallest body weight changes averaged closer to two pounds, reports Medical News Today. Those who were already overweight or obese at the beginning of the study and who experienced the highest level of weight fluctuation experienced 124% more deaths, 136% more strokes, and 117% more heart attacks, meaning these CHD patients were more than twice as likely to suffer these negative outcomes in just a few years than people with CHD whose weight only fluctuated two pounds. This doesn't pinpoint a cause, as pre-existing heart problems could be the culprit, but researchers hope to study this correlation further. (Yo-yo dieting is bad for women's hearts in general.)

**Odessa Funeral Home Theft: Family Seeks Justice for Stolen Wedding Ring**

A disturbing incident has shaken the community of Odessa, Texas, after a woman was caught on camera stealing a wedding ring from a deceased woman's body at the Sunset Memorial Gardens & Funeral Home. The theft, which occurred on April 8th, has left the family of the deceased woman, Lois Hicks, heartbroken and seeking justice.

According to the Odessa Police Department, the suspect is described as a heavy-set white female wearing a brown long sleeve sweater, black sweat pants, and black sandals. She was seen fleeing the scene in a red or maroon 4-door Saturn. The police have released higher-quality video footage of the suspect and are urging anyone with information to come forward.

Lois Hicks' daughter, Vel McKee, spoke out about the incident, saying that it was "sickening" and "horrible." She explained that the wedding ring was a symbol of her mother's love and devotion to her husband, Gayle, who passed away three years ago. McKee and her sister, Brenda Vaughan, had only been gone for 15 minutes when they discovered the ring was missing.

The funeral home's general manager, Bill Vallie, said that the video surveillance showed the unidentified woman parking her car, getting out of it, and entering the building. He also stated that several shots of the vehicle's license plate can be enhanced for close-ups, and Hicks' family is planning to press charges.

Jewelry theft from the dead at funeral homes is not a new phenomenon, but Vallie said that this incident was particularly brazen and disturbing. The Odessa funeral home has been in operation since 1999, and Vallie has worked in the industry for 40 years, making this the first time such an incident has occurred at the home.

The Odessa Police Department and Odessa Crime Stoppers are urging anyone with information about the woman or her whereabouts to come forward. The police department's number is 335-5784, and Crime Stoppers' number is 333-8477.
details.ground_truth: A Texas woman was caught on surveillance video stealing a wedding ring off the finger of a dead woman Friday, the Odessa American reports. The body of Lois Hicks, 88, was in an open casket at Odessa's Sunset Funeral Home. The woman's family had just left the funeral home after a visitation service when a stranger parked outside, came in, and asked to use the restroom. Instead, the funeral home's manager says, she walked into the room with Hicks' coffin, grabbed the ring off her finger, and left. Hicks' daughter says some of her mother's skin was actually torn off during the theft. "I can't believe someone would be that low," she says. The surveillance video captured the alleged thief's license plate, and the family is pressing charges. The incident is being investigated as a felony, KOSA reports. The suspect is described as a heavy-set white woman who was wearing a brown long-sleeved sweater, black sweat pants, and black sandals at the time; she drove off in a red or maroon four-door Saturn.

**Tragic Death of 3-Year-Old Girl at Hawaii Dentist's Office**

A devastating incident has shaken the community in Hawaii after a 3-year-old girl, Finley Puleo Boyle, died following a dental procedure at Island Dentistry for Children in Kailua. According to the autopsy report by Honolulu Chief Medical Examiner Dr. Christopher Happy, the girl's death was likely caused by the sedatives and local anesthesia administered during the procedure.

Finley was taken to the dentist's office on December 3, 2013, for a root canal and cavity fillings. The medical examiner's report revealed that she was given five different drugs, including Demerol, hydroxyzine, and chlorohydrate, as well as laughing gas and an injection of lidocaine with epinephrine. Immediately after the lidocaine injection, Finley became unresponsive and went into cardiopulmonary arrest.

The Boyle family, consisting of parents Ashley and Evan, filed a negligence lawsuit against Dr. Lilly Geyer and her practice while their daughter was in a coma. The lawsuit alleged that Finley was not properly monitored while she was sedated, with her oxygen levels not checked for 26 minutes instead of the recommended every five minutes. The family also claimed that no one at the dentist's office administered CPR, and a doctor from another office had to be called to assist.

The medical examiner's office ultimately ruled the death an accident, but no criminal charges have been brought against Dr. Geyer. The incident has led to a tightening of oversight of dental sedation in Hawaii, with new state rules taking effect in January 2014.

Dr. Geyer's attorney, John Nishimoto, has called the allegations against his client "unproven," while the Boyle family's attorney, Rick Fried, has declined to comment on the autopsy report. The dentist's office has since closed, and the community is left to mourn the loss of a young life and reflect on the importance of proper medical care and oversight.

**Key Facts:**

- Date of incident: December 3, 2013
- Cause of death: Sedatives and local anesthesia administered during dental procedure
- Autopsy report: Released by Honolulu Chief Medical Examiner Dr. Christopher Happy
- Lawsuit: Filed by Boyle family against Dr. Lilly Geyer and her practice
- New state rules: Tightening oversight of dental sedation in Hawaii, took effect in January 2014
details.ground_truth: Drugs are likely to blame for the death of Finley Boyle, the 3-year-old Hawaii girl who died a month after going into cardiac arrest during a root canal at a Kailua dentist's office on Dec. 3, according to the official autopsy report. The report says the combination of sedatives and anesthesia Finley received during the procedure probably caused her death, the AP reports. The report noted that Finley was healthy, and ruled out any signs of an underlying heart condition or an allergic reaction. The medical examiner's office ruled the toddler's death an accident, and no charges have been brought against Dr. Lilly Geyer, KHON reports; however, Finley's family has filed a lawsuit claiming Finley was not monitored for 26 minutes while she was under sedation. Geyer's office has since shut down, but Geyer's attorney calls the Boyle family's allegations "unproven." (Meanwhile, a young mother in Hawaii is in a coma following wisdom teeth surgery.)

**O.J. Simpson's Parole and the Resurgence of a Notorious Figure**

In a recent development, former NFL star and infamous celebrity O.J. Simpson was granted parole by a Nevada parole board after serving nine years of a nine-to-33-year sentence for an armed robbery and kidnapping conviction in 2007. The parole hearing, which garnered wall-to-wall coverage from cable news shows, recalled the "trial of the century" and the many months that transfixed a nation over two decades ago.

Simpson, now 70, appeared before the board and apologized for his role in the crime, stating that he had done his time as well and as respectfully as anyone can. He also claimed that associates misled him during the robbery and then turned on him in court, and that he had lived a "conflict-free life." However, one of those associates, Tom Riccio, disputed Simpson's account, stating that Simpson was the one who orchestrated the robbery and gathered up men with guns.

The parole board members voted unanimously to grant Simpson parole, with board member Tony Corda grading him a "low risk to reoffend." Simpson is expected to be released as early as October and plans to move to his home in Florida.

The resurgence of O.J. Simpson's story in recent years, through the award-winning documentary "O.J.: Made in America" and the FX true-crime drama "The People v. O.J. Simpson," has brought attention to the themes of criminal justice and race, trust in police, celebrity and domestic violence that remain remarkably resonant in modern culture.

**The Rise and Fall of O.J. Simpson**

A photo gallery of O.J. Simpson's life, from his college days as a University of Southern California running back to his recent parole hearing, highlights the highs and lows of his career. Simpson's football career, which included stints with the Buffalo Bills and the San Francisco 49ers, was marked by his charisma and talent on the field. However, his personal life was marred by the murders of his ex-wife Nicole Brown Simpson and her friend Ronald Goldman in 1994, for which he was acquitted of murder charges.

Simpson's subsequent conviction in the 2007 armed robbery and kidnapping case, which involved a confrontation with sports memorabilia dealers, led to a nine-to-33-year sentence. His lawyers argued that the sentence was a form of payback for his acquittal in the murders, and that he had been misled by his associates during the robbery.

**The
details.ground_truth: OJ Simpson's big moment has arrived. A hearing is underway at which he is trying to convince members of the Nevada Board of Parole that he should go free. "I haven't made any excuses in the nine years that I've been here and I'm not trying to make an excuse now," said the 70-year-old Simpson early in the proceedings, per the AP. Later, he added, "I am sorry that things turned out the way they did. I had no intent to commit a crime." He also appeared to stifle a sob in his appeal to the board. Simpson has been imprisoned since 2008 over an armed robbery and kidnapping case, but he and many legal analysts think that the board will vote to release him. If so, he's expected to be out on Oct. 1. The televised hearing is being livestreamed at various sites, including CNN. Per CNN, one of the first questions posed to Simpson by a board member in regard to the robbery, in which he and other men entered a Vegas hotel room to reclaim sports memorabilia, was, "What were you thinking?" Simpson called it a "big mistake" and emphasized that he wasn't armed. "I would never pull a gun on anybody." He insisted that he learned only afterward that one of the men with him, who was "behind me," brandished a gun. Another board member noted that he hadn't taken an alcohol-abuse program as he promised he would at his last hearing. Other Simpson quotes: "I'm at a point in my life where all I want to do is spend as much time with my children." "I've basically spent a conflict-free life." Daughter Arnelle Simpson said her father is "remorseful," and she wants him to come home so they can "move forward," adding, "It's been hard."

President Donald Trump held a 77-minute press conference on February 16, 2017, in the East Room of the White House. The conference was called to address a week of negative news and to defend his administration's accomplishments. Trump took on the role of media critic, excoriated CNN for its poor ratings, and mocked the BBC as "that's another beauty." He also responded to a question about anti-Semitism, saying to his questioner, "OK, sit down, I understand the rest of your question."

Trump made several claims during the conference that were later fact-checked by the Associated Press. He said he inherited a mess from his predecessor, Barack Obama, but the facts show that Obama inherited a far worse situation when he became president in 2009. Trump also claimed that ISIS has spread like cancer, but the group has actually been losing ground before he took office. He said his administration is running like a fine-tuned machine, but his first month has been consumed by a series of missteps and firestorms.

Trump also addressed stories alleging contacts between people associated with his campaign and Russian intelligence officials. He said, "The leaks are real, but the news is fake," and "I have nothing to do with Russia." He was repeatedly asked about those contacts, and he finally said, "Not that I know of."

In addition to addressing Russia and the media, Trump also talked about his administration's plans to replace the Affordable Care Act, his new executive order revising the travel ban, and his plans to address anti-Semitic incidents. He blamed those incidents on his opponents, saying, "They'll do signs, and they'll do drawings that are inappropriate. It won't be my people. It will be the people on the other side to anger people like you."

The conference was seen as an attempt by Trump to get his groove back and to show that the dysfunction in the White House is not real. However, it was also seen as a bizarre and fact-challenged affair, with Trump taunting reporters and waving away their attempts to fact-check him in real time.

Overall, the press conference was a defining moment for Trump's presidency, with him assigning blame elsewhere and ignoring the nation's healthy economy and relative peace when he took office. It was also a continuation of his theme that the media are the opposition party, and it may have been cheered by his supporters across the country who had packed arenas last year to jeer reporters and chant "tell the truth" at the press pen.

In related news, CNN
details.ground_truth: President Trump ripped into the "dishonest" and "out of control" media during his press conference Thursday—and it didn't take long for the media to fire back. Fox News anchor Shepard Smith was among those who criticized the president, saying he "keeps repeating ridiculous throwaway lines that are not true at all and sort of avoiding this issue of Russia as if we're some kind of fools for asking the question," the Hill reports. Smith said Trump "owes it to the American people" to answer legitimate questions liked the ones about Russia that he dismissed as "fake news." But not all the reviews were so negative. A look at coverage: Yes, he was "combative," observes Mara Liasson at NPR, "but he was also funny and charming like he was with the press during all those years in New York as a fixture in the tabloids. ... I think that he will get a lot of credit for doing this. I think it will thrill his supporters." The Washington Post fact-checks what it describes as 15 "dubious claims" from the conference, including Trump's claim that he had the "biggest electoral college win since Reagan." Trump had a total of 304 electoral college votes, which ranks sixth in the eight elections since Reagan's 525-vote 1984 landslide. Only George W. Bush won with fewer than Trump. "I was given that information," Trump said when a reporter challenged him on the statement. The AP takes a close look at Trump's "I inherited a mess line" and decides the claim itself is messy. CNN contributor Jeffrey Lord, reacting to scathing reviews: “Lord, I think we saw two different press conferences," he said, per Raw Story. "From my perspective, I thought he was relaxed, he was funny, he was on point. He took the whole issue of the media, and he had a very candid conversation." Politico has an exhaustive list of the topics Trump covered during the 77-minute presser, ranging from nuclear holocaust to CNN, which he downgraded from "fake news" to "very fake news." BuzzFeed has video of one of the conference's most heavily commented upon moments: when Trump asked a black reporter if she could set up a meeting between him and the Congressional Black Caucus. Presidential historians tell the AP that no president, including Nixon during the Watergate scandal, has publicly turned on the press the way Trump did. "It was bizarre theater," says Rice University history professor Douglas Brinkley. "He turned a presidential press conference into a reality-TV show in which he can be the star and browbeat anyone who objects to him with the power of his office." GOP strategist and CNN contributor Alice Stewart also found a few things to praise in Trump's performance. "You can't say he's not responsive to the press," she tells the Las Vegas Review-Journal. "He answered all the questions from all over the press corps and put to rest the long-standing dialogue that he shuts down certain news outlets." Vox lists nine moments that it suggests back up Jake Tapper's description of the press conference as "wild" and "unhinged," including Trump's claim that "the leaks are real" but "the news is fake." Late-night hosts approached the press conference as gleefully as a kid unwrapping Christmas presents, and the Washington Post rounds up some of their best lines. "My guess is he did it because he's mad and he just wanted to blow off some steam," Jimmy Kimmel said. "The tone of the press conference was like if your dad found a pack of cigarettes under your mattress." David Graham at the Atlantic looks at the continual theme of complaint in the conference and wonders: Does Trump hate his new job?

**International Space Station Faces Cooling System Malfunction, Possible Spacewalk Ahead**

The International Space Station (ISS) has encountered a critical issue with one of its external cooling loops, Loop-A, which shut down on Wednesday after reaching a pre-set temperature limit. The malfunction has forced NASA to reroute coolant into Loop-B and has resulted in the shutdown of some non-critical systems on the station, including the freezers that preserve scientific samples.

According to NASA spokesman Josh Byerly, the crew was never in any danger and is fine for the near future. However, engineers believe that a malfunctioning flow control valve for the station's ammonia coolant may have caused the problem. Mission managers are trying to determine whether a software fix can get the valve working again or whether a spacewalk will be required.

The ISS has experienced similar issues in the past, with NASA astronauts mounting a spacewalk to replace a leaky coolant pump control box in May. The crew of the ISS consists of six people, including two Americans, three Russians, and a Japanese astronaut, who are living on the station for a tour of duty lasting four to six months.

The current mission of the ISS, officially called Expedition 38, is scheduled to go until March 2014. The station has been continuously occupied since 2000 and is expected to be maintained through 2020, although the United States and the 14 other countries supporting the $100 billion-plus station project are already considering extending its operating life.

**Key Facts:**

* The ISS's external cooling loop, Loop-A, shut down on Wednesday after reaching a pre-set temperature limit.
* NASA has rerouted coolant into Loop-B and has shut down some non-critical systems on the station.
* Engineers believe that a malfunctioning flow control valve for the station's ammonia coolant may have caused the problem.
* Mission managers are trying to determine whether a software fix can get the valve working again or whether a spacewalk will be required.
* The crew of the ISS consists of six people, including two Americans, three Russians, and a Japanese astronaut.
* The current mission of the ISS, officially called Expedition 38, is scheduled to go until March 2014.
details.ground_truth: NASA is reporting a glitch with one of the two cooling pumps on the International Space Station, but it says none of the six crew members are in danger, reports CNN. The crew shut down some non-essential electrical systems as a precaution while they figure what went wrong and how to fix it. Best case, it's a software glitch and a relatively easy repair. Otherwise, a spacewalk will be necessary, reports NBC News. It will likely take days or perhaps weeks to sort it all out. "They're fine for the near future," says a NASA spokesman.

**A Decade After Hurricane Katrina: Remembering the Warning that Changed History**

Ten years have passed since the devastating landfall of Hurricane Katrina in Louisiana, but the memory of the storm's destruction and the warning that preceded it remain etched in the minds of those who lived through it. National Weather Service meteorologist Robert Ricks, who was the forecast coordinator for the Slidell, Louisiana, office at the time, played a crucial role in issuing a warning that would go down in history as one of the most dire and effective ever issued by the National Weather Service.

Ricks' warning, which was titled "URGENT... SPECIAL MESSAGE," predicted that "MOST OF THE AREA WILL BE UNINHABITABLE FOR WEEKS. PERHAPS LONGER. AT LEAST ONE HALF OF WELL-CONSTRUCTED HOMES WILL HAVE ROOF AND WALL FAILURE. ALL GABLED ROOFS WILL FAIL. THE VAST MAJORITY OF NATIVE TREES WILL BE SNAPPED OR UPROOTED. OTHERS WILL BE TOTALLY DEFOLIATED." The warning also predicted that "WATER SHORTAGES WILL MAKE HUMAN SUFFERING INCREDIBLE BY MODERN STANDARDS."

Ricks' warning was not just a prediction, but a personal plea to the people of the region to evacuate and seek safety. He had a deep understanding of the storm's potential, having grown up in the hard-hit Lower Ninth Ward section of New Orleans and having experienced the devastation of previous storms, such as Hurricane Betsy and Camille.

The warning was issued on August 27, a day before the storm made landfall, and it was broadcast on NBC News and other networks across the country. While Ricks would have preferred to be wrong and avoid the devastation that ensued, he knew that his local expertise and the data he had access to indicated that this was a storm like no other.

The impact of Ricks' warning cannot be overstated. It helped to save countless lives and prevent further suffering, and it marked a turning point in the way that the National Weather Service issues warnings during severe weather events. In recent years, the agency has encouraged the use of bold and direct language in its warnings, and Ricks' warning is often cited as an example of the power of clear and concise communication in the face of disaster.

Today, Ricks is back at work alongside his colleagues, who are still recovering from the loss of their homes and belongings. While the memory of Hurricane Katrina remains a painful one,
details.ground_truth: It's one of the most famous weather forecasts of the modern era, and it came out exactly 13 years ago to the day. That would be Aug. 28, 2005, one day before Hurricane Katrina devastated New Orleans. National Weather Service meteorologist Robert Ricks issued a forecast so dire that some news agencies thought it was a hoax, writes meteorologist James Spann in a tweet commemorating the Ricks forecast. As it turns out, Ricks was spot on. "Most of the area will be uninhabitable for weeks…perhaps longer," he wrote. "At least one half of well constructed homes will have roof and wall failure. All gabled roofs will fail…leaving those homes severely damaged or destroyed." And this: "Water shortages will make human suffering incredible by modern standards." (Spann's tweet includes the full text.) The forecast has been credited with saving lives, and it is now in the Smithsonian Museum of American History, as noted by Slate on the 10-year anniversary. Ricks told Brian Williams of NBC News on Sept. 15, 2005, that he went through the warning line by line to verify each doomsday-sounding statement before making it public. He ended up removing nothing. "I would much rather have been wrong in this one," he told Williams. "I would much rather be talking to you and taking the heat and crying wolf. But our local expertise said otherwise. You know, 'Hey, let's gear up for the big one, this is going to be the big one.'" (Read about what JJ Watt's crowdsourced fundraiser accomplished in the wake of Hurricane Harvey.)

**Twitter's Struggle with Harassment and Abuse: A Call for Change**

Twitter, a social media platform with over 300 million active users, has been facing a growing concern over harassment and abuse on its platform. The recent incident involving Robin Williams' daughter, Zelda Williams, being bullied off the social network, has brought attention to the issue and prompted Twitter to promise to improve and expand its policies.

According to reports, Zelda Williams was subjected to a barrage of abusive messages, including pictures of her father with bruises around his neck, after his death. Twitter's vice president of trust and safety, Del Harvey, stated that the company will not tolerate abuse of this nature and has suspended a number of accounts related to the issue. However, many users, including senior legal analyst Imani Gandy, have expressed that Twitter's tools do not address the problem effectively, as persistent attackers can create new accounts to continue their actions.

Gandy, who faces abuse on a daily basis due to her race, gender, and medical condition, has collected instances of harassment similar to hers, including a tweet threatening to rape a woman that was deemed not to violate Twitter's community rules. She recommends that Twitter take a leaf from a third-party software called "Block Together," which lets users automatically block the accounts of new Twitter users who send them direct "@" replies.

Media critic and feminist activist Soraya Chemaly has expressed that Twitter's commitment to free expression may be clouding its judgment on this issue, suggesting that the company does not fundamentally care about the effects of harassment on certain members of its customer base. She adds that if Twitter does not take steps to protect the character of its community, everyone loses.

In a separate development, Alexa Internet, a company that provides web information services, has been donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine since 1996. This data is added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period, providing a valuable resource for researchers and the public.

**Key Takeaways:**

* Twitter has promised to improve and expand its policies after the incident involving Zelda Williams.
* Many users, including Imani Gandy, have expressed that Twitter's tools do not address the problem of harassment and abuse effectively.
* Gandy recommends that Twitter take a leaf from the third-party software "Block Together."
* Soraya Chemaly has expressed that Twitter's commitment to free expression may be clouding its judgment on this issue.
* Alexa Internet has been donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine since 1996.
details.ground_truth: Twitter has promised to take a look at its policies after some sickening abuse over her father's death forced Zelda Williams off the site. After she tweeted a touching tribute to her father, the grieving daughter received messages on Twitter and Instagram blaming her for her father's death, as well as images of her father Photoshopped to show bruises around his neck, Forbes reports. "We will not tolerate abuse of this nature on Twitter," a Twitter exec said in a statement. "We have suspended a number of accounts related to this issue for violating our rules and we are in the process of evaluating how we can further improve our policies to better handle tragic situations like this one." Twitter says the changes will include "expanding our policies regarding self-harm and private information, and improving support for family members of deceased users." An activist who has worked with Facebook to help protect people facing online abuse says the site needs to do more to tackle persistent abusers. "While I am truly sorry for what the Williams family is experiencing during this time, I am concerned that it takes an event like this to bring heightened attention to a problem that so many face every day," she tells the Washington Post. "As is often the case, it is Williams, the target of abuse, and not her abusers, who's left Twitter," she adds.

**Australian Radio Hosts Apologize for Prank Call that Led to Nurse's Death**

In a shocking and tragic turn of events, an Australian radio station's prank call to a London hospital where the Duchess of Cambridge was being treated led to the death of a nurse, Jacintha Saldanha. The nurse, who was 46 years old and a mother of two, was found dead at her home in London on Friday, and the police are treating her death as "unexplained."

The prank call, which was made by two Australian radio hosts, Mel Greig and Michael Christian, who were impersonating the Queen and the Prince of Wales, was broadcast on the radio station 2Day FM. The hosts had called the hospital's switchboard and were put through to Saldanha, who was on duty at the time. They asked for information about the Duchess's condition, and Saldanha provided it to them.

The hosts, who had been in hiding and undergoing counseling since the prank call was broadcast, have now spoken out about their distress and apologized for their actions. Greig said that she had been thinking about Saldanha's family nonstop since the news of her death broke, and Christian said that they were "shattered, gutted, heartbroken" by the news.

The radio station, Southern Cross Austereo, has suspended all prank calls and pulled advertising from the station. The company's CEO, Rhys Holleran, said that the station had attempted to contact the hospital five times before broadcasting the prank call, but the hospital had not responded.

The incident has sparked widespread outrage and condemnation, with many people calling for the hosts to be charged with manslaughter. The hospital where the Duchess was being treated has also slammed the radio station's decision to broadcast the prank call as "truly appalling."

The Duchess and her husband, the Duke of Cambridge, have expressed their sadness and condolences to Saldanha's family, and the palace has offered its full and heartfelt support to the nurses involved and hospital staff.

* The nurse, Jacintha Saldanha, was found dead at her home in London on Friday, and the police are treating her death as "unexplained."
* The prank call was made by two Australian radio hosts, Mel Greig and Michael Christian, who were impersonating the Queen and the Prince of Wales.
* The hosts have spoken out about their distress and apologized for their actions.
* The radio station, Southern Cross Austereo
details.ground_truth: Australian DJs Michael Christian and Mel Greig have broken their silence on their royal prank call gone horribly wrong, reports the Guardian, tearfully telling an interviewer that they're "shattered, gutted, heartbroken" over the death of nurse Jacintha Saldanha. "No one could've imagined this to happen," Christian said. "Naturally, we're shattered. We're people, too." "There's not a minute that goes by that I don't think about what (Saldanha's) family is going through, and the thought that we may have contributed to it is gut-wrenching," says Greig. The DJs meant no harm, adds Christian: "At every single point it was innocent on our behalf." Innocent or not, Greig and Christian "will not return to the airwaves until further notice," says parent company Southern Cross Austereo, which has also axed their show, halted all prank calls, and nixed ads amid an internal review, CNN reports. But the station is doubling down on its decision to air the prank, saying that it had tried to contact King Edward VII's Hospital no fewer than five times to talk about the recording before airing it. "It is absolutely true to say that we actually did attempt to contact those people on multiple occasions," says Southern Cross' CEO. It's not clear if the hospital responded, notes the AFP, but it has mounted a withering assault against the station in the aftermath of Saldanha's death.

**Tragic Accident at Fleming Athletic Complex Leaves 12-Year-Old Boy Dead and Two Others Injured**

A devastating incident occurred at the Fleming Athletic Complex in Augusta, Georgia, on Monday, resulting in the death of a 12-year-old boy and injuring two others who attempted to help him. Melquan Kwame Robinson, a seventh-grader at Morgan Road Middle School, was electrocuted when he touched a fence that had been electrified by a live wire. The incident report states that Melquan climbed over the fence to retrieve a football and was electrocuted when he attempted to come back over.

**Investigations and Condolences**

The Richmond County Coroner's Office, the Richmond County Sheriff's Office, and the city of Augusta are conducting separate investigations into the incident. Georgia Power released a statement stating that they do not control or maintain the voltage that energized the fences at the complex. Mayor Hardie Davis offered his condolences and prayers to the family of Melquan, stating that the city and community should come together to support them.

**Community Reaction and Support**

The community has been left reeling by the tragic loss of a young life. Melquan's former football coach, Don Clark, described him as a future leader, athlete, and amazing child. Clark praised the courage of the two boys who attempted to help Melquan, stating that they are an inspiration to everyone. The Trinity Elite Titans football team, of which Melquan was a member, issued a statement on Facebook expressing their sadness and sending their prayers to the family.

**Vigil and Support for the Family**

A vigil will be held at the Bernie Ward Community Center on Thursday at 6:00 PM to honor Melquan's memory and support his family. The community is encouraged to attend and show their support. The Richmond County school system has provided additional support to Morgan Road Middle School for students and staff.

**Update on the Injured**

One of the boys who attempted to help Melquan, David Sette, is recovering in the hospital and has been moved from the ICU to a regular room. His mother stated that doctors have concerns about his blood levels involving muscle breakdown, which can lead to future problems. The condition of the other boy who attempted to help Melquan is not available.

This tragic incident serves as a reminder of the importance of safety and vigilance in our communities. Our thoughts and prayers are with the family of Melquan Kwame Robinson and the two boys who attempted to help him.
details.ground_truth: A 12-year-old was killed and two other boys were injured after a chain link fence electrified by a live underground wire electrocuted them at a city park in Georgia. During football practice at Fleming park in Augusta on Monday, Melquan Robinson hopped a fence to retrieve a ball, WRDW reports, citing the incident report that says when he "went to come back over the fence, he was electrocuted." The seventh-grader was pronounced dead later at Children's Hospital of Georgia, according to the Augusta Chronicle. Two other boys who tried to help their friend were injured and hospitalized, according to reports. The mother of one of them, David Sette, tells WRDW that her son is improving. Per the Chronicle, an adult also was injured in the incident. "We lost a future leader. We lost a future athlete. We lost a child in our city," Melquan's former coach, acting as a spokesman for the bereaved family, tells the Chronicle. The oldest of three children, Melquan loved his family and was very protective of his siblings, he says. Multiple investigations into the incident are reportedly underway, with city officials looking into how the fence became electrified. Georgia Power, offering "condolences and sympathy," said in a statement that the utility "does not control or maintain the voltage" that caused the electrocution. Melquan's family will hold a vigil Thursday at the Bernie Ward Community Center. "This young man was where he needed to be," his former coach says, per WRDW. "He was doing what he loved." (A medieval re-enactor was impaled in a freak accident.)

**Target Impersonation Theft: Fairfax County Police Seek Suspect**

In a recent string of incidents, a woman has been accused of impersonating a Target employee in Fairfax County, Virginia, and stealing over $40,000 worth of iPhones. The suspect, whose identity remains unknown, allegedly gained access to the stockroom at the 6600 Richmond Highway location in Alexandria, Virginia, on March 15.

According to police, the woman was familiar with store procedures, employee hours, and the location of the iPhones in the stockroom. She placed the stolen devices in a box and left the store, getting into a Volvo station wagon. Surveillance footage of the suspect leaving the store has been released by the police.

Detectives in Virginia are also investigating a similar incident, where a woman disguised herself as a Target employee and stole over $40,000 worth of iPhones. The woman, who is not affiliated with the store, appeared to know the store's procedures and the location of the iPhones in the stockroom.

The Fairfax County police are urging anyone with more information about the case to come forward and contact them at 703-691-2131. The investigation is ongoing, and the suspect remains at large.

**Key Details:**

- Location: 6600 Richmond Highway, Alexandria, Virginia
- Date: March 15
- Stolen items: Over $40,000 worth of iPhones
- Suspect: Unknown, female
- Vehicle: Volvo station wagon
- Surveillance footage: Released by the police
details.ground_truth: She wasn't a Target employee, but a woman who sure seemed like one allegedly made off with around $40,000 worth of iPhones from a Virginia store. NBC Washington reports Fairfax County cops are looking for the retail impostor, who they say donned attire resembling a worker's getup, waltzed into the stockroom of the Alexandria location with a box, and loaded the box with dozens of iPhones before taking off. WTOP reports the woman, whose image was caught on tape, seemed to be familiar with how things worked at the store, including employee hours and where the iPhones were stored. Police say the theft occurred March 15, but posted about it on Facebook Monday with a call to "help us nab an iPhone thief." (Target recently had a Boston problem.)

details.ground_truth: Apparently there are places even South Park fears to tread. After getting a death threat from the jihadist website RevolutionMuslim.com, the show pulled the prophet Mohammed out of this week's episode, bleeping out all mention of his name, putting “censored” banners over parts of the episode and, where necessary, replacing him with Santa Claus in a bear suit, the Huffington Post reports.
