**North Korea's Nuclear Escalation: A Global Concern**

In recent days, North Korea has continued to escalate its nuclear program, sparking a global response and raising concerns about the stability of the region. The latest development comes after the country's sixth nuclear test, which was conducted on Sunday, resulting in the most powerful explosion ever detonated by the rogue nation.

According to a South Korean lawmaker, North Korea is believed to be moving an intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM), which has been detected by the country's intelligence service. The direction of the projectile and how it is being transported remain unclear, but the movement has been seen as a potential indication of an upcoming ICBM test.

The United Nations has condemned the nuclear test, with Nikki Haley, the American ambassador to the UN, calling for the strongest sanctions measures possible to stop Pyongyang's nuclear program. The US has also announced that it will circulate a resolution in response to the test.

In a show of force, the South Korean Navy conducted live-fire drills off the east coast of the Korean Peninsula, with a statement from the Navy warning that it will "immediately destroy and bury" any North Korean provocation at sea.

Despite the global response, signs of a rift between the US and South Korea have emerged, with President Donald Trump criticizing South Korea for its "appeasement" policy towards North Korea. The two leaders spoke by phone on Monday, agreeing to lift current restrictions on the payload weight of South Korea's ballistic missiles.

The situation has also sparked a diplomatic response, with leaders from around the world calling for an end to the nuclear program. Russian President Vladimir Putin reiterated calls for North Korea to stop nuclear tests, but said dialogue needs to be the answer. Chinese President Xi Jinping also called for global peace and cooperation, but stopped short of condemning the North Korean nuclear test.

In the lead-up to the nuclear test, there were concerns that North Korea could launch an ICBM on September 9, the country's founding day. While that has not yet happened, the situation remains fluid and unpredictable, with the potential for further escalation and conflict in the region.

details.ground_truth: Following North Korea's most powerful weapons test yet on Sunday, a South Korean lawmaker tells CNN his country's intelligence service believes the North has an intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM) on the move. Things get pretty vague beyond that: He didn't know the method of transport or when the movement was noticed. But Reuters does pick up some alleged details on those fronts, via an unnamed source quoted by South Korea’s Asia Business Daily. Its source says the ICBM started moving Monday, is only being moved at night, and is headed toward the nation's west coast. Reuters notes there's some likelihood that another test could come on or around Saturday: Sept. 9 is the country's founding day holiday. CNBC notes North Korea conducted a nuke test on Sept. 9, 2016. Two more details that allegedly came from the South's National Intelligence Service briefing: word that additional tunnels have been dug out at the North's nuclear test site that would facilitate future detonations, and suspicions that the next ICBM test firing might be directed toward the North Pacific but aimed at a lower angle, which would in theory allow it to fly further than previous tests, which had more of an upward trajectory. Meanwhile, the White House tells Reuters it has given "in-principle approval" to a request by South Korean President Moon Jae-in to abolish a limit regarding warhead weights, a move that would strengthen the power with which it could attack the North, if necessary. Meanwhile, Vladimir Putin on Tuesday gave his take on the situation: North Korea "will eat grass but will not give up the (nuclear) program if they don't feel safe."

**Tragic Accident: 6-Year-Old Boy Killed in Deputy-Involved Shooting**

A devastating incident occurred in Schertz, Texas, when a 6-year-old boy, Kameron Prescott, was fatally shot in a deputy-involved shooting. The Bexar County Sheriff, Javier Salazar, described the incident as a "tragic accident."

According to the investigation, the incident began when a deputy responded to a report of a stolen vehicle and spotted a suspect, Amanda Lene Jones, 30, who he believed was armed with a weapon. Jones physically threatened the deputy and verbally stated her intention to shoot him. She managed to get away, and deputies did not see her again until a couple of hours later, when she turned up at the mobile home park where Kameron lived.

Jones forced her way into Kameron's home, threatened to shoot members of his family, and said she was taking their vehicle. Witnesses heard the woman threaten to shoot the law enforcement officers, and deputies were outside, waiting. When Jones left the home, the deputies believed she was holding an object in her hand that they thought was a weapon.

The deputies who opened fire, identified as John Aguillon, George Herrera, Jesse Arias, and Johnny Longoria, a reservist, have been placed on administrative leave. The local district attorney is investigating the incident.

Kameron was a kind and energetic first-grader who loved to make everyone laugh and had a great sense of humor and a caring heart. His teacher, Shanda Ince, described him as the "kindest-hearted little boy that I have ever had the pleasure of teaching." His school counselor, Maria Morales, said he was "a kind soul" and a "ball of energy, happy, smart and could strike up a conversation with anyone."

The school district and the community are mourning the loss of Kameron, and a fund has been established to assist his family with expenses. Monetary donations can be made at the two Schertz Bank & Trust branches in Schertz or by mail to the Kameron Prescott Memorial Fund.

The incident has left the community and the law enforcement officers shaken, and it is a reminder of the complexities and challenges of policing and the importance of de-escalation techniques and community trust-building.
details.ground_truth: Sheriff's deputies shot and killed a 6-year-old boy Thursday in Texas, WOAI reports. Bexar County Sheriff Javier Salazar says four deputies opened fire on 30-year-old Amanda Jones, a suspected car thief, after they "cornered" her outside a mobile home occupied by Kameron Prescott and his family in the town of Schertz. One of the bullets went through the mobile home and hit Kameron in the abdomen. According to CNN, Salazar says deputies performed first aid on the boy, but he was pronounced dead at the hospital. Jones was also killed. "The deputies are, of course, understandably shaken up," Salazar says. He says the shooting is under investigation, but in his opinion the boy's death is "a tragic accident." Salazar says one deputy is "adamant" he saw Jones with a gun and that she threatened to shoot him. He says Jones threatened both police and Kameron's family, who didn't know her, just before deputies opened fire. But investigators haven't been able to locate a gun despite the use of a helicopter and dive team. Salazar says they have found a pipe with Jones' blood on it near the scene of the shooting. One deputy at the scene was wearing a body camera, but he blocked it with his rifle during the shooting. Meanwhile, Kameron is being remembered by those who knew him. "Kameron was a ball of energy, happy, smart, and could strike up a conversation with anyone,” school counselor Maria Morales tells KSAT. “He also had a great sense of humor and caring heart."

**Vegan Mother's Custody Battle: A Timeline of Events**

In June 2023, a Seminole County, Florida, mother, Sarah Markham, was arrested and charged with child neglect after she refused to take her newborn son, Caleb, to the hospital due to her strict vegan diet. The baby had lost 10% of his body weight and was diagnosed by a pediatrician as dehydrated. Markham wanted to try supplementing breast milk with vegan formula instead of following the doctor's orders.

The case drew national attention, with many criticizing Markham's decision to prioritize her dietary beliefs over her child's health. However, Markham's family and supporters argued that she was simply exercising her rights as a mother and challenging the medical establishment.

In a recent development, a Seminole County judge has ruled in Markham's favor, granting her custody of her son Caleb with the provision that she provide a nutritionist's report within 10 days. The judge threw out Seminole County Child Protective Services' claim that Markham was not fit to care for the child.

Markham's attorney, Mark O'Mara, expects the criminal case against his client to be thrown out, citing a lack of evidence and a misunderstanding of the law. Caleb, who was removed from his mother's care and stayed with his grandparents, now weighs 17 pounds and is thriving on a soy formula.

The case highlights the complexities of the relationship between parents, medical professionals, and the law, and raises important questions about the role of dietary choice in the care of children. While some may view Markham's actions as reckless and irresponsible, others see her as a devoted and determined mother who is willing to challenge the status quo to ensure the best for her child.

* Mark O'Mara: Markham's attorney who expects the criminal case against his client to be thrown out.
* Bo Markham: Caleb's grandfather and Sarah Markham's father, who has been a vocal supporter of his daughter.

* June 2023: Sarah Markham is arrested and charged with child neglect after refusing to take her newborn son to the hospital.
* August 2023: Markham's case goes to trial, with the judge throwing out Semin
details.ground_truth: A judge in Florida has allowed a vegan mom who defied a doctor's orders to regain custody of her infant son, reports WFTV. The strange case began in June when police in Casselberry charged Sarah Markham with neglect and took away 2-week-old Caleb. The charges came after a pediatrician diagnosed Caleb as dehydrated and underweight, and told Markham to take him to the hospital immediately, recounts WKMG. Instead, Markham bought soy formula to supplement her breast milk. When she didn't show up at the hospital, police came to her house, and she told them she wanted a second doctor's opinion and stressed that she did not want her son getting formula from animal products. Today, a Seminole County judge granted her custody again with the provision that she provide a nutritionist's report in 10 days. "There's no case, there's no abuse, there's no neglect—there's simply a doctor who has been challenged by a mother, and he didn't like it," says Caleb's grandfather, Bo Markham. The child has been with his grandparents since he was taken out of his mother's care, and now weighs about 17 pounds. "He's still on a soy formula," says Bo Markham. The criminal neglect charges are still pending, though Sarah Markham's attorney—Mark O'Mara, who defended George Zimmerman—says he expects they will be dismissed now. (PETA wants an Indiana sheriff to feed an alleged cannibal a vegan diet.)

**Rising Colon Cancer Rates Among Younger Adults: A Wake-Up Call for the Medical Community**

A recent analysis of data from the United States has revealed a disturbing trend: colon cancer rates are rising among men and women under the age of 50, the age at which guidelines recommend screenings start. According to the study, published in the journal Cancer, one in seven colon cancer patients is under 50, and these younger patients are more likely to have advanced stage cancer, but they live slightly longer without a cancer recurrence because they are treated aggressively.

The study, led by Dr. Samantha Hendren of the University of Michigan, analyzed data from nearly 260,000 patients diagnosed with colon cancer between 1998 and 2011. The researchers found that younger patients were more likely to be diagnosed with advanced cancer and more likely to have surgery than older patients (72% versus 63%). Radiation therapy was also used more often in younger patients than in older patients (53% versus 48%).

Despite the challenges of treating advanced cancer, younger patients tended to live longer without a cancer recurrence. For patients under 50, about 68% survived five years, while about 67% of the patients 50 and older survived five years.

The findings raise the question of whether screening for colon cancer should begin at an earlier age. Dr. Hendren noted that "the cancer community needs to prepare for the increasing number of very young colorectal cancer survivors who will need long-term support to cope with the physical and psychological consequences of their disease and treatments."

Experts agree that the reasons for the increase in colon cancer among younger adults are not yet known, but may be related to environmental factors such as smoking, obesity, and physical inactivity. Dr. Andrew Chan of Harvard Medical School noted that "it's not simply a change in diagnoses, it's a very real increase. There may be an effect of our environment that could be contributing to the increase."

The study's findings are a wake-up call for the medical community to be more vigilant in detecting the warning signs of colon cancer, such as anemia, bowel bleeding, or a blockage in the colon. Dr. Hendren emphasized that "doctors should be on the lookout for these warning signs, and people with a family history of colon cancer and others who are at higher risk should begin screening earlier than the age of 50."

In conclusion, the rising colon cancer rates among younger adults are a concerning trend that requires the attention of the medical community. By being more vigilant in detecting the warning signs
details.ground_truth: A new study says one in seven US colon cancer patients is younger than 50—raising questions about why more young people seem to be getting the disease and what can be done about it, reports HealthDay via the Philadelphia Inquirer. "Colon cancer has traditionally been thought of as a disease of the elderly," says lead author Samantha Hendren. "This study is really a wake-up call to the medical community that a relatively large number of colon cancers are occurring in people under 50." Using government data on almost 260,000 colon-cancer patients from 1998 to 2011, the study also says younger patients more often have advanced cancer and undergo surgery (72% compared to 63% of patients over 50). The young are also more likely to use radiation therapy (53% to 48%) and have a slight advantage in surviving for five years (68% to 67%). Younger people are more likely to have advanced colon cancer partly because they get tested after noticing symptoms (like colon blockage, bleeding, and anemia), while people over 50 are advised to begin screenings, says Hendren. "Unfortunately, these symptoms are often ignored by the patient or doctor or ascribed to something like hemorrhoids," a scientist adds, per Reuters. Hendren's team conducted the study after noticing a rise in colon cancer among young people, Medical Daily reports, but that spike remains unexplained. Physical inactivity, obesity, smoking, and "an effect in our environment" are all possible causes, a Harvard professor says. More screenings for young people might help, but the yield would be low because under-50s are still less likely to get colon cancer. "This would be a big and costly change," says Hendren, who recommends "a lot of research" be done first. (A new killer is expected to top cancer by 2050.)

**Ancient Human Settlements in Australia Uncovered**

A groundbreaking discovery in the Flinders Ranges of southern Australia has revealed a human settlement dating back 49,000 years, making it one of the most important prehistoric sites in the country. The site, known as Warratyi, was discovered by chance when a colleague of archaeologist Giles Hamm needed to use the restroom and stumbled upon a rock shelter with a blackened roof, indicating signs of human activity.

**Excavation and Dating Methods**

The excavation of the site was carried out using a 1-m grid system, with each grid further subdivided into 25 cm square units. The team used a range of dating methods, including optical dating of quartz grains, radiocarbon dating of hearth charcoal and eggshell samples, and Bayesian age modeling to integrate all stratigraphically reliable chronological information. The results suggest that humans occupied the site from 49,000 to 46,000 years ago.

**Significance of the Discovery**

The discovery of Warratyi pushes back the earliest-known dates on the development of key bone and stone axe technologies and the use of ochre in Australia. The site also provides reliably dated evidence of hafted axe technology about 38,000 years ago. The team's findings also suggest that humans lived alongside and hunted megafauna, including the Diprotodon optatum, a marsupial measuring 2m in height and weighing more than 2,500kg.

**Challenging Assumptions about Indigenous Australians**

The discovery of Warratyi challenges certain assumptions that Indigenous Australians were not as innovative as similar populations elsewhere in the world. The team's findings suggest that the development of fine stone tools, bone technology, and other technologies was a local innovation, due to a local cultural evolution. This challenges the Eurocentric view that material culture in Australia is quite simplistic and backward.

**Controversy and Debate**

The discovery of Warratyi is not without controversy, with some archaeologists questioning the dating methods used by the team. However, the team's methodology is considered to be as good as it gets, and the discovery is considered to be a significant find. The debate will likely rage among archaeologists for some time, but the most important thing is that we're finally seeing real evidence of just how widespread the Indigenous Australians were within just 1,000 years of hitting the coast.

**Conclusion**

The discovery of Warratyi is a significant milestone in the study of human history in Australia. It
details.ground_truth: "A man getting out of the car to go to the toilet led to the discovery of one of the most important sites in Australian pre-history," archaeologist Giles Hamm tells ABC News. Hamm was surveying a section of Australia's Flinders Ranges when his partner, aboriginal elder Clifford Coulthard, had to go to the bathroom. "Nature called and Cliff walked up this creek bed into this gorge and found this amazing spring surrounded by rock art," Hamm says. At the time, Hamm thought the newly discovered human settlement, a site called Warratyi, was only 5,000 years old. It turns out it's closer to 49,000 years old, and that has huge ramifications for our knowledge of ancient Australian civilization, Sky News reports. Hamm and his team published a study on Warratyi this week in Nature. The discovery of Warratyi in the arid interior of the continent means humans either arrived in Australia up to 10,000 years earlier than previously believed—or they expanded across the continent at a much quicker rate, Science Alert reports. Researchers at the site found 4,300 objects, including tools, and 200 bone fragments from multiple animals, including a 5,500-pound marsupial. The bones help explain how early Australians interacted with megafauna, including hunting them. The tools show Australians were using bone and stone axes earlier than believed and are evidence that early Australians developed some technologies on their own rather than adopting them from other peoples as previously believed. (Ninety rocks in Australia could rank up there with Stonehenge.)

The Gaza conflict has entered its 24th day, with both sides suffering heavy losses. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has vowed to destroy the Hamas tunnel network in the Gaza Strip "with or without a cease-fire," as the military called up another 16,000 reservists to pursue its campaign in the densely-populated territory.

**Death Toll Mounts**

The death toll in the Gaza conflict has surpassed 1,300 Palestinians, mostly civilians, and 50 Israelis, almost all of them soldiers. The Palestinian Health Ministry has reported that 1,364 people have been killed, including 315 children and 166 women. The number of wounded has risen to 6,785, with 2,307 children and 1,529 women.

**International Efforts to End the Conflict Stall**

International efforts to end the conflict have stalled, despite concern over the mounting death toll. The United States has agreed to an Israeli request for several types of ammunition, including 120mm mortar rounds and 40mm ammunition for grenade launchers, from a stockpile worth over $1 billion. Israel says it wants to decimate Hamas' rocket-launching capability, diminish its weapons arsenal, and demolish the tunnels. details.ground_truth: Benjamin Netanyahu today vowed to destroy Hamas' tunnels at all costs, even as Israel called up an additional 16,000 reservists—bringing the total so far to 86,000 troops. "We are determined to complete this mission with or without a ceasefire," he said of the tunnels' destruction, as per Haaretz. "Therefore I will not agree to any offer that does not allow the military to complete this important mission for the security of the people of Israel." He also issued a get-in-line message to two ministers who have been critical of his actions against Hamas, saying "beware of what you're doing." **Snap's Spectacles Now Available for Purchase Online**

Snap, the parent company of the popular social media platform Snapchat, has announced that its camera-enabled sunglasses, Spectacles, are now available for purchase online in the United States. The glasses, which allow users to take circular videos and post them to their Snapchat accounts, were previously only available through vending machines that popped up in major metro areas or a store in Manhattan.

The online purchase option is a significant shift for the company, which has been trying to prove to investors that Spectacles can generate revenue for the business. According to Snap's SEC filing, the company lost $514.6 million last year, and selling 4 million pairs of Spectacles would be enough to break even this year.

The online purchase option is also a response to the positive feedback the company has received since the launch of Spectacles in November. Users have been pleased with the glasses, which can record 10 to 30 seconds of video at a time, and the circular, view-with-your-phone-in-any-orientation video format Snap pioneered gives its Snapchat app something that its popular clone Instagram Stories can't copy.

Spectacles are available for $130 and come in three colors: teal, coral, and black. The company is currently only shipping in the United States, but users can also buy charging cases and charging cords from Snap, which will no longer be sold on Amazon.

The online purchase option is a significant step for the company, and it will be interesting to see how it affects the demand for Spectacles. The company has also announced that pop-up vending machines around the country will remain a part of its distribution strategy, though they'll be taking a short break.

**Key Details:**

* Spectacles are now available for purchase online in the United States
* The glasses were previously only available through vending machines or a store in Manhattan
* Online purchase option is a response to positive feedback and a way to prove to investors that Spectacles can generate revenue
* Spectacles are available for $130 and come in three colors: teal, coral, and black
* The company is currently only shipping in the United States, but users can also buy charging cases and charging cords from Snap
* Pop-up vending machines around the country will remain a part of its distribution strategy, though they'll be taking a short break
details.ground_truth: Snapchat found itself with a marketing sensation on its hands late last year when it began selling funky-looking sunglasses called Spectacles. The trick is they allow users to record quick videos and photos and send them directly to their Snapchat accounts, but the catch was they were available only through vending machines that would turn up in random locales or through a now-shuttered pop-up store in Manhattan, notes New York. Now, however, parent company Snap is selling them online at Spectacles.com for $129. Just how crazy did demand get? CNBC notes that the glasses were selling for up to $5,000 on secondary markets, adding that its own reporters stood in line for 18 hours to get them. (There's a limit of six per household for the new online purchases.) Snap has said the glasses haven't been big money-makers, but TechCrunch thinks the new move is designed to show prospective IPO investors that they could be. The vending machines will go on hiatus for awhile, but they're expected to resurface eventually.

**Seven Men Charged in 2014 Hazing Death, Fraternity Sues for Unauthorized Use of Name**

In a recent development, seven men have been charged in connection with the 2014 hazing death of University at Albany student Trevor Duffy. The men are accused of forcing Duffy to drink a 60-ounce bottle of vodka, leading to his death.

The fraternity, Zeta Beta Tau (ZBT), has released a statement denouncing the men and claiming that they were not part of the sanctioned fraternity chapter at the University of Albany. The statement states that the group using the ZBT name is a "rogue group" not authorized to use the name and that ZBT is retaining counsel to file a lawsuit against the individuals for falsely representing themselves and damaging the fraternity's reputation.

ZBT has indeed followed through on this threat, filing a lawsuit against the group of University of Albany students connected with the 2014 hazing death. The fraternity claims that the group was never recognized as a chapter of ZBT and that they falsely represented themselves as members of the Epsilon Gamma chapter, which is recognized by the national fraternity and the university.

The lawsuit also highlights that the name Zeta Beta Tau Fraternity, ZBT, its symbols, slogans, and materials are protected by United States Copyright and Trademark law, and that anyone found to be engaged in unauthorized use of ZBT's intellectual property may be subject to legal action.

This development has sparked a reaction from the defense team of Joseph Angilletta, one of the students accused in connection with Duffy's death. Attorney Paul DerOhannesian stated that the lawsuit is "novel" and "different" and that he is not sure why ZBT would take this step.

The lawsuit and the charges against the seven men bring to light the ongoing issue of hazing and the unauthorized use of fraternity names, highlighting the need for accountability and transparency in these organizations.
details.ground_truth: The seven men accused of the 2014 hazing of a SUNY Albany student that caused his death are already in trouble with the school and the police. Now they've got a new problem on their hands: a lawsuit set to be filed by the fraternity whose name they apparently appropriated. Zeta Beta Tau's national office says the "rogue" group of SUNY Albany students charged in the death of 19-year-old Trevor Duffy used the frat's name without authorization and isn't linked to the actual recognized chapter on campus, NBC News reports. "Zeta Beta Tau is retaining counsel for the purposes of filing suit against these individuals who falsely represented themselves, deceiving the public and damaging the otherwise exceptional reputation of our Fraternity," a fraternity statement reads, per News10. Duffy died after allegedly being made to consume nearly half a gallon of vodka in a case that made headlines; his BAC was seven times the legal limit, notes NBC. The national fraternity says its intellectual property—including its name, the ZBT initials, and other materials—are protected under copyright rules, WRGB reports. That means anyone who falsely claims to be affiliated with the frat is fair game for legal action, a move the attorney for one of the accused says he's baffled by. "It's certainly novel, and I'm not sure why they'd take that step," the lawyer for Joseph Angilletta, one of two men arrested just last week in the case, tells the station. "Clearly one aspect here is they seem to want to distance themselves from this tragic event." (Five Baruch College frat members may face up to 20 years behind bars for allegedly taking part in the "glass ceiling.")

Kathryn Bigelow's latest film, "Detroit," is a powerful and unflinching reenactment of a 1967 police brutality incident in which three unarmed black men were killed by white police officers in the Algiers Motel. The film, which premiered in July 1967, is set against the backdrop of the Detroit riots, which left 43 people dead, 1,200 injured, and 7,200 arrested.

The film follows the story of several characters, including Larry Reed, a rising R&B singer, and Melvin Dismukes, a private security guard who tries to prevent the bloodshed. The film's central antagonist is Philip Krauss, a young and trigger-happy patrol officer who is played by Will Poulter.

The film's script, written by Mark Boal, is based on several witness accounts, including those of Larry Reed and Melvin Dismukes. However, some critics have accused the film of being too passive and not harsh enough in its condemnation of the police brutality.

The film's director, Kathryn Bigelow, has a history of making films that explore the complexities of identity and policing, including her 1990 film "Blue Steel" and her 2012 film "Zero Dark Thirty." Bigelow's style of filmmaking is often described as immersive and visceral, and she has a reputation for pushing the boundaries of what is acceptable in mainstream cinema.

The film has received widespread critical acclaim, with a 93% Tomatometer score on Rotten Tomatoes. However, some critics have accused the film of being too long and meandering, and of not providing enough context or background information on the events of the 1967 riots.

Overall, "Detroit" is a powerful and thought-provoking film that explores the complexities of police brutality and the ongoing legacy of the 1967 Detroit riots. The film is a must-see for anyone interested in American history, social justice, and the power of cinema to shape our understanding of the world.

The film is rated R for strong violence and pervasive language, and runs for 143 minutes. It is a powerful and unflinching look at a dark moment in American history, and is sure to leave audiences feeling disturbed and challenged.

In an interview with the Washington Post, Bigelow said that she doesn't think it's up to the filmmaker to judge, but rather to present the facts and let the audience draw their own conclusions. This approach is reflected in the film's portrayal of the police brutality, which
details.ground_truth: Kathryn Bigelow's Detroit tells the story of a black singer who takes refuge in a dingy motel to escape the 1967 Motor City riots. Based on real events, the film reunites Bigelow with screenwriter Mark Boal, both of the Oscar-winning film The Hurt Locker. It's got a strong 89% "fresh" rating at Rotten Tomatoes. Here's what critics are saying: "Bigelow drills down into one of American history's most egregious cases of abuse of police power, bringing it to life with visceral detail and slowed-down meticulousness" in what "feels like her timeliest movie yet," writes Ann Hornaday at the Washington Post. It's "daring, sophisticated and unforgettably disturbing." And "in scale, scope and the space it offers for a long-awaited moral reckoning, it's nothing less than monumental." "It's hard to overstate just how visceral and harrowing an experience it is," writes Lindsey Bahr at the AP, calling Detroit "a well-made and evocative film" jam-packed with "stomach-churning horror." She argues there could be more nuance and perspective. But "maybe anger is all you're supposed to feel when you step outside the theater. Maybe not feeling satisfied with Detroit is the point." Chris Klimek says Detroit is "maddeningly imperfect but still honorable," citing "lightly fictionalized" aspects of the otherwise true story that set it up "for maximum outrage." This is "messy work, even when it’s done in good faith," he writes at NPR. He applauds John Boyega, previously of The Force Awakens. Radiating a "Denzel-like calm," he shows "he's a bonafide movie star," Klimek writes. Peter Howell's main gripe is that the film "tries to tell us everything about the circumstances without telling us much about the people." But Detroit still makes for "urgent viewing," he writes at the Toronto Star. Why? "The injustice and anger behind it all feels like current reality, even a half-century on," when "a person's skin can still determine everything from employment opportunities to treatment by police."

**Tesla's Board Confirms Consideration of Elon Musk's Proposal to Take the Company Private**

In a shocking move, Tesla's CEO Elon Musk announced on Twitter that he had secured funding to take the electric-car maker private in a deal valued at over $70 billion. The proposal, which would be the largest buyout in history, has left investors and regulators scrambling to understand the details of the deal.

**Tesla's Board Response**

In a statement, several independent directors of Tesla's board confirmed that they had met several times over the past week to discuss Musk's proposal. The statement said that the talks included how being a private company could "better serve Tesla's long-term interests" and addressed the funding for the deal. However, the statement left many questions unanswered, including whether the board was satisfied with Musk's method and timing for announcing the approach.

**Regulatory Concerns**

The announcement of the possible delisting has raised questions about how Musk opted to disclose the information to investors. Andrew M. Calamari, a partner at the law firm Finn Dixon & Herling and former director of the New York office of the Securities and Exchange Commission, said that the announcement was "very irregular" and raised questions about Musk's intent. The Securities and Exchange Commission has inquired about the issue, according to the Wall Street Journal.

**Deal Structure and Funding**

The structure of the deal remains ambiguous, with some experts questioning how Musk can raise the necessary finance when Tesla has still not made a profit. Adam C. Pritchard, professor of securities law at the University of Michigan, said that if more than 2,000 investors opt to retain their shares, then the firm would be subject to the disclosure rules of a public company. Steven Kaplan, a University of Chicago professor who researches private equity, said that it would be difficult for Musk to raise the necessary finance when Tesla is cash-flow negative.

**Market Reaction**

Tesla shares surged close to their all-time high of $385 after Musk's tweets on Tuesday, but fluctuated on Wednesday after the board members issued their statement. The company's stock has climbed by 21% so far this year, despite being a consistent target of investors aiming to short the stock.

**Conclusion**

The proposal to take Tesla private has left many questions unanswered, including the deal structure, funding, and regulatory concerns. While Musk's intention is to give existing investors the option to retain their shares, the proposal has raised concerns about the company's ability to raise the necessary finance and the implications of the deal for the
details.ground_truth: Elon Musk wasn't blowing smoke. The BBC reports on a three-sentence statement issued by six of Tesla's nine board members saying the board had "met several times over the last week" to discuss Musk's proposal to take the company private, which he announced via an unexpected tweet Tuesday. The talks "included discussion as to how being private could better serve Tesla's long-term interests" and the statement ends by saying the board "is taking the appropriate next steps to evaluate this." The three board members who did not sign the statement are Musk, brother Kimbal Musk, and a board member who is on leave, reports the Wall Street Journal. At MarketWatch, Steve Goldstein flags some unresolved points: The statement didn't elaborate on Musk's claim of "funding secured" and raised but didn't answer a question of timing. "'Last week' predates Tesla's filing on Monday of its 10-Q with the Securities and Exchange Commission, which didn't mention such discussions. News of a takeover would certainly be considered material," he writes. Shares of Tesla are currently down about 1%.

**Tennis and Sports News Update**

In the world of tennis, several top players are facing injuries and setbacks that may impact their participation in upcoming tournaments. In a separate development, Alexa Internet has been donating its crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996. This partnership has helped to preserve a vast amount of internet content and make it available for research and historical purposes.

Overall, these news stories highlight the challenges and triumphs in the world of tennis and sports, as well as the importance of preserving internet content for future generations.

**The Search for Advanced Alien Civilizations: Harnessing Energy from the Universe**

A recent paper by Dan Hooper, a senior scientist at the Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory, has revived the Kardashev scale, a model proposed by Russian astrophysicist Nikolai Kardashev in 1962 to measure the energy consumption of technologically advanced civilizations. According to Hooper, harvesting energy from distant stars is not only the best way to increase a civilization's available resources but also the only way to prevent the ever-expanding universe from leaving it alone in the vastness of space.

The Kardashev scale consists of three phases: Level I, where a civilization harnesses all the resources of a planet; Level II, where it harnesses all the radiation of a star; and Level III, where it harnesses all the resources of a galaxy. Humans, currently at a 0.7 on the scale, might reach Level II in a few thousand years and Level III in a few hundred thousand to a million years.

To harness energy from stars, advanced civilizations would need to capture and drag them back to their home galaxy. This could be done using a swarm of millions of solar-powered satellites, known as Dyson spheres, which would permanently hover around a star, beaming energy back to a nearby planet. Alternatively, the star's energy could be used to accelerate the whole ball of fire back toward the planet.

Dyson spheres and other mega-structures have appeared frequently in science fiction, but they are also being explored as a real possibility by scientists. In his 1937 novel "Star Maker," science fiction author Olaf Stapledon described spherical, energy-trapping alien structures, while physicist Freeman Dyson proposed similar structures in a scientific paper in 1960.

Astronomers could detect the presence of advanced civilizations by looking for unusual galaxies that have had their prime stars ripped away from them. They could also watch for stars being dragged unceremoniously across distant galaxies, which would produce significant fluxes of electromagnetic radiation. However, humans likely don't have precise enough instruments yet to detect these unusual light signatures beaming from the depths of the universe.

The search for advanced alien civilizations is an ongoing and fascinating area of research, with scientists and science fiction authors alike exploring the possibilities of harnessing energy from the universe.
details.ground_truth: Watch out for stars moving across galaxies or losing power—it could be aliens gathering energy before the lights go out. Dan Hooper, an astronomy and astrophysics professor at the University of Chicago, posted a paper in the preprint journal arXiv.org arguing that aliens in far-off galaxies may be sucking energy from stars to offset risks posed by an expanding universe, LiveScience reports. "The presence of dark energy in our universe is causing space to expand at an accelerating rate," writes Hooper. "As a result, over the next approximately 100 billion years, all stars residing beyond the Local Group [of galaxies including the Milky Way] will fall beyond the cosmic horizon and become not only unobservable, but entirely inaccessible, thus limiting how much energy could one day be extracted from them." Therefore, he argues, an advanced civilization may use something like "Dyson spheres"—an imagined solar-powered satellite dating back to 1930s science-fiction, notes LiveScience—to harvest a star's energy or use that energy to move it closer to home. Hooper hinges his case on the work of Russian astronomer Nikolai Kardashev, who argued in 1962 that technologically advanced civilizations would expand via three steps: harvesting resources of their planet, then the nearest star, then all stars in the galaxy and nearby galaxies. So how to spot energy-grabbing aliens? Massive stars emanate certain light wavelengths, which will appear in galaxies' light signatures if stars are drained energy—that is, when humans have powerful enough instruments to detect it.

**Disneyland Dry Ice Bomb Incident: Employee Arrested and Investigation Ongoing**

A 22-year-old Disneyland employee, Christian Barnes, has been arrested and charged with possession of a destructive device after a dry ice bomb exploded in the Mickey's Toontown section of the theme park on Tuesday evening. The incident forced a brief evacuation of the area, but no one was injured in the blast.

According to Anaheim police, Barnes, who worked as an outdoor vendor selling sodas and water, placed dry ice in a plastic bottle and left it in a trash can, which caused the explosion. Detectives found fragments of a water bottle in the trash can and believe Barnes was responsible for the incident.

Barnes was held on $1 million bail and is cooperating with investigators, who have stated that the incident appears to be isolated and unanticipated. Disneyland officials have also released a statement, saying they are working closely with authorities to investigate the incident.

The explosion was confined to the trash can and did not cause any significant damage. However, it caused brief chaos in a popular area of the park, where young children often visit. Witnesses, including a West Covina resident who was at the park with a toddler, have expressed shock and concern about the incident, emphasizing the need for everyone to do their part to be safe.

The incident is not believed to be connected to similar dry-ice explosions in Anaheim in recent months. The investigation is ongoing, and Barnes' motive for the incident has not been determined.

**Related News**

* A Palmdale boy's torture death has triggered a Los Angeles County probe.
* Police say two witnesses dispute Justin Bieber's Ferrari story.
* Cops who took a car on a joyride to Vegas blame a "momentary" lapse.

**Sources**

* Anaheim police
* Disneyland officials
* Los Angeles Times
details.ground_truth: Police have arrested one of Disneyland's own employees after yesterday's dry-ice explosion in the Toontown section of the park, reports the Los Angeles Times. Christian Barnes, a 22-year-old vendor who sold soda and water from a cart, is being held on $1 million bond. Police say he filled a bottle with dry ice and dropped it in a trash can. The ensuing blast caused a lot of noise and chaos, but no injuries or serious damage, reports AP. No word on motive, but police don't think it's related to previous dry-ice bombs in the Anaheim area.

**Former Winnipeg Broadcaster and Journalism Instructor Charged with Bank Robberies**

Steve Vogelsang, a 53-year-old former Winnipeg broadcaster and journalism instructor, has been charged with two counts of robbery in connection with two bank robberies in Medicine Hat, Alberta. Vogelsang, who worked as a news director and sports anchor at CKY-TV (now CTV Winnipeg) from the early 1990s to 2002, and later taught journalism at Red River College from 2002 to 2011, was arrested on Saturday at a hotel in Medicine Hat.

**The Bank Robberies**

According to police, a man walked into a Royal Bank of Canada on October 19 and a Bank of Montreal on October 20, passed a note to the teller, and received an undisclosed amount of cash. There were no weapons seen or brandished by the accused, but an applied threat was made in the sensitive environment of a financial institution.

**Financial and Legal Difficulties**

Court documents obtained by CTV News reveal that Vogelsang is going through financial difficulties. He was accused of missing several mortgage payments on a home in Winnipeg and borrowing money. Vogelsang also faces three charges of disobeying a court order, laid in August 2017, and a protection order was filed and granted in March 2017. Additionally, he was charged with sexual assault in September 2016, but the charge was stayed in October 2017.

**Reaction from Former Students**

Former students of Vogelsang expressed shock and disappointment at the allegations. "I'm shocked and upset, in disbelief, as I know many of Steve's former students are," said Matt Preprost in an emailed statement. "Steve was a tough, but fair teacher—one who built his lessons and rapport with us through a mix of fun and humour, always challenging us to step outside our comfort zones and to be better than our last assignment."

**Vogelsang's Personal Life**

Vogelsang and his wife separated in 2015 after 24 years together, and he moved back to Winnipeg. She filed for divorce last summer, and court documents reveal that they sold three properties in Nelson, B.C. for a total loss of $85,000. Vogelsang also planned to move to Vancouver Island in August and was working on developing a career as a keynote speaker on millennials.

Vogelsang remains in custody in Alberta to face the two robbery charges and is expected to appear in court tomorrow.
details.ground_truth: A former well-known Canadian TV sports anchor is in jail after police say he robbed two banks in as many days in the western province of Alberta last week. Police in the small city of Medicine Hat say Steve Vogelsang robbed a Royal Bank of Canada on Thursday, Oct. 19, and then turned around the next day and robbed a Bank of Montreal, CBC reports. He entered both banks without a disguise and after demanding money, left with an undisclosed sum. Police finally arrested Vogelsang at a nearby hotel at around 3am on Saturday. Per CTV Winnipeg News, Vogelsang, 53, was a longtime news director and sports broadcaster at CKY, now CTV Winnipeg. He also taught journalism at Red River College in Winnipeg from 2002 to 2011. Conor Lloyd, a spokesman for the college, says, “I can confirm he is no longer an employee here.” Vogelsang is scheduled to appear in provincial court on Tuesday.

**Oakleigh Nance's Health Battle: A Summary of the News**

Eleven-month-old Oakleigh Nance, a premature and feisty baby, has been facing a series of health challenges since her birth. Born weighing just three pounds, Oakleigh spent a significant amount of time in the Neonatal Intensive Care Unit (NICU) before being discharged and seeming to thrive for six months. However, her health issues took a turn for the worse when she contracted a bacterial infection, which led to a series of complications, including a severe Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) caused by a dangerous, antibiotic-resistant strain of Pseudomonas.

The infection, which spiked to 104°F, was initially treated with antibiotics, but it returned a week later, prompting further testing and a diagnosis of stage 3 kidney reflux. Oakleigh's heart condition, moderate ASD and pulmonary stenosis, was also revealed during the testing, adding to the complexity of her case.

In an effort to alleviate future infections and improve Oakleigh's quality of life, she is scheduled to undergo a 4.5-hour surgery at the end of this month to fix the reflux in her left kidney, which now has only 22% functionality. The surgery will involve cutting back the ureters to a length that will prevent reflux and removing a pocket on the outside of the kidney.

After the surgery, Oakleigh will meet with a cardiologist to discuss additional testing for her heart condition. Her family, who is facing significant medical expenses, is trying to raise money through a GoFundMe account.

In a separate, but related, news story, an Oklahoma infant, also named Oakleigh, has contracted a "super strain" of E. coli, a bacterial infection that can be deadly and is resistant to many types of antibiotics. The infant's family is hoping that she can fight off the disease before an upcoming kidney surgery that could save her life.

Pseudomonas infections, like the one Oakleigh Nance contracted, are caused by a common type of bacteria that can be found in many environments, including hospitals, hot tubs, and swimming pools. They can be hard to treat because the bacteria can resist many types of antibiotics, and they can cause a range of symptoms, from a skin rash to pneumonia and septicemia.

In both cases, the families' faith and support system are playing a crucial role in their children's recovery and well-being.
details.ground_truth: An Oklahoma girl diagnosed with "super" E. coli is facing a life-or-death battle—and worse, she's only 11 months old. "With this strain of E. coli, it can get in her brain, it can get in her spinal cord, basically cause her to go into cardiac arrest," her grandfather, Chris Curtis, tells KFOR. "And we heard that it was a very, very scary situation." Her family says it's not clear how Oakleigh Nance got the infection, which appears resistant to antibiotics, but the CDC is investigating. It all started when Oakleigh was born premature at only 3 pounds and "started off on 4 liters of oxygen," according to a GoFundMe page to help pay her medical bills. "In the NICU, they used to call her Miss Sassy because of her ability to express her displeasure with her circumstances," the page says. Then she went home and was fine for months until a 104-degree temperature and severe urinary tract infection sent her back to the hospital. Since then, she's been diagnosed with moderate ASD and pulmonary stenosis of the heart, stage 3 kidney reflux, and "a dangerous, antibiotic resistant strain of Pseudomonas," says the GoFundMe page. Now doctors are planning a 4 1/2-hour procedure on her kidney, which is functioning at 22%, to possibly prevent other infections. "I know that God is in control of all else, and when I think about what’s going on in her life, I think about my faith in God," says Curtis. "That’s what sustains me and gets me through it." (Read about a brain-eating bacteria that has returned to Louisiana.)

A recent incident at Portland's Mt. Tabor Reservoir system has sparked a heated debate over the city's decision to dump 38 million gallons of drinking water after a man allegedly urinated in the reservoir. According to surveillance video, an 18-year-old man, later identified as Dallas Swonger, was seen urinating in the reservoir early Wednesday morning. He has expressed his frustration and disappointment with the city's decision to dump the water, citing the presence of dead animals and birds in the reservoir as a more significant health concern.

The Portland Water Bureau has insisted that Swonger urinated in the water, and has cited him for trespassing and public urination. The incident has left Swonger feeling rattled and disappointed, with his mother expressing her concern for his well-being. His skateboard buddy, Trey McDaniel, has defended him, stating that he is a "good guy at heart" who just makes poor decisions. The city's decision to dump the water has also raised questions about the effectiveness of its public health policies and the management of its water resources.

details.ground_truth: The Portland teen accused of peeing in a city reservoir on Wednesday insists he didn't do it—in a relatively NSFW defense. "Yeah, it's f---ing retarded, dude," 18-year-old Dallas Swonger tells Vocativ in what the site calls an "expletive-filled interview" given as Swonger smoked a Newport cigarette. "I didn't piss in the f---ing water." He explains that he did urinate, after a night of skateboarding at Mount Tabor Park, but he did so against the wall of the reservoir, not into the reservoir. "I was like, 'Dudes I have to piss so bad.' So I just went over to the wall. I leaned up against the wall and pissed on it. Right there on the wall, dude. I don’t know else how to describe it." Officials beg to differ (a Water Bureau official got very specific about it, saying that surveillance video showed Swonger making sure "to get his little wee wee right up to the iron bars"), and thus decided to dump 38 million gallons of water, but Swonger notes that even if his urine had made it into the water, he doesn't see what the big deal is. "Dude, I’ve seen dead birds in there. During the summer time I've see hella dead animals in there. Like dead squirrels and s---. I mean, really, dude?" Vocativ also got amusing quotes from Swonger's mom ("I’m sorry, he just graduated from high school. He’s trying to get his stuff together") and one of the friends skateboarding with him that night ("He just doesn’t make the best decisions. Honestly, he has the potential to do really good"). Possible criminal charges are still pending, the Oregonian notes. The paper also reports that the water tested clean of urine-related toxins, but all 38 million gallons will still be dumped.

**North and South Korea Unite for the Winter Olympics**

In a historic breakthrough, North and South Korea have agreed to march together under a unified flag at the Winter Olympics opening ceremony in Pyeongchang, South Korea. The two countries have also formed a joint women's ice hockey team for the Games, which begin early next month.

The unification ministry announced a range of joint activities between the countries for the Games, including joint training for skiers at a resort in North Korea and a cultural event at Mount Kumgang. North Korea will also send around 230 supporters to cheer on its athletes, and a smaller delegation of North Korean athletes and supporters will attend the Paralympics.

The Korean Unification Flag features a blue silhouette of the peninsula and outlying islands, and has been used in rare shows of unity before, including at the 1991 World Table Tennis Championships and the 2006 Winter Games in Turin, Italy.

The International Olympics Committee (IOC) will need to approve the countries' agreements, and those that affect competition, such as the joint hockey team, could be more complicated than the ceremonial proposals. The IOC has received a number of "interesting proposals" that it will discuss with delegates from both countries in Switzerland on Saturday.

**Diplomatic Thaw and Concerns**

The announcement is the latest breakthrough since talks began between the two Koreas earlier this month, after years of broken communications. While the two sides have earned praise for ratcheting down military tensions in recent weeks, some of Seoul's allies have voiced concern that Pyongyang may be using the talks to buy time to pursue its weapons program.

The US military has moved more firepower to the region, and Secretary of State Rex Tillerson warned of complacency at a Tuesday summit in Vancouver where the top diplomats from the United States, Japan, Canada, and the United Kingdom were in town to discuss North Korea. Japanese Foreign Minister Taro Kono called on the international community to be clear-eyed about North Korea's motivations for participating in the talks.

**North Korea's Charm Offensive**

China has denounced the Vancouver talks, and the United Nations Command, under whose framework they are being held, as demonstrating "Cold War mentality." North Korea has remained adamant throughout this month's thaw in relations with South Korea that its nuclear bombs and ballistic missiles are here to stay.

North Korean leader Kim Jong Un has refused to give up development of nuclear missiles capable of hitting the United States in spite of increasingly severe U.N. sanctions, raising fears of a new war on the Korean peninsula.
details.ground_truth: North and South Korea will compete together under one unified flag at next month's Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang, in what will be the first such arrangement since 2006. South Korea previously announced North Korea would send officials, athletes, and a cheer squad to the Olympics following talks between the two countries. It has now revealed North Korea's intention to send a 550-member delegation. Among those arriving in South Korea beginning on Jan. 25 will be female ice hockey players joining a team of South Korean athletes (they'll compete under a flag showing the Korean Peninsula in blue on a white background), as well as 230 cheerleaders, 140 orchestra members, and 30 people who will take part in a taekwondo demonstration, report Sky News and CNN. Ahead of the Olympics, skiers from both countries will also train together in North Korea, per CNN. The thawing of relations between the North and South has drawn praise from some. But others, like Japanese Foreign Minister Taro Kono, fear Kim Jong Un only hopes to ease international pressure while continuing to develop a nuclear weapons arsenal. "It is not the time to ease pressure, or to reward North Korea," Kono said Tuesday in Vancouver, Canada, where 20 countries met and agreed to consider tougher sanctions on the North, per Reuters. Warning countries not to be fooled by its "charm offensive," Kono added, "The fact that North Korea is engaging in dialogue could be interpreted as proof that the sanctions are working." China avoided the meeting, saying it showed a "Cold War mentality."

**Breaking News: Man Jumps White House Fence, Apprehended by Secret Service**

On Tuesday, a 41-year-old homeless man, identified as James Dirk Crudup, jumped the White House fence and was apprehended by uniformed Secret Service officers. The incident was broadcast live on CNN's "John King USA" program, which was produced from the North Lawn of the White House.

According to the Secret Service, Crudup will be charged with unlawful entry and contempt of court. The contempt charge is in connection with an order requiring him to stay away from the White House due to past incidents. This is not the first time someone has attempted to breach the White House security. In an unusual twist, a 6-year-old girl reached the lawn on Sunday night by slipping between the black metal pickets, but was escorted out by the Secret Service.

The incident ended shortly after 9 p.m. ET when authorities issued an all-clear directive at the White House. There was no immediate information on whether the intruder had represented a security threat. A backpack that apparently belonged to Crudup was being examined by security officers as a precautionary measure.

The president's whereabouts at the time of the incident were not immediately known, but the last event on his official schedule was set for 4:30 p.m. The Secret Service officers who apprehended Crudup tackled him immediately, and he was in the custody of two uniformed officers.
details.ground_truth: A homeless man was arrested after hopping the White House fence Tuesday night in an incident captured by CNN's John King USA program, which was filming from the North Lawn. The man was quickly taken into custody by Secret Service agents who approached him with guns drawn. Security officers locked the area down after finding a backpack that had been thrown over the fence nearby. The 41-year-old intruder was charged with unlawful entry and contempt of court for breaking a judicial order requiring him to stay away from the White House. It's not uncommon for the White House fence to be breached, notes the Washington Post: A 6-year-old girl made it onto the White House lawn over the weekend by slipping through the fence instead of going over it. Secret Service agents returned her to her parents.

**The Evolution of The Daily Show: An Oral History**

The Daily Show, a satirical news program, has undergone a significant transformation since its inception in 1996. The show's early years, under the hosting of Craig Kilborn, focused on creating humorous, often absurd, news headlines. However, when Jon Stewart took over as host in 1999, the show's tone and direction shifted.

Stewart's arrival was met with resistance from the existing writing staff, who felt that their creative freedom was being threatened. Stewart, however, saw an opportunity to take the show in a new direction, focusing on satire and social commentary. He brought in new writers, including Ben Karlin and David Javerbaum, who shared his vision.

The field department, led by Stewart Bailey, played a crucial role in shaping the show's tone. Their early excursions to New Hampshire to cover the presidential primaries were initially met with intimidation by the straight political media pack. However, it was a five-minute segment in December 1999, featuring Steve Carell on Senator John McCain's bus, that marked a turning point for the show.

The segment, which asked McCain about his record on pork-barrel politics, was a masterclass in satire and social commentary. It forced McCain to go off-book and provided a level of insight into the process that didn't exist. The show's focus on deconstructing the process, rather than just making jokes, came to the fore.

The show's correspondents, including Carell, Mo Rocca, and Stephen Colbert, played a crucial role in shaping the show's tone. They were encouraged to develop their own characters and styles, and to push the boundaries of what was considered acceptable in satire.

The show's impact on the media landscape cannot be overstated. It paved the way for a new generation of satirical news programs, including The Colbert Report and Last Week Tonight with John Oliver. It also forced politicians and media figures to confront the absurdity and hypocrisy of their actions.

In conclusion, The Daily Show's evolution is a testament to the power of creativity and vision. From its early days as a humorous, often absurd, news program to its current status as a satirical and social commentary powerhouse, the show has undergone a significant transformation.
details.ground_truth: Jon Stewart took over as host of the Daily Show in January 1999, but when did the program truly start becoming the Daily Show that legions of fans would come to love? Try December of that year, when Steve Carell boarded John McCain's campaign bus "and changed the entire trajectory" of the show, writes Chris Smith at Vanity Fair. The article is a fascinating oral history of the show's beginning that includes interviews with Stewart, Carell, Stephen Colbert, and pretty much everyone involved. The first year was rough for Stewart as he fought with the holdover writing staff from previous host Craig Kilborn and sought to put his stamp on the show. Things came together in that Carell interview, in which he peppers McCain with softball questions, then springs a policy zinger. After a few seconds of awkward silence, Carell says, "I was just kidding! I don’t even know what that means!" And the tense moment subsides. (See the clip.) Carell: "It was making fun of a gotcha moment. And I think that a lot of what we do on The Daily Show is making fun of journalistic tropes, and I think that was one of them." Head writer Ben Karlin: "I remember seeing it in the editing room. I remember Jon called me down, and seeing it and thinking, Yeah, this is what we should be doing. This is the goal. It was one of Carell’s most incredible moments. He asks McCain a question in a way that no journalists were talking to the candidates. And it was like, Oh s---, we are able, in this weird, unintentional way, to add a level of insight to the process that doesn’t exist. That was really, really exciting." Click for the full piece, in which Stewart talks about how that 2000 campaign provided his "aha moment" of how to present the show—by "deconstructing the process."

**Quick-Thinking 911 Dispatcher Saves Woman Trapped in Trunk of a Car**

In a remarkable display of quick thinking and resourcefulness, a 911 dispatcher in Johnston County, North Carolina, helped save a woman who was trapped in the trunk of a car. The incident occurred on January 14, 2017, around 4 a.m., when a 29-year-old woman called 911, saying she had been kidnapped by her jealous boyfriend and locked in the trunk of a car.

The 911 dispatcher, Tim Medlin, responded to the call and tried to communicate with the woman, but the call was lost due to poor reception. Medlin then had the idea to use the office's emergency cellphone, an old flip phone, to text the woman. To his surprise, the woman responded, providing him with the make and color of the vehicle, as well as the direction it was headed.

With this information, Medlin was able to contact the Cumberland County Sheriff's office and Verizon Wireless, who helped locate the phone's location. Fayetteville police spotted the car and rescued the woman, arresting her boyfriend, Nicholas Mattevi, on charges of false imprisonment.

The incident highlights the importance of technology in emergency situations and the need for 911 centers to be able to initiate texts from their systems. Johnston County's 911 director, Jason Barbour, praised Medlin's quick thinking, saying it was a "prime example of technology being used to the fullest at someone's greatest time of need."

Mattevi was released on a $10,000 bond, and the woman was found unharmed, thanks to the quick actions of Medlin and the emergency responders. The incident serves as a reminder of the importance of staying vigilant and seeking help in emergency situations.

**Key Takeaways:**

* A 911 dispatcher in Johnston County, North Carolina, helped save a woman trapped in the trunk of a car by using the office's emergency cellphone to text her.
* The woman provided the dispatcher with the make and color of the vehicle, as well as the direction it was headed, which helped authorities locate the phone's location.
* The incident highlights the importance of technology in emergency situations and the need for 911 centers to be able to initiate texts from their systems.
* Nicholas Mattevi, the woman's boyfriend, was arrested and released on a $10,000 bond on charges of false imprisonment.
* The woman was found unharmed, thanks to the quick actions of the emergency responders.
details.ground_truth: A North Carolina woman allegedly locked in the trunk of a moving car by her boyfriend was saved by a quick-thinking 911 dispatcher and a lowly flip phone, ABC News reports. The 29-year-old woman called 911 around 4am Jan. 14. The woman had gotten in a fight with her boyfriend, who was "jealous," according to WRAL. Before dispatcher Tim Medlin could get much information from the woman, the call was dropped. He was unable to get her to answer when he called back. Then Medlin remembered a flip phone the 911 communications center keeps around in case the phone lines go down, WTVD reports. He says texting was the only way he could think to contact her without getting "her in trouble." The woman texted Medlin back, letting the dispatcher know she was in a red Mustang headed toward Fayetteville. Verizon provided the location of the woman's phone, and police stopped her and her boyfriend, Nicholas Mattevi, in Fayetteville. Mattevi, 32, was arrested and charged with false imprisonment. "I can go to bed," Medlin recalls feeling upon hearing of the arrest. "We've done our job." He says he's planning to buy a smartphone for the office to replace the old flip phone and make it easier to text if there's a next time. Medlin's boss tells ABC the dispatcher "really stood by our motto: Failure is not an option." (This 911 dispatcher was accused of hanging up on callers.)

In a significant move to preserve the integrity of the web, the Internet Archive has launched a massive effort to archive pages as they are created and the pages they refer to. This initiative aims to replace broken links with references to the archived archived pages, allowing users to see the original content intended by the authors. Deputy Hopkins was responding to a disturbance call when he was confronted and shot by Jack Lee Breiner, 47. The incident led to a gunbattle, with Breiner being shot and wounded. He has since been charged with murder and other crimes in connection with the killing.

These two news stories highlight the importance of preserving the web and the risks and sacrifices faced by law enforcement officers in their daily work.
details.ground_truth: Police in Stanislaus County, Calif., say a 20-year veteran of the force was "executed" early Sunday after investigating a stolen van and a suspicious person outside Modesto. Sheriff's Deputy Dennis Wallace was shot twice in the head. Police say a suspect was arrested more than 150 miles away later in the day after allegedly carrying out a carjacking, an armed robbery at a convenience store, and an attempted purse-snatching, the Los Angeles Times reports. David Machado, a 37-year-old described by police as a "known criminal," was connected to the Wallace killing after police in Tulane County recognized his tattoos. Wallace, who was married with children, "was executed," Sheriff Adam Christianson told reporters. "We believe that Dep. Wallace was killed outside of the car and we know for a fact that the gun used in this crime was in direct contact with his head when the trigger was pulled twice." We need to keep law enforcement in our thoughts and prayers," he said. The Bradenton Herald reports after a knee injury in 2007, Wallace was off for two years without pay and fought a long legal battle to get his job back.

Lauren Bacall, the legendary movie star, left behind an estimated $26.6 million estate after her passing at the age of 89. The actress, known for her iconic roles in films like "To Have and Have Not" and "The Big Sleep," made sure to provide for her beloved dog, Sophie, a Papillon, by bequeathing $10,000 to her son, Sam Robards, to cover the canine's costs.

Bacall's will, signed last fall, also remembered two of her employees, Ilsa Hernandez and Maria Santos, with $15,000 and $20,000 respectively. The rest of her sizable estate, including her apartment in the Dakota building on the Upper West Side and its contents, is to be split evenly among her three children: Leslie Bogart, Stephen Humphrey Bogart, and Sam Robards.

The actress also directed that money from a trust left to her by her late husband, Humphrey Bogart, be divvied up between the three as well. She passed on the rights to her likeness and her movie and book royalties to her kids, and named them the executors of her estate. However, she asked for one thing in return: that her children respect her wish to keep private certain personal letters, writings, diaries, and other papers or memorabilia.

Bacall's generosity towards her family and employees is not unique among celebrities. She was a lifelong dog lover and was often seen walking her Papillon, Sophie, outside her $9 million apartment at The Dakota. Other celebrities, like Leona Helmsley and Alexander McQueen, have also left significant sums to their pets in their wills.
details.ground_truth: Papillion Sophie has lived a pampered life, but that doesn't have to end now that her famous owner—Hollywood legend Lauren Bacall—has died. According to estate papers filed last week, Bacall left $10,000 to her son Sam Robards for Sophie's care and upkeep, Page Six reports. This bequeath isn't a surprise, considering Bacall called herself a "dog yearner" in a 2008 interview, Us reports. "I didn't have a dog growing up in the city with a working mother. As an only child, I yearned for someone to talk to," she said. She gave Sophie quite a glitzy life attending book signings and film festivals. Bacall didn't ignore her human family: The New York Daily News reports her $26.6 million estate was mostly split among her three children; her grandkids reportedly got $250,000 each. And two of her employees also received $20,000 and $15,000, respectively. Considering other famous four-legged heirs, Sophie's 10 grand isn't shocking: Leona Helmsley left $12 million to her Maltese and Alexander McQueen left $82,000 to his three dogs. But the oddest may be Dusty Springfield, who gave orders that her cat be serenaded with her own music and "marry" another cat.

**Politics and Elections**

The Republican presidential primary season continues to heat up, with several key developments emerging in the past few days. In the CPAC presidential straw poll, Mitt Romney emerged victorious with 38% of the vote, followed by Rick Santorum with 31%. However, Santorum has questioned the legitimacy of the poll, suggesting that Romney's campaign may have "bought" tickets for supporters to attend the event. Romney's campaign has rejected these claims, with spokeswoman Andrea Saul accusing Santorum of making "statements that aren’t grounded in the truth."

Meanwhile, Ron Paul has expressed his own skepticism about Romney's self-description as a "severe conservative," suggesting that he needs to provide a clearer definition of what this means.

**The White House and Birth Control Controversy**

In a bid to address the controversy over birth control and Catholic institutions, the White House has announced a new "accommodation" that would require insurance companies to cover the cost of birth control for church-run schools and hospitals. However, this plan has been met with skepticism by some Catholic leaders, who argue that it does not go far enough in addressing their concerns.

White House chief of staff Jack Lew has defended the plan, suggesting that it is a "consistent" position for the administration and that it is as far as they will go in addressing the issue. Lew has also suggested that health insurers will actually benefit from covering birth control costs, as it reduces the number of pregnancies and associated healthcare costs.

**Key Takeaways**

* Mitt Romney emerges victorious in the CPAC presidential straw poll, but Rick Santorum questions the legitimacy of the poll.
* Ron Paul expresses skepticism about Romney's self-description as a "severe conservative."
* The White House announces a new "accommodation" on birth control and Catholic institutions, but it is met with skepticism by some Catholic leaders.
* White House chief of staff Jack Lew defends the plan, suggesting that it is a "consistent" position for the administration.
details.ground_truth: Fresh off his loss to Mitt Romney at CPAC, Rick Santorum hit the talk shows this morning, and he's downplaying the victory, reports Politico: "You have to talk to the Romney campaign and how many tickets they bought—we've heard all sorts of things," Santorum told CNN. "Ron Paul has won those (straw polls) because he trucks in a lot of people, pays for their ticket, and they come in and vote. I don't try to rig straw polls." Elsewhere on the Sunday dial, as per Politico: Santorum on his chances: "I think we can do well (in Michigan). I think we can do reasonably well in Arizona, and really make this a two-person race." Sarah Palin on Romney: He's "a great candidate," but "his idea of conservatism is evolving, and I base this on a pretty moderate past that he has had, even in some cases a liberal past. Now that’s a problem. He’s still in the 30 percentile mark ... because we are not convinced." Obama chief of staff Jack Lew on the birth control ruckus: The Friday "accommodation" is as far as the White House will go. "There are others who don't have the same objective, they will have to speak for themselves, (but) this is our plan." Ron Paul on 'severe conservative' Romney: "That was the first time I’ve heard that definition, so I guess Mitt will have to tell us exactly what it means. Obviously, he means he’s a serious conservative, and he was trying to defend himself, or portray himself as such, but I don’t know exactly what he was meaning by that."
